topics.title: Definition and Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT863 - ADHOC AND WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition and Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: Ad-hoc Wireless Networks

An **Ad-hoc Wireless Network** is a decentralized, infrastructure-less collection of autonomous wireless nodes (mobile or static) that dynamically self-organize and self-configure to form a temporary, multi-hop communication network without relying on any pre-existing fixed infrastructure such as base stations, access points, or centralized administration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A wireless ad-hoc network is a self-configuring network of mobile hosts (nodes) connected by wireless links, where the union of which forms an arbitrary topology. The hosts are free to move randomly and organize themselves arbitrarily; thus, the network's wireless topology may change rapidly and unpredictably.

## 1.2 Formal Definition: Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a spatially distributed autonomous network of tiny, low-power, resource-constrained sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions (such as temperature, sound, pressure, humidity, motion, or pollutants) and forward the collected data through multi-hop wireless communication to a central **sink** or **Base Station (BS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** While all WSNs are ad-hoc in nature, NOT all ad-hoc networks are WSNs. Ad-hoc networks focus on **communication** between devices, whereas WSNs focus on **sensing** the environment.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 🌍 Ad-hoc Network Analogy: The Mountain Rescue Team
Imagine a group of mountaineers trapped in a valley with **no cell towers and no internet**. Each climber holds a walkie-talkie. To send a message from Climber A to Climber Z, the message must be **relayed** by Climbers B, C, D, and E — each acting as a "router." If Climber C moves away, the group must **re-route** the message through a new path instantly. This is exactly how an ad-hoc network behaves — **no central boss, everyone helps pass the message**.

### 🌡️ WSN Analogy: The Smart Forest Fire Detector
Picture a vast national park where hundreds of tiny, battery-powered "matchbox-sized" devices are scattered across trees. Each device measures **temperature, smoke, and humidity** every second. When a device senses abnormal heat, it whispers the data to its nearest neighbor, which passes it on, and eventually the alert reaches the **forest ranger's control room**. If one device dies (battery drained), the others automatically find a new path. This is a **Wireless Sensor Network**.

## 1.4 Core Characteristics of Ad-hoc & Wireless Sensor Networks

| # | Characteristic | Description | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Self-Organization** | Nodes autonomously form the network without central controller | Eliminates infrastructure cost |
| 2 | **Multi-hop Routing** | Data traverses multiple intermediate nodes to reach destination | Extends coverage beyond single radio range |
| 3 | **Dynamic Topology** | Network topology changes due to node mobility/failure | Requires adaptive routing protocols (AODV, DSR) |
| 4 | **Energy Constrained** | Nodes operate on limited battery (often non-rechargeable) | Energy efficiency is the primary design goal |
| 5 | **Limited Bandwidth** | Shared wireless medium with variable capacity | Throughput optimization is critical |
| 6 | **Scalability** | Network may contain hundreds to thousands of nodes | Protocol must scale to dense deployments |
| 7 | **Heterogeneity (in WSN)** | Nodes may have varying sensing, computing, and power capabilities | Enables tiered/hybrid architectures |
| 8 | **Data-centric** | Communication is based on data/attributes, not node IDs | Uses attribute-based addressing (e.g., temperature > 40°C) |
| 9 | **Application-specific** | Network designed for a specific task (monitoring, tracking) | Allows tailored protocol design |
| 10 | **Unattended Operation** | Deployed in harsh/inaccessible areas (volcanoes, battlefields) | Must operate autonomously for years |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** A typical WSN node operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band**, with transmission range of **10–100 meters**, data rates of **10–250 kbps**, and battery life targeted at **1–5 years** on 2 AA cells (≈ 1–2 Ah capacity).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture Breakdown

A Wireless Sensor Network follows a layered operational model that distinguishes it from traditional wired networks.

### 2.1.1 The Three-Tier WSN Architecture

- **Tier 1 — Sensor Field (Lower Layer):** Contains the actual **sensor motes** that sense physical phenomena. These are resource-constrained (limited CPU, memory, energy). Communication is **hop-by-hop** with neighboring motes.
- **Tier 2 — Network Backbone (Middle Layer):** Comprises **Cluster Heads (CHs)** or **gateways** that aggregate data from multiple sensor motes, perform local processing, and forward compressed information upstream.
- **Tier 3 — Sink / Base Station (Upper Layer):** The high-power node that connects the WSN to the **internet or a central server** for end-user application access (often called the **sink**, **observer**, or **Base Station**).

### 2.1.2 The Five Subsystems of a Sensor Node (Smart Dust Architecture)

A single sensor node is composed of five fundamental subsystems:

- **Sensing Subsystem** — converts physical phenomena (light, heat, motion) into analog/digital signals using transducers/ADC.
- **Processing Subsystem** — microcontroller/CPU that executes protocols and processes data (e.g., Mica2 motes use ATmega128L @ **8 MHz** with **4 KB RAM**).
- **Communication Subsystem** — radio transceiver (e.g., CC2420) operating typically at **2.4 GHz** with data rates from **20 to 250 kbps**.
- **Power Subsystem** — battery unit (often **2 × AA alkaline** = 3V, 1500 mAh) that powers all other subsystems.
- **Localization/Subsystem (optional)** — GPS or beacon-based hardware for position awareness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Budget Reality:** In typical sensor motes, the **radio subsystem consumes 60–80%** of total energy. The **sensing subsystem consumes ~1–5%**, and **processing consumes ~10–20%**. This is why WSN protocols (like LEACH, SPIN) aggressively minimize radio "ON" time.

## 2.2 Energy Consumption Model (First-Order Radio Model)

The most cited analytical model for WSN energy analysis is the **Heinzelman First-Order Radio Model** used in the LEACH protocol.

### 2.2.1 Transmission Energy (E_TX)

To transmit a *k*-bit message over a distance *d*, the energy consumed is:

$$E_{TX}(k, d) = E_{TX\text{-}elec}(k) + E_{TX\text{-}amp}(k, d)$$

Breaking it down:
- $E_{TX\text{-}elec}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$ → energy consumed by the **transmitter circuitry** (radio electronics).
- $E_{TX\text{-}amp}(k, d) = k \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$ → energy consumed by the **transmit amplifier**, where *n* is the **path loss exponent**.

The path loss exponent *n* takes different values based on the environment:
- $n = 2$ → **Free-space propagation** (line-of-sight).
- $3 \le n \le 4$ → **Multi-path fading** (urban/obstructed environments).
- $n = 4$ → **Two-ray ground propagation** (typical default in LEACH analysis).

### 2.2.2 Reception Energy (E_RX)

To receive a *k*-bit message, the energy consumed is:

$$E_{RX}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Reception is **NOT free** in WSNs. Unlike wired networks, a node must expend energy to listen to the radio channel. This is why duty-cycling (sleep/wake schedules like S-MAC) is critical.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Values / Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transmission Energy** | $E_{TX}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$ | $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m² | Energy analysis for sending data |
| 2 | **Reception Energy** | $E_{RX}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$ | $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit | Energy analysis for receiving data |
| 3 | **Data Aggregation Energy** | $E_{DA}(k) = k \cdot E_{DA}$ | $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit | Energy for fusion at cluster head |
| 4 | **Network Lifetime** | $T_{life} = \dfrac{E_{initial}}{E_{consumed\_per\_round}}$ | seconds, hours, or rounds | Quantifying WSN lifetime |
| 5 | **Coverage Area** | $A = \pi \cdot R^{2}$ | $R$ = sensing radius (m) | Determines node density required |
| 6 | **Node Density (Coverage)** | $\rho = \dfrac{N \cdot \pi R^{2}}{A_{region}}$ | nodes per m² | Ensuring full coverage |
| 7 | **Path Loss (Free-Space)** | $PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz | Link budget calculations |
| 8 | **Average Energy per Bit per Distance** | $E_{bit\_dist} = E_{elec} + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$ | Joules/bit/m^n | Comparing routing efficiency |
| 9 | **Cluster Head Selection Probability (LEACH)** | $P_{CH} = \dfrac{k}{N}$ | $k$ = optimum CHs, $N$ = total nodes | LEACH protocol design |
| 10 | **Degree of Connectivity** | $\mu = \dfrac{N \cdot \pi r^{2}}{A} - \ln(N) + c$ | asymptotic formula | Full connectivity probability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - **Short-distance threshold** ($d < d_0$): use **free-space** model with $d^2$.
> - **Long-distance** ($d \geq d_0$): use **multi-path** model with $d^4$.
> - $d_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}$ (crossover distance, typically **≈ 87 m** for default LEACH parameters).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The WSN and ad-hoc characteristics are not academic curiosities — they drive critical engineering decisions in:

- **Military Surveillance:** Battlefield sensor deployment demands unattended operation, anti-jamming, and self-organization.
- **Precision Agriculture:** Soil moisture and temperature sensors require multi-year battery life, sparse deployment, and weather-resilient housing.
- **Healthcare Monitoring:** Body-area sensor networks (BAN) require ultra-low power, real-time response, and data privacy.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Smart factories use WSNs for predictive maintenance, with high reliability and deterministic routing.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Volcano monitoring (e.g., Ecuador's Tungurahua), glacier movement tracking, and wildlife habitat monitoring rely on multi-hop data delivery from inaccessible areas.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Cluster Count for LEACH (Karthik-Heinzelman Result)

This is a **classic KTU-style derivation** that frequently appears in examinations. It derives the optimal number of clusters to **minimize total energy dissipation per round** in a clustered WSN.

### Step 1: Define Network Geometry
Assume $N$ sensor nodes are uniformly distributed over an $M \times M$ region, with **1 cluster head (CH) per cluster**, and there are $k$ clusters total. The average number of nodes per cluster is:

$$N_{\text{per\_cluster}} = \frac{N}{k}$$

### Step 2: Energy Dissipated by a Non-CH Node
Each non-CH node transmits its data to its CH over a short distance. The expected squared distance from a node to its CH is derived from the **M/M cluster geometry** as:

$$E[d_{\text{toCH}}^{2}] = \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}$$

A non-CH node dissipates (transmit only):

$$E_{\text{nonCH}} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}$$

Here we use the free-space model (short intra-cluster distance) with path loss exponent $n = 2$, so $\epsilon_{amp} = \epsilon_{fs}$.

### Step 3: Energy Dissipated by a Cluster Head
The CH must receive data from all $\frac{N}{k} - 1$ member nodes, perform data aggregation, and then transmit the aggregated packet to the base station (BS) located at the center.

$$E_{\text{CH}} = \left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{N}{k} \cdot k \cdot E_{DA} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4}$$

The base station is far away, so we use the **multi-path model** with $d^4$.

### Step 4: Total Energy per Cluster per Round
Energy dissipated per cluster per round:

$$E_{\text{cluster}} = E_{\text{CH}} + \left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) \cdot E_{\text{nonCH}} \approx E_{\text{CH}} + \frac{N}{k} \cdot E_{\text{nonCH}}$$

### Step 5: Total Energy per Round
$$E_{\text{total}} = k \cdot E_{\text{cluster}} = k \cdot E_{\text{CH}} + N \cdot E_{\text{nonCH}}$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$E_{\text{total}} = k \cdot \left[ \frac{N}{k} \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{N}{k} \cdot k \cdot E_{DA} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4} \right] + N \cdot \left[ k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k} \right]$$

After algebraic simplification (assuming long-range transmission dominates):

$$E_{\text{total}} \approx k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4} + N \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}$$

### Step 6: Minimize Energy by Differentiating
To find the optimum $k$, differentiate $E_{\text{total}}$ with respect to $k$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d(E_{\text{total}})}{dk} = \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4} - N \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k^{2}} = 0$$

Solving for $k$:

$$k^{2} = \frac{N \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot M^{2}}{2\pi \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4}}$$

$$\boxed{k_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{\text{toBS}}^{2}}}$$

### Step 7: Interpretation

$$k_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{\text{toBS}}^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insights from the Result:**
> 1. **More nodes (N ↑)** → more clusters are needed (denser deployment benefits from more parallel aggregation).
> 2. **Larger field (M ↑)** → more clusters needed (distant nodes need their own local aggregators).
> 3. **Farther base station (d_toBS ↑)** → **fewer clusters** are optimal (grouping more nodes per CH reduces expensive long-haul transmissions).
> 4. **Better radio hardware (ε_fs/ε_mp higher)** → more clusters preferred.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A WSN has $N = 100$ nodes deployed in a $100 \text{ m} \times 100 \text{ m}$ field. The base station is located $d_{\text{toBS}} = 75$ m away. Given $\epsilon_{fs} = 10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^2$ and $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^4$. Find the optimum number of clusters.

### Solution Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Substitute values into the formula:

$$k_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{\text{toBS}}^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute each term:

$$\sqrt{\frac{100}{2\pi}} = \sqrt{15.915} \approx 3.99$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} = \sqrt{\frac{10}{0.0013}} = \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7$$

$$\frac{M}{d_{\text{toBS}}^{2}} = \frac{100}{75^{2}} = \frac{100}{5625} \approx 0.01778$$

**Step 3:** Multiply:

$$k_{\text{opt}} \approx 3.99 \times 87.7 \times 0.01778$$

$$k_{\text{opt}} \approx 6.22$$

**Step 4:** Round to nearest integer:

$$\boxed{k_{\text{opt}} = 6 \text{ clusters}}$$

**Step 5:** Verification — probability of being a CH:

$$P_{CH} = \frac{k}{N} = \frac{6}{100} = 0.06 = 6\%$$

This matches LEACH's default recommendation of $P_{CH} = 5\%$, validating the analysis.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Single Sensor Node Architecture

This is the canonical "Smart Dust" / Berkeley Mote architecture as required by KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR[Power Subsystem Battery 2xAA]:::power
    SENS[Sensing Subsystem Sensors and ADC]:::sense
    PROC[Processing Subsystem MCU ATmega128L]:::proc
    MEM[Memory Flash and RAM]:::proc
    RAD[Communication Subsystem Radio CC2420 2.4GHz]:::comm
    LOC[Localization Subsystem GPS Optional]:::loc
    APP[Application User Logic]:::proc

    PWR -->|Supplies 3V DC| SENS
    PWR -->|Supplies 3V DC| PROC
    PWR -->|Supplies 3V DC| RAD
    PWR -->|Supplies 3V DC| LOC

    SENS -->|Analog and Digital Data| PROC
    PROC <-->|Read/Write| MEM
    PROC -->|Tx/Rx Packets| RAD
    PROC <-->|Coord Data| LOC
    PROC -->|Control Signals| APP

    RAD -->|Wireless Link| RAD2((Other Motes))
    RAD2 -.->|Multi-hop| SINK((Base Station Sink))

    classDef power fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#8B7500,color:#000
    classDef sense fill:#A8E6CF,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#D6E1FF,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef comm fill:#FFC1CC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    classDef loc fill:#E0BBE4,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
```

**Explanation of Block Interactions:**
- The **Power Subsystem** is the heart — it supplies regulated DC voltage to all other blocks.
- The **Sensing Subsystem** converts physical phenomena (heat, light) into electrical signals, then digitizes via the ADC embedded in the MCU.
- The **Processing Subsystem** runs the protocol stack (MAC, routing, application) and decides when to wake the radio.
- The **Communication Subsystem** transmits/receives wireless packets, consuming the most energy.
- The **Localization Subsystem** (optional) provides coordinates for geographic routing protocols.

## 4.2 WSN Network Topology (Multi-hop, Clustered)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FIELD[Sensor Field 100m x 100m]
        direction TB
        N1[Node 1]:::node
        N2[Node 2]:::node
        N3[Node 3]:::node
        N4[Node 4]:::node
        N5[Node 5]:::node
        N6[Node 6]:::node
        N7[Node 7]:::node
        N8[Node 8]:::node
        N9[Node 9]:::node
    end

    CH1[Cluster Head 1]:::ch
    CH2[Cluster Head 2]:::ch

    BS[Base Station Sink]:::bs
    USER((End User Server)):::user

    N1 -->|intra-cluster| CH1
    N2 -->|intra-cluster| CH1
    N3 -->|intra-cluster| CH1
    N4 -->|intra-cluster| CH2
    N5 -->|intra-cluster| CH2
    N6 -->|intra-cluster| CH2
    N7 -.->|inter-cluster hop| CH1
    N8 -.->|inter-cluster hop| CH2
    N9 -.->|inter-cluster hop| CH1

    CH1 -->|aggregated data| BS
    CH2 -->|aggregated data| BS
    BS -->|Internet Gateway| USER

    classDef node fill:#C7E9FF,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef ch fill:#FFD3B6,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef bs fill:#A0FFA0,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef user fill:#E0BBE4,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
```

**Explanation:**
- **Nodes 1–9** are ordinary sensor motes that sense and forward data to their assigned **Cluster Head (CH)**.
- **CH1 and CH2** aggregate the data (reducing redundancy) and forward it via long-range transmission to the **Base Station (BS)**.
- The **Base Station** is the gateway to the external world (internet, user application).
- This is the **LEACH-style two-tier architecture**.

## 4.3 Ad-hoc Network vs. Cellular Network (Comparative Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ADHOC[Ad-hoc Network No Infrastructure]
        direction LR
        A1[Node A Mobile]:::adhoc
        A2[Node B Mobile]:::adhoc
        A3[Node C Mobile]:::adhoc
        A4[Node D Mobile]:::adhoc
        A1 <-->|hop 1| A2
        A2 <-->|hop 2| A3
        A3 <-->|hop 3| A4
    end

    subgraph CELL[Cellular Network With Infrastructure]
        direction LR
        C1[Mobile A]:::cell
        C2[Mobile B]:::cell
        BTS[Base Station Tower]:::tower
        C1 -->|uplink| BTS
        C2 -->|uplink| BTS
    end

    classDef adhoc fill:#FFD3B6,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef cell fill:#C7E9FF,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef tower fill:#A0FFA0,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

**Explanation of Contrast:**
- **Left (Ad-hoc):** No central tower. Nodes A→B→C→D must **cooperate** to relay messages. Each node is both a host and a router.
- **Right (Cellular):** Every mobile phone talks **directly** to the central **Base Station (BTS)**. The BTS is the central coordinator.
- The trade-off: Ad-hoc is **flexible and infrastructure-free** but routing is complex; cellular is **simple** but requires heavy infrastructure investment.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**
**Define a Wireless Sensor Network. List any FOUR distinct characteristics that differentiate WSNs from traditional wired networks.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a collection of spatially distributed, autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions and communicate wirelessly in a multi-hop fashion to a central sink.
>
> **Four distinguishing characteristics of WSNs:**
> 1. **Severe energy constraints** — nodes operate on limited battery; energy is the primary design metric.
> 2. **Self-organization** — no central controller; nodes autonomously form topology.
> 3. **Data-centric communication** — queries are attribute-based (e.g., "temperature > 40°C") rather than address-based.
> 4. **Dense deployment with high redundancy** — hundreds to thousands of nodes, with overlap providing fault tolerance.
> 5. *(Optional)* Limited memory, processing, and bandwidth resources.

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**Distinguish between MANETs and WSNs in terms of their primary design objective.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **MANET** | **WSN** |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Communication between mobile users (high throughput, low latency) | Sensing the environment (energy efficiency, longevity) |
| **Node Mobility** | High and unpredictable | Mostly static after deployment |
| **Energy Constraint** | Moderate (rechargeable) | Severe (often non-rechargeable battery) |
| **Node Identity** | Address-centric (each node has unique ID) | Data-centric (queries on data attributes) |
| **Data Flow** | Arbitrary peer-to-peer | Many-to-one (sensors → sink) |
| **Scale** | Tens to hundreds | Hundreds to thousands |
| **Application** | Tactical communication, disaster recovery | Environmental monitoring, IoT, healthcare |

> A MANET is designed for **who-communicates-with-whom**, whereas a WSN is designed for **what-is-happening-where**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Past 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Define a Wireless Sensor Network with a neat block diagram of a sensor node. Explain the function of each subsystem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the unique characteristics of WSNs when compared to traditional wireless networks? Justify each characteristic with one real-world application. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

A **WSN** is a self-organizing, multi-hop wireless network of densely deployed sensor motes that sense, process, and forward data from a physical environment to a sink.

**Block Diagram of Sensor Node (describe the architecture):**

A sensor node consists of **four mandatory subsystems** and **one optional subsystem**:

1. **Sensing Subsystem (Sensors + ADC):** Contains physical transducers (temperature, humidity, pressure, acoustic) that convert physical phenomena into electrical signals. The ADC digitizes analog signals for the processing unit. *Example sensor board on Mica2 mote: MTS300CA.*

2. **Processing Subsystem (MCU + Memory):** The "brain" of the node. Executes protocol stack, performs data processing and aggregation. *Example: ATmega128L microcontroller, 8 MHz clock, 128 KB flash, 4 KB SRAM.*

3. **Communication Subsystem (Radio Transceiver):** Enables wireless communication using RF. Operates typically in **2.4 GHz** ISM band with data rate of **250 kbps**. *Example radio: CC2420.*

4. **Power Subsystem (Battery):** Supplies regulated power to all other blocks. Typically **2 × AA alkaline cells = 3V, ~1500 mAh**. May include DC-DC converters and sleep-mode controllers.

5. **Localization Subsystem (Optional):** Provides geographic position using GPS or beacon-based techniques, essential for location-aware routing.

> The **functional flow** is: Sensor → ADC → MCU → (optional processing) → Radio → Wireless link to other motes → eventually to Base Station.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

The **unique characteristics** that distinguish WSNs from traditional wireless networks (like Wi-Fi, cellular) are:

| # | Characteristic | Real-World Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Energy Scarcity** — Node battery is finite; recharge/replacement is hard. | **Glacier monitoring sensors** in Antarctica — cannot replace batteries. |
| 2 | **Application-Specific Design** — Hardware/protocol tailored to single task. | **Habituating monitoring of Zebra** in Africa — only movement sensors needed. |
| 3 | **Data-Centric Networking** — Queries on attributes, not IP addresses. | **Precision agriculture** — query "moisture < 30%" across entire field. |
| 4 | **Dense, Redundant Deployment** — High node density provides fault tolerance. | **Forest fire detection** — overlap ensures a missed node doesn't miss fire. |
| 5 | **Unattended Long-Term Operation** — No human maintenance for years. | **Volcano monitoring** (Tungurahua project) — nodes operate unattended for years. |
| 6 | **Many-to-One Traffic Pattern** — Most flows are toward the sink. | **Structural health monitoring of bridges** — sensors forward to base station. |
| 7 | **Limited Resources** — Memory (4 KB), processing (8 MHz), bandwidth (250 kbps). | **Smart dust for body-area networks** — tiny, low-power ingestible sensors. |

**Justification:** In each case, traditional wireless networks (Wi-Fi, 4G) cannot meet these constraints because they require infrastructure, expect high power, and assume mobile user devices with replaceable batteries.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Drawing the **network topology** instead of the **node block diagram** in part (a). KTU specifically asks for the **node architecture** (subsystems inside one mote), not the network architecture. **You will lose 3 marks** for this confusion.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Listing WSN characteristics without tying them to a specific real-world application. Each characteristic **must** be justified with a concrete use-case for full marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing the **sink** (Base Station) with the **CH** (Cluster Head). The CH is a sensor node with extra responsibility; the sink is a high-power gateway outside the sensor field.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Alt Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the multi-tier architecture of a Wireless Sensor Network. Discuss the role of cluster heads and the base station. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **optimum number of clusters** ($k_{opt}$) in the LEACH protocol using the first-order radio model. Briefly explain the engineering significance of each parameter. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

The **multi-tier (3-tier) architecture** of a WSN consists of:

**Tier 1 — Sensor Field (Lower Tier):**
Contains $N$ low-cost, low-power **sensor motes** that sense the environment. Each mote has limited resources. Motes are organized into **clusters** of $N/k$ nodes each (where $k$ = number of clusters). Communication within a cluster is short-range and free-space.

**Tier 2 — Cluster Heads (Middle Tier):**
Each cluster has a designated **Cluster Head (CH)** responsible for:
- Receiving data from all member nodes.
- Performing **data aggregation / fusion** to eliminate redundancy.
- Forwarding a single compressed packet to the Base Station.

CHs consume more energy and often rotate (LEACH protocol randomly rotates CH role among all nodes for energy balancing).

**Tier 3 — Base Station (Upper Tier):**
A high-power, resource-rich node (laptop-class computer) located at the network edge. It:
- Receives aggregated data from all CHs.
- Acts as the **gateway to the internet / user application**.
- Performs global analysis and triggers alerts.

**Architectural Diagram** (refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the mermaid block).

> The energy efficiency of this architecture comes from **compressing** $N$ data packets into $k$ aggregated packets before the expensive long-haul transmission to the BS.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

The derivation follows the seven-step process in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final result is:

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}$$

**Engineering significance of each parameter:**

- $N$ (number of nodes): More nodes → need more clusters for parallelism and load balancing.
- $M$ (field side length): Larger field → more clusters needed because the average intra-cluster distance scales with $M$.
- $d_{toBS}$ (distance to Base Station): **Inverse-square** dependence — farther BS strongly reduces optimal cluster count, because grouping more nodes per CH is needed to amortize the expensive long-haul transmission.
- $\epsilon_{fs}, \epsilon_{mp}$ (radio amplifier energy): Their ratio $\sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}}$ is a hardware constant. Better radios (lower amplifier energy) permit more clusters.

> If $N = 100, M = 100$ m, $d_{toBS} = 75$ m, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, then **$k_{opt} = 6$ clusters** (as computed in Section 3.2).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Part b):**
> - **Do not skip the radio model** — you must state the first-order radio model equations ($E_{TX}$ and $E_{RX}$) **before** beginning the derivation. KTU awards 1–2 marks for this.
> - **Do not forget the $d^{4}$ term** for CH-to-BS transmission (multi-path model). Many students erroneously use $d^2$ throughout. **This is a 2-mark deduction.**
> - **Do not omit the differentiation step** $\frac{d(E_{total})}{dk} = 0$. Showing the derivative is critical to earn full marks.
> - **Unit consistency:** Use SI units (meters, joules) — conversion of kbits, mbits, etc., is checked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Definition and Characteristics"**

- ✅ **WSN** = network of tiny, energy-constrained, wirelessly connected sensor motes that sense environment and forward data to a sink.
- ✅ **MANET** = mobile, multi-hop, infrastructure-less network focused on **communication** among peers.
- ✅ A WSN is a **special case of ad-hoc networks** optimized for **sensing**, not communication.
- ✅ The **5 subsystems** of a sensor node: **S, P, C, P, L** (Sensing, Processing, Communication, Power, Localization).
- ✅ The **first-order radio model**: $E_{TX} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$ with $n=2$ (free-space) or $n=4$ (multi-path).
- ✅ **Reception is NOT free** — $E_{RX} = k \cdot E_{elec}$.
- ✅ **Radio consumes 60–80% of node energy** — protocol design minimizes radio "ON" time.
- ✅ **Optimum cluster count in LEACH**: $k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}$.
- ✅ **Cluster Head ≠ Base Station** — CH is a sensor mote elected to aggregate; BS is a high-power external gateway.
- ✅ **Data-centric** vs. **Address-centric**: WSNs query data attributes; traditional networks query IP addresses.
- ✅ **Unique WSN characteristics**: Energy scarcity, self-organization, dense deployment, unattended operation, many-to-one traffic, data-centricity.
- ✅ **Standard parameters** to memorize: $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit.
- ✅ **Path-loss crossover distance**: $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}} \approx 87$ m.
- ✅ **Architecture tiers**: Sensor Field → Cluster Heads → Base Station → User.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "define and list characteristics" in the KTU exam, **always pair each characteristic with a real-world example** to demonstrate application knowledge — this is what distinguishes a 7-mark answer from a full-mark 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Adhoc Networks: MANETs, VANETs, and FANETs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT863 - ADHOC AND WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Adhoc Networks: MANETs, VANETs, and FANETs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Adhoc Networks: The Foundation

An **Adhoc Network** is a decentralized, self-configuring, infrastructure-less wireless network where individual nodes (devices) communicate with each other directly, without relying on a pre-established central infrastructure such as base stations, routers, or access points. Each node in such a network acts as both a **host** (end-system) and a **router** (forwarder of packets), dynamically forming multi-hop paths to relay data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1 Anchor Topic):**
> "Adhoc networks are autonomous, self-organizing wireless networks where the topology is dynamically created and maintained by the participating nodes themselves. They can be classified based on the nature of the mobility, application domain, and physical characteristics of the constituent nodes."

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Whisper Game in a Moving Train":**
> Imagine you and your friends are scattered in different compartments of a moving train, with no announcement system. If a person in the last compartment wants to pass a message to a person in the front, they cannot shout across the entire train. Instead, the message is **whispered from person to person**, where each friend acts as both the **receiver of the whisper** and the **forwarder of the same whisper** to the next friend. Now, imagine everyone in the train is **constantly moving** — entering, exiting, switching compartments. The chain of whispers must be **continuously re-organized** to reach the destination. This is exactly how an ad-hoc wireless network functions, with **wireless signals replacing whispers** and **mobile nodes replacing people**.

---

## 1.2 Mobile Ad-Hoc Networks (MANETs)

### Formal Definition
A **Mobile Ad-Hoc Network (MANET)** is a continuously self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of mobile devices connected wirelessly, where the mobility pattern of the nodes is **random, multi-directional, and constrained primarily to 2D ground-based human movement**. Each node is free to move in any direction at any time, and will therefore change its links to other devices frequently. Each device in a MANET must forward traffic unrelated to its own use, and therefore acts as a **mobile router**.

### Key Characteristics
- **Topology:** Highly dynamic, unpredictable, and frequently changing.
- **Mobility Model:** Pedestrian/vehicular-speed mobility (typically **0–30 m/s**).
- **Network Density:** Variable — sparse to dense.
- **Energy Constraints:** Battery-powered devices with **limited energy budgets**.
- **Routing Complexity:** High, due to unpredictable link breakages.
- **Standard Protocols:** AODV, DSR, OLSR, TORA, DSDV.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Use-Cases:** Emergency disaster recovery, military battlefield communication, conference room networks, sensor field monitoring, personal area networks (PANs).

---

## 1.3 Vehicular Ad-Hoc Networks (VANETs)

### Formal Definition
A **Vehicular Ad-Hoc Network (VANET)** is a subclass of MANETs specifically designed to provide communication among **vehicles (V2V — Vehicle-to-Vehicle)** and between **vehicles and roadside infrastructure (V2R / V2I — Vehicle-to-Roadside / Infrastructure)**. The defining feature of VANETs is that the nodes (vehicles) follow **predictable, constrained, and high-speed mobility patterns dictated by road topology, traffic regulations, and driving behavior**.

### Key Characteristics
- **Topology:** Rapidly changing but **partially predictable** (road-bound).
- **Mobility Model:** High velocity, **typically 10–40 m/s** for urban areas, up to **80 m/s** on highways.
- **Energy Constraints:** Vehicles have **virtually unlimited power** (car batteries/alternators).
- **Computational Capacity:** Rich — modern vehicles have powerful onboard units (OBUs).
- **Specialized Standards:** **IEEE 802.11p (WAVE — Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments)** and **IEEE 1609.x** suite.
- **Infrastructure:** Often hybrid — uses **RSUs (Roadside Units)** as fixed access points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Use-Cases:** Collision avoidance, traffic flow optimization, infotainment, autonomous driving coordination, toll collection, emergency vehicle prioritization.

---

## 1.4 Flying Ad-Hoc Networks (FANETs)

### Formal Definition
A **Flying Ad-Hoc Network (FANET)** is a specialized class of ad-hoc networks in which the communicating nodes are **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) / drones** that establish multi-hop communication links with one another. Unlike MANETs and VANETs, FANETs exhibit **3-Dimensional (3D) mobility** since UAVs can move freely in **all three spatial dimensions** (X, Y, Z axes), are not restricted to road or ground constraints, and typically operate at higher relative speeds.

### Key Characteristics
- **Topology:** Rapid and often orchestrated (e.g., swarm formations).
- **Mobility Model:** 3D, **typically 10–100 m/s** depending on UAV class.
- **Energy Constraints:** **Critical** — limited by battery payload of UAV.
- **Specialized Standards:** **IEEE 802.11 (ad-hoc mode)**, **IEEE 802.15.4**, **MAVLink** protocol.
- **Communication Range:** Often **longer line-of-sight (LoS)** links due to aerial positioning.
- **Challenge:** Antenna orientation, wind disturbances, payload weight limits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Use-Cases:** Aerial surveillance, precision agriculture, search & rescue, military reconnaissance, delivery drones, weather monitoring, live event broadcasting.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

## 2.1 Operational Logic: What Makes Each Network Unique?

### MANET Operational Logic
1. **Node Initialization** — Each device broadcasts a "hello" packet upon joining the network.
2. **Neighbor Discovery** — Nodes use beaconing to maintain a **neighbor table** within transmission radius ($R$).
3. **Route Establishment** — On-demand protocols (AODV, DSR) flood **RREQ (Route Request)** packets; destination responds with **RREP**.
4. **Data Forwarding** — Packets traverse multi-hop paths via intermediate nodes.
5. **Route Maintenance** — **RERR (Route Error)** packets are sent upon link breakage; re-route triggered.

### VANET Operational Logic
1. **Vehicle Role Assignment** — Each vehicle equipped with **OBU (On-Board Unit)** acts as a node.
2. **DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communications)** — Operates in the **5.9 GHz** band (US) / 5.8 GHz (Europe) with bandwidth of **75 MHz**.
3. **Periodic Beaconing** — Vehicles broadcast **Cooperative Awareness Messages (CAMs)** at **1–10 Hz** containing position, velocity, acceleration.
4. **Infrastructure Assist** — **RSUs** deployed at intersections/highways provide Internet access, GPS correction, and content distribution.
5. **Geo-Routing** — Most VANET protocols are **position-based** (GPSR, A-STAR) since topology is highly volatile.

### FANET Operational Logic
1. **UAV Clustering** — A leader UAV coordinates a cluster of follower UAVs for hierarchical routing.
2. **3D Localization** — Each UAV has **GPS + IMU** for 3D coordinate awareness.
3. **LoS Optimization** — Path planning ensures **line-of-sight** to neighbor UAVs to minimize multi-path fading.
4. **Energy-Aware Routing** — Routing algorithms consider **battery State-of-Charge (SoC)** as a primary metric.
5. **Ground Control Station (GCS) Relay** — One or more UAVs act as **gateway nodes** connecting the swarm to terrestrial networks.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important comparison** for KTU 2024 exams on this topic. Memorize the column-wise contrasts.

| Parameter | MANET | VANET | FANET |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Node Type** | Mobile handheld/portable devices | Vehicles (cars, buses, trucks) | UAVs / Drones |
| **Mobility Space** | 2D (ground) | 2D (road-bound) | 3D (volumetric) |
| **Speed Range** | 0–30 m/s | 10–80 m/s | 10–100 m/s |
| **Mobility Pattern** | Random, unpredictable | Predictable, road-constrained | Semi-predictable (mission-based) |
| **Topology Change Rate** | Moderate | Very high | High but orchestrated |
| **Energy Constraint** | High (battery) | Negligible (vehicle battery) | Critical (UAV payload) |
| **Computational Power** | Moderate | High (OBU) | Moderate (limited by weight) |
| **Communication Standard** | IEEE 802.11 / 802.15 | IEEE 802.11p (WAVE) | IEEE 802.11 / 802.15.4 |
| **Routing Approach** | Topology-based (AODV, DSR) | Position-based (GPSR) | Swarm/Cluster-based |
| **Scalability** | Limited | High (large-scale) | Limited (small swarms) |
| **Primary Use-Case** | Tactical, disaster response | Intelligent Transport Systems | Aerial surveillance, delivery |
| **Channel Issues** | Multi-path fading, hidden terminals | Doppler shift (high speed) | Antenna misalignment, wind |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although this topic is primarily conceptual, the following quantitative metrics are frequently tested:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Formulas for Adhoc Networks**

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $N_c = \pi R^2 \cdot \rho$ | Node count within transmission radius ($R$) and node density ($\rho$) | nodes |
| $L_{max} = \frac{R}{d_{min}}$ | Maximum hop count where $d_{min}$ is minimum inter-node distance | hops |
| $T_{handoff} = \frac{2R}{v_{rel}}$ | Link sustainability time for relative velocity $v_{rel}$ | seconds |
| $P_{link} = e^{-\frac{t}{T_{handoff}}}$ | Probability a link survives for time $t$ | dimensionless |
| $E_{tx} = k \cdot d^n \cdot E_{elec} + E_{amp}$ | Energy consumed for transmission ($n$ = path-loss exponent) | Joules |
| $BER = \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Bit Error Rate for AWGN channel | dimensionless |
| $D_{fading} = \frac{v \cdot f_c}{c}$ | Maximum Doppler frequency ($v$ = velocity, $f_c$ = carrier, $c$ = speed of light) | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In VANETs, $D_{fading}$ is exceptionally high due to vehicle speed, which is why **IEEE 802.11p** uses a **shorter symbol duration** (8 μs vs 4 μs in 802.11a) to combat inter-carrier interference. In FANETs, $E_{tx}$ dominates the **energy budget** since UAVs must transmit over longer LoS distances, making **energy-efficient routing** the central research challenge.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Link Sustainability Time in Mobile Adhoc Networks

The **link sustainability time** (also called the **link lifetime** or **residence time**) is a foundational parameter that determines how long two mobile nodes can communicate before their relative motion causes one to exit the transmission range of the other.

### Step 1 — Problem Setup
Consider two nodes, Node A and Node B, separated by a distance $d$ where:

$$0 < d \leq R$$

where $R$ is the **transmission radius** of each node. Both nodes move with velocities $v_A$ and $v_B$ at angles $\theta_A$ and $\theta_B$ respectively relative to the line joining them.

### Step 2 — Relative Velocity
The **relative velocity** of node B with respect to node A is given by:

$$v_{rel} = \sqrt{v_A^2 + v_B^2 - 2 v_A v_B \cos(\theta_B - \theta_A)}$$

### Step 3 — Worst-Case Relative Motion
The **maximum relative velocity** occurs when both nodes move in **opposite directions** along the line joining them (i.e., $\theta_B - \theta_A = 180^\circ$):

$$v_{rel}^{max} = v_A + v_B$$

### Step 4 — Link Sustainability Time
The link breaks when the relative displacement equals $R - d$. Therefore, the **link sustainability time** is:

$$T_{link} = \frac{R - d}{v_{rel}^{max}} = \frac{R - d}{v_A + v_B}$$

### Step 5 — Average Link Lifetime
Averaging over all possible distances $d$ uniformly distributed in $[0, R]$:

$$\bar{T}_{link} = \frac{1}{R} \int_0^R \frac{R - d}{v_A + v_B} \, dd$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\bar{T}_{link} = \frac{1}{R(v_A + v_B)} \left[ Rd - \frac{d^2}{2} \right]_0^R$$

$$\bar{T}_{link} = \frac{1}{R(v_A + v_B)} \cdot \frac{R^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{T}_{link} = \frac{R}{2(v_A + v_B)}}$$

### Step 6 — Practical Interpretation
This means for **MANETs** with pedestrian speeds (≈1.5 m/s) and R = 100 m:

$$\bar{T}_{link}^{MANET} = \frac{100}{2(1.5 + 1.5)} = 16.67 \text{ seconds}$$

For **VANETs** with $v_A = v_B = 25$ m/s (highway speed) and R = 250 m:

$$\bar{T}_{link}^{VANET} = \frac{250}{2(25 + 25)} = 2.5 \text{ seconds}$$

For **FANETs** with $v_A = v_B = 50$ m/s (high-speed UAV) and R = 1000 m:

$$\bar{T}_{link}^{FANET} = \frac{1000}{2(50 + 50)} = 5 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** VANETs experience the **shortest link lifetimes** at highway speeds, which is why VANET routing protocols must be **extremely reactive and lightweight** to maintain connectivity. FANETs benefit from **larger transmission ranges** (line-of-sight), compensating for their high speeds.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Network Selection Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> The following decision matrix helps a KTU exam aspirant decide **which ad-hoc network** to recommend for a given scenario.

| Scenario / Requirement | Recommended Network | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Soldiers in a battlefield with no infrastructure | **MANET** | Random mobility, no road/path constraint, battery-powered |
| Collision avoidance in smart city traffic | **VANET** | Road-bound vehicles, requires V2V + V2I, time-critical |
| Surveillance of a 50 km² forest fire | **FANET** | Need aerial coverage, LoS to ground, 3D mobility |
| Conference room of 30 people sharing files | **MANET** | Low speed, small area, ad-hoc setup |
| Highway platoon driving (truck convoy) | **VANET** | High speed, lane-bound, requires coordination |
| Agricultural crop health monitoring | **FANET** | Must fly over fields, GPS-guided, long endurance |
| Earthquake rescue in collapsed building | **MANET** | Foot-mobile rescuers, indoor, infrastructure destroyed |
| Drone light show / aerial filming | **FANET** | Precise 3D formation control required |
| Bus fleet passenger Wi-Fi | **VANET** | RSUs at bus stops, vehicles act as gateways |
| Submarine / underwater mesh | **Not in scope** (uses underwater acoustic networks) | — |

---

## 3.3 Energy Model Derivation (FANET-Centric)

Since FANETs are the **most energy-constrained**, KTU 2024 frequently tests the energy model.

### Transmission Energy ($E_{tx}$)
The energy required to transmit a $k$-bit packet over distance $d$:

$$E_{tx}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot E_{amp} \cdot d^n$$

where:
- $E_{elec}$ = energy/bit consumed by the **transmitter electronics** (typically **50 nJ/bit**)
- $E_{amp}$ = energy/bit consumed by the **transmit amplifier** (typically **100 pJ/bit/m²** for free space, $n=2$)
- $n$ = path-loss exponent (**2** for free space, **3–4** for multipath)

### Reception Energy ($E_{rx}$)
Reception consumes only electronics energy:

$$E_{rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

### Total Energy for One Hop
For a packet relayed over distance $d$ through a relay node:

$$E_{hop} = E_{tx}(k, d) + E_{rx}(k) = 2k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot E_{amp} \cdot d^n$$

### Multi-Hop Aggregation
For a path of $m$ hops, each covering distance $d/m$ of the total path length $D$:

$$E_{total} = m \cdot \left[ 2k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot E_{amp} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{m}\right)^n \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In FANETs, since $d$ can be large (LoS over kilometers), increasing the number of hops (m) reduces the per-hop distance, but each intermediate hop **doubles the electronics energy cost**. The optimal $m$ balances **amplifier savings vs. relay overhead**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Adhoc Networks

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Adhoc Wireless Networks] --> B[MANET]
    A --> C[VANET]
    A --> D[FANET]
    A --> E[Other Variants]
    E --> F[SPAN Sensor Networks]
    E --> G[WBAN Body Area Networks]
    E --> H[Underwater Networks]
    B --> B1[Nodes Pedestrian Speed 0 to 30 m/s]
    B --> B2[Random 2D Mobility]
    B --> B3[Battery Limited Energy]
    B --> B4[Protocols AODV DSR OLSR]
    C --> C1[Vehicles Road Bound 10 to 80 m/s]
    C --> C2[Predictable Mobility along Roads]
    C --> C3[Unlimited Vehicle Power]
    C --> C4[Standard IEEE 802.11p WAVE]
    C --> C5[Components OBU and RSU]
    D --> D1[UAVs and Drones 10 to 100 m/s]
    D --> D2[3D Volumetric Mobility]
    D --> D3[Critical Battery Constraint]
    D --> D4[LoS Dominant Communication]
    D --> D5[Swarm and Cluster Routing]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Architecture of FANET

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Ground Control Station GCS] -->|Command and Telemetry| B[Leader UAV]
    B -->|Multi Hop Relay| C[Follower UAV 1]
    B -->|Multi Hop Relay| D[Follower UAV 2]
    C -->|Multi Hop Relay| E[Follower UAV 3]
    D -->|Multi Hop Relay| E
    C -->|Sensor Data| F[Ground Sensor Node]
    D -->|Sensor Data| G[Target Object under Surveillance]
    E -->|Long Range LoS| H[Satellite Backhaul]
    A -->|Internet Gateway| I[Cloud Server]
    B -.->|Beacon GPS Position| C
    B -.->|Beacon GPS Position| D
    C -.->|Beacon GPS Position| E
    D -.->|Beacon GPS Position| E
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: VANET Communication Modes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[VANET Communication Modes] --> B[V2V Vehicle to Vehicle]
    A --> C[V2I Vehicle to Infrastructure]
    A --> D[V2R Vehicle to Roadside]
    A --> E[V2P Vehicle to Pedestrian]
    A --> F[V2X Vehicle to Everything]
    B --> B1[Used for collision avoidance and cooperative driving]
    C --> C1[Vehicle to cellular tower or Internet backbone]
    D --> D1[Vehicle to RSU Roadside Unit at intersection]
    E --> E1[Alerts to vulnerable road users]
    F --> F1[Umbrella term for all V2X communication]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: MANET Routing Protocol Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[MANET Routing Protocols] --> B[Proactive Table Driven]
    A --> C[Reactive On Demand]
    A --> D[Hybrid]
    B --> B1[DSDV]
    B --> B2[OLSR]
    B --> B3[CGSR]
    C --> C1[AODV]
    C --> C2[DSR]
    C --> C3[TORA]
    D --> D1[ZRP Zone Routing Protocol]
    D --> D2[HSR]
    B1 --> X[Maintain routing tables periodically]
    C1 --> Y[Discover route only when needed]
    D1 --> Z[Combine both strategies]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU 2024:** Examiners frequently award 2–3 marks for "draw the architecture of VANET" or "classify FANET routing" type questions. The mermaid-rendered ASCII flow diagrams above can be **redrawn as labelled block diagrams** in your answer sheet for full credit.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define Mobile Adhoc Network (MANET). List any two characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**
A Mobile Adhoc Network (MANET) is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of mobile devices connected wirelessly, where each node acts as both a host and a router, dynamically establishing multi-hop paths for data communication.

**Two characteristics:**
1. **Dynamic topology** — Nodes move freely, causing continuous topology changes.
2. **Energy-constrained operation** — Most nodes are battery-powered, limiting transmission power and lifetime.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]`
**Differentiate between V2V and V2I communication in VANETs.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle) | V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Direct communication between two vehicles | Communication between a vehicle and a fixed infrastructure (e.g., RSU) |
| Range | Short-to-medium (typically 100–300 m) | Can be longer with cellular or RSU coverage |
| Purpose | Collision avoidance, cooperative driving | Internet access, traffic updates, infotainment |
| Dependency | Requires only on-board OBU | Requires deployed RSUs or cellular towers |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

> **"Explain in detail the three main types of ad-hoc networks: MANET, VANET, and FANET, with suitable diagrams. Compare their characteristics in a tabular form."**

#### Part (a) — Detailed Explanation (7 Marks)

**MANET (Mobile Ad-Hoc Network):**
A MANET is a collection of mobile nodes that form a temporary network without any pre-existing infrastructure. Each node is autonomous and can move in any direction, leading to arbitrary and unpredictable topology changes. The mobile devices are typically handheld (smartphones, laptops, PDAs) or vehicle-mounted, and operate on battery power. Routing is challenging due to frequent link breakages, and protocols such as **AODV (Ad-hoc On-demand Distance Vector)** and **DSR (Dynamic Source Routing)** are commonly employed. MANETs are widely used in **military tactical networks, disaster recovery operations, and conference-room file sharing**.

**VANET (Vehicular Ad-Hoc Network):**
A VANET is a specialized form of MANET where the nodes are vehicles equipped with **On-Board Units (OBUs)** that communicate wirelessly. Two primary communication modes exist: **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle)** for peer cooperation and **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure)** through **Roadside Units (RSUs)**. The communication standard is **IEEE 802.11p (WAVE)**. Vehicles move at high speeds but follow road topology, making mobility **partially predictable**. Applications include **collision avoidance, traffic signal coordination, and autonomous driving**. Vehicle batteries provide nearly unlimited power.

**FANET (Flying Ad-Hoc Network):**
A FANET consists of **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs)** communicating with each other in a 3D space. Unlike MANETs and VANETs, FANETs exhibit **volumetric (3D) mobility** since drones are not constrained to a 2D plane. The **Ground Control Station (GCS)** acts as the central coordinator, while the UAVs form an aerial mesh. Routing is typically **swarm-based or cluster-based**, with a leader-follower hierarchy. FANETs benefit from **long line-of-sight (LoS) links** since air-to-air propagation suffers less multipath fading. Energy is a critical constraint since UAVs are payload- and battery-limited. Applications include **aerial surveillance, search & rescue, precision agriculture, and military reconnaissance**.

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 Marks)

| Feature | MANET | VANET | FANET |
|---|---|---|---|
| Node Type | Mobile handheld devices | Vehicles (cars, trucks) | UAVs / Drones |
| Mobility Dimension | 2D | 2D (road-bound) | 3D (volumetric) |
| Node Speed | 0–30 m/s | 10–80 m/s | 10–100 m/s |
| Mobility Pattern | Random | Predictable (road-bound) | Mission-based / orchestrated |
| Energy Constraint | High (battery-limited) | Low (vehicle-powered) | Critical (UAV battery) |
| Primary Standard | IEEE 802.11 | IEEE 802.11p (WAVE) | IEEE 802.11 / 802.15.4 |
| Routing Approach | Topology-based | Position-based | Cluster / swarm-based |
| Example Use | Battlefield, disaster recovery | Smart city, ITS, autonomous cars | Aerial surveillance, delivery drones |
| Scalability | Limited | High | Limited |
| Main Challenge | Energy & routing overhead | High mobility & link lifetime | Energy & antenna orientation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Many students **conflate MANET and VANET**, claiming "VANET is just MANET in vehicles." This is **wrong**. The examiner expects you to highlight the **uniqueness of VANET** — namely, the **hybrid V2V + V2I architecture, predictable mobility, and IEEE 802.11p standard**. Likewise, do **not** describe FANET as a "MANET in the air" — emphasize the **3D mobility, swarm hierarchy, and critical energy constraint**. **Loses 2 marks** if these distinctions are not made explicit.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]`

> **"With neat diagrams, describe the architecture and applications of FANETs. Discuss in detail the challenges faced by FANET routing protocols."**

#### Part (a) — Architecture and Applications of FANET (7 Marks)

**Architecture:**
A FANET is typically organized in a **hierarchical cluster-based architecture**, comprising:
1. **Ground Control Station (GCS)** — The central command and monitoring entity on the ground.
2. **Leader UAV** — Coordinates the swarm, aggregates data, and relays to the GCS.
3. **Follower UAVs** — Execute the mission (e.g., surveillance, spraying) and forward data to the leader.
4. **Relay UAVs** (optional) — Extend communication range through multi-hop aerial links.
5. **Satellite/Backhaul Link** — Connects the leader to cloud infrastructure for persistent data storage.

Each UAV is equipped with a **GPS module** (for 3D position), an **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** (for orientation), a **wireless transceiver** (typically IEEE 802.11 in ad-hoc mode or 802.15.4), and **mission-specific sensors** (camera, LiDAR, multispectral imager).

**Applications:**
- **Military reconnaissance** — Border surveillance, target tracking.
- **Precision agriculture** — Crop health monitoring, pesticide spraying.
- **Search and rescue** — Locating survivors in disaster zones.
- **Delivery logistics** — Amazon Prime Air, medical supply delivery.
- **Aerial cinematography** — Coordinated camera drones for film production.
- **Environmental monitoring** — Air quality, wildfire tracking, wildlife observation.
- **Telecommunication relay** — Temporary cellular coverage in disaster-struck areas.

#### Part (b) — Challenges in FANET Routing (7 Marks)

1. **High 3D Mobility** — UAVs move in all three dimensions at high speeds, leading to rapid link breakages and frequent topology changes.

2. **Energy Constraints** — UAV battery capacity is limited by payload weight. Routing algorithms must incorporate **battery State-of-Charge (SoC)** as a primary metric, unlike MANET/VANET routing.

3. **Antenna Orientation Issues** — Unlike ground-based networks with fixed antenna orientations, UAV antennas rotate and tilt, causing **polarization mismatch** and signal loss.

4. **Line-of-Sight Dependence** — Although LoS is generally available in air-to-air links, it can be **obstructed by buildings, terrain, or other UAVs**, leading to multi-path fading.

5. **Scalability Limitations** — Most FANET protocols are validated only for small swarms (≤ 10 UAVs). Scaling to **hundreds of UAVs** introduces **control-packet overhead** that saturates the limited bandwidth.

6. **Security Vulnerabilities** — Wireless aerial links are susceptible to **jamming, eavesdropping, and GPS spoofing**, demanding lightweight encryption and authentication mechanisms.

7. **Heterogeneous UAV Capabilities** — Different UAVs may have different **payload, range, and battery capacity**, making uniform routing difficult.

8. **Wind and Environmental Disturbances** — Atmospheric turbulence causes UAV drift, which must be **compensated in the routing metric** (using position-prediction algorithms).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> When answering the "FANET challenges" question, students often **copy generic MANET challenges** (e.g., "hidden terminal problem," "mobility"). This shows the student has **not understood FANET-specific** issues. The examiner awards full marks **only when answers highlight the *uniqueness* of FANET** — 3D mobility, antenna orientation, energy constraint, and LoS dependence. **Loses 3 marks** for generic MANET/VANET answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 1: Types of Adhoc Networks**

- **Adhoc Network** = infrastructure-less, self-configuring, multi-hop, decentralized wireless network.
- **MANET** = general-purpose, **2D random mobility**, battery-powered, topology-based routing (AODV/DSR).
- **VANET** = vehicle-based, **road-bound 2D mobility**, **V2V + V2I**, IEEE 802.11p (WAVE), position-based routing (GPSR), nearly unlimited energy.
- **FANET** = UAV-based, **3D volumetric mobility**, **swarm/cluster routing**, **energy-critical**, long LoS links, **MAVLink protocol** common.
- **Speed Order (Typical):** MANET < VANET < FANET.
- **Energy Constraint Severity:** FANET > MANET > VANET.
- **Mobility Predictability:** VANET > FANET > MANET.
- **Key Standard for VANET:** **IEEE 802.11p** (5.9 GHz, 75 MHz bandwidth in US).
- **Key Standard for FANET communication:** IEEE 802.11 (ad-hoc mode) / 802.15.4.
- **Link Sustainability Formula:** $\bar{T}_{link} = \dfrac{R}{2(v_A + v_B)}$
- **Doppler Shift Formula:** $D_{fading} = \dfrac{v \cdot f_c}{c}$
- **VANET Components to Remember:** **OBU (On-Board Unit)**, **RSU (Roadside Unit)**, **CAM (Cooperative Awareness Message)**.
- **FANET Components to Remember:** **UAV / Drone**, **GCS (Ground Control Station)**, **IMU + GPS** for 3D localization.
- **MANET Routing Families:** **Proactive** (DSDV, OLSR), **Reactive** (AODV, DSR), **Hybrid** (ZRP).
- **VANET vs MANET key distinction:** VANET has **predictable road-bound mobility** and uses **RSUs**, MANET does not.
- **FANET vs MANET key distinction:** FANET has **3D mobility** and **critical energy constraints**, MANET has 2D random mobility and moderate energy.
- **FANET Communication Modes:** **UAV-to-UAV (A2A)**, **UAV-to-GCS (A2G)**, **UAV-to-Satellite (A2S)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When a question asks "**Compare MANET and VANET**" or "**Why is FANET different from MANET?**", the examiner is **specifically looking for the distinguishing feature in bold terms** — *road-bound mobility*, *3D mobility*, *energy constraints*, *standards used*. Do not write generic descriptions; **pinpoint the contrasts** to score full marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Challenges in Adhoc Networks: Scalability, Mobility, and Energy Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT863 - ADHOC AND WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Challenges in Adhoc Networks: Scalability, Mobility, and Energy Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Ad Hoc Network** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less collection of autonomous wireless nodes that dynamically form a temporary multi-hop network without centralized administration. The three principal design challenges explicitly mandated in the KTU 2024 Module-1 syllabus are:

1. **Scalability** — The ability of the network protocols (routing, MAC, security) to maintain acceptable performance (throughput, delay, packet delivery ratio) as the number of nodes $N$ and network area grow.
2. **Mobility** — The continuous, unpredictable change in node positions leading to dynamic topology, broken links, and route instability.
3. **Energy Efficiency** — The optimization of the limited battery power of mobile nodes to maximize network operational lifetime, since recharging is often infeasible in deployed scenarios (battlefield, forest, disaster zones).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-1 Highlight:** These three challenges are *interdependent* — improving scalability by adding more control packets increases energy drain, and aggressive mobility handling through frequent re-routing multiplies the energy cost. The exam frequently asks students to explain this trade-off.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Moving Flash-Mob"

Imagine a group of **30 strangers** standing in a large, dark football stadium. Each person carries only a **small torch (wireless radio)** and a **single AA battery (energy budget)**. They must collectively deliver a message from the entrance gate (source) to the exit gate (destination).

- **Scalability** — If instead of 30 people there are 3000, the shouting and torch-passing becomes chaotic; too many people crowd around one torch-bearer (bottleneck).
- **Mobility** — People keep moving to find better seats; the person you handed the message to may have walked 50 meters away by the time you finish the sentence — the chain of torch-passers **breaks**.
- **Energy Efficiency** — Each torch has only 1 hour of battery. If everyone keeps the torch on at full brightness *all the time* (idle listening, retransmissions), the message dies before it reaches the exit.

This is exactly the dilemma in a Mobile Ad Hoc Network (MANET).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are the *quantitative yardsticks* KTU examiners use to grade answers on this topic.

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Target |
|---|---|---|
| **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** | $\eta$ | $\geq 95\%$ |
| **End-to-End Delay** | $D$ | $< 500 \text{ ms}$ |
| **Routing Overhead** | $R_{oh}$ | $< 50\%$ (control/total) |
| **Network Lifetime** | $T_L$ | Maximize |
| **Throughput per Node** | $S_{node}$ | Non-zero as $N \to \infty$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Challenge 1 — Scalability

### 2.1.1 The Core "Why"
In a flat (proactive) routing protocol like **DSR** or **OLSR**, every node may maintain a route to every other node. The number of possible routes in a network of $N$ nodes is $O(N^2)$. As $N$ grows, the **routing table size**, **control message count**, and **collision probability** all explode.

### 2.1.2 Operational Breakdown
- **Proactive Protocols (Table-Driven):** Update tables continuously → high overhead in large $N$.
- **Reactive Protocols (On-Demand):** Discover route only when needed → still suffer from **route cache pollution** when $N$ is large.
- **Hierarchical Protocols (e.g., ZRP, Clustering):** Group nodes into clusters → scalability improves but cluster-head becomes a **single point of failure**.

### 2.1.3 The Scalability Equation
A well-known result (Gupta & Kumar, 2000) for a random ad hoc network states that the **per-node throughput capacity** scales as:

$$
S_{node} = \Theta\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{N \log N}}\right)
$$

This means as you **double** the nodes, each node's fair share of bandwidth drops by roughly $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$. This is the *fundamental scalability ceiling* of any flat ad hoc protocol.

## 2.2 Challenge 2 — Mobility

### 2.2.1 The Core "Why"
A wireless link between two nodes $i$ and $j$ exists **only while** they remain within radio range $R$. If either moves, the link may break. The **relative mobility** between nodes determines link lifetime.

### 2.2.2 Mobility Models (KTU Frequently Tested)
1. **Random Waypoint Model:** Node picks a random destination, travels at random speed $v \in [v_{min}, v_{max}]$, pauses, repeats.
2. **Random Direction Model:** Node picks a random direction and travels to the boundary.
3. **Reference Point Group Mobility (RPGM):** Nodes move in groups (used in battlefield simulations).

### 2.2.3 Link-Breaking Equation
For two nodes moving with relative velocity $v_{rel}$ at relative angle $\theta$, the **time until link breakage** is:

$$
T_{link} = \frac{R - d_0}{v_{rel} \cos\theta}
$$

where $d_0$ is the current inter-node distance and $R$ is the transmission range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobility's Hidden Cost:** A reactive protocol must perform **route discovery** (RREQ flood) every time a link breaks. Under high mobility, the routing overhead can exceed **80% of total bandwidth**, starving the actual data traffic.

## 2.3 Challenge 3 — Energy Efficiency

### 2.3.1 The Core "Why"
A node's radio is the single largest power consumer. Energy is spent in four states: **Transmit ($T_x$)**, **Receive ($R_x$)**, **Idle Listening ($I$)**, and **Sleep ($S$)**.

### 2.3.2 The First-Order Radio Energy Model
For transmitting a $k$-bit packet over distance $d$ with path-loss exponent $\alpha$:

$$
E_{Tx}(k, d) = 
\begin{cases}
E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}, & d < d_0 \\
E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4}, & d \geq d_0
\end{cases}
$$

The reception energy (no distance term):

$$
E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

The crossover distance:

$$
d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table — it is the *minimum* required for Part-B answers on this topic.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_{node} = \Theta\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{N \log N}}\right)$ | Per-node throughput ceiling | Bits/sec |
| 2 | $T_{link} = \frac{R - d_0}{v_{rel} \cos\theta}$ | Link lifetime under mobility | Seconds |
| 3 | $E_{Tx} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon \cdot k \cdot d^{\alpha}$ | Transmit energy | Joules |
| 4 | $E_{Rx} = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | Receive energy | Joules |
| 5 | $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs} / \epsilon_{mp}}$ | Free-space to multipath crossover | Meters |
| 6 | $R_{oh} = \frac{\text{Control Pkts}}{\text{Total Pkts}}$ | Routing Overhead Ratio | Unitless (0-1) |
| 7 | $T_L = \min_{i \in V} \{ \text{time until } E_i = 0 \}$ | Network Lifetime | Seconds |
| 8 | $\eta = \frac{\text{Pkts Received}}{\text{Pkts Sent}}$ | Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR) | Unitless (0-1) |
| 9 | $D = \frac{1}{N_p} \sum_{i=1}^{N_p} (t_{recv,i} - t_{sent,i})$ | Average End-to-End Delay | Seconds |
| 10 | $P_{idle} \approx 0.8 P_{Tx}$ | Idle listening power (often forgotten) | Watts |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Which Challenge Dominates |
|---|---|
| **Battlefield MANET (soldiers/tanks)** | Mobility + Energy |
| **Wildlife tracking sensor field** | Scalability + Energy |
| **Vehicular Ad Hoc Networks (VANETs)** | Mobility (extreme: 0-200 km/h) |
| **Disaster-rescue networks** | All three simultaneously |
| **IoT Smart Farming** | Energy Efficiency (battery-only, 5-year lifetime) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Gupta-Kumar Scalability Ceiling

**Goal:** Show that per-node throughput $\to 0$ as $N \to \infty$ in a flat random ad hoc network.

**Step 1 — Set up the network model.**

Consider $N$ nodes uniformly distributed in a disk of area $A = 1 \text{ m}^2$ (normalized). Each node transmits at rate $W$ bits/sec using a fixed transmission range $R$.

**Step 2 — Identify the number of hops.**

The average number of hops between a source and destination is:

$$
\bar{h} = \Theta(\sqrt{N})
$$

**Reasoning:** The network diameter scales as $\sqrt{N}$ because $N$ nodes are spread over a unit area, so the typical source-destination Euclidean distance is $\Theta(1)$ and each hop covers distance $\Theta(1/\sqrt{N})$.

**Step 3 — Compute the per-hop capacity.**

Because each transmission *interferes* with neighboring transmissions in a disc of radius $(1 + \Delta) \cdot R$, the network can support at most:

$$
\text{Concurrent Transmissions} = \Theta\left(\frac{1}{N \cdot R^2}\right) = \Theta(1)
$$

After careful spatial-reuse analysis, the **aggregate network capacity** is:

$$
C_{agg} = \Theta(W \sqrt{N})
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by the number of nodes.**

Fair sharing among $N$ flows (one per node) gives:

$$
S_{node} = \frac{C_{agg}}{N} = \Theta\left(\frac{W \sqrt{N}}{N}\right) = \Theta\left(\frac{W}{\sqrt{N}}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the trend.**

$$
\boxed{S_{node} = \Theta\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)}
$$

**Interpretation:** Doubling $N$ cuts each node's throughput by $1/\sqrt{2}$. This is the **fundamental scalability barrier** that no flat protocol can overcome.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Energy Per Bit Over an End-to-End Path

**Goal:** Compute the total energy spent delivering one bit from source to destination over an $H$-hop path.

**Step 1 — Energy to transmit one bit over a single hop of distance $d_i$ (in free-space regime, $d_i < d_0$):**

$$
E_{Tx,1} = E_{elec} + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_i^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy to receive one bit:**

$$
E_{Rx,1} = E_{elec}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum over the $H$ intermediate relays (each relay = 1 Tx + 1 Rx):**

$$
E_{relay} = \sum_{i=1}^{H-1} \left( E_{elec} + \epsilon_{fs} d_i^{2} + E_{elec} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Add source transmit and destination receive:**

$$
E_{total} = (E_{elec} + \epsilon_{fs} d_{src}^{2}) + E_{relay} + E_{elec}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine like terms.** Each hop contributes two $E_{elec}$ terms (one Tx, one Rx) except endpoints:

$$
\boxed{E_{total} = 2H \cdot E_{elec} + \epsilon_{fs} \sum_{i=1}^{H} d_i^{2}}
$$

**Insight:** This derivation proves a **counter-intuitive result** — *receiving is just as expensive as transmitting electronics-wise*! Thus, simply reducing transmission power is not enough; the protocol must also **minimize the number of receivers** (e.g., via sleep scheduling) to save energy.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Link-Breaking Time Under Random Waypoint Mobility

**Goal:** Derive the mean link lifetime $\mathbb{E}[T_{link}]$.

**Step 1 — Set up the relative motion model.**

Two nodes A and B move independently with speeds $v_A$ and $v_B$ chosen uniformly from $[0, v_{max}]$. The relative speed magnitude is:

$$
v_{rel} = \sqrt{v_A^{2} + v_B^{2} - 2 v_A v_B \cos\phi}
$$

where $\phi$ is the angle between their velocity vectors (uniform in $[0, 2\pi]$).

**Step 2 — Take the expectation.**

For a uniform speed distribution and uniform angle, the expected relative speed is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[v_{rel}] = \frac{128 \cdot v_{max}}{45 \pi} \approx 0.905 \cdot v_{max}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the link-lifetime equation.**

With $\mathbb{E}[\cos\theta] = 0$ (isotropic), the mean time to leave the transmission disc of radius $R$ is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{link}] = \frac{R}{\mathbb{E}[v_{rel}]} = \frac{R}{0.905 \, v_{max}} \approx \frac{1.105 \, R}{v_{max}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathbb{E}[T_{link}] \approx \frac{R}{v_{max}}}
$$

**Insight:** If a node moves at $v_{max} = 10$ m/s with radio range $R = 250$ m, the **average link lasts only 25 seconds**. A route discovered at $t=0$ will be stale by $t=25$ s — the routing protocol must *re-discover* frequently.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Challenge Trade-offs

| Design Lever | Effect on Scalability | Effect on Mobility | Effect on Energy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase Tx power (range $R$) | ✅ Fewer hops, larger area covered | ❌ More interference, hidden terminals | ❌ $d^4$ energy explosion |
| Reduce Tx power | ✅ Spatial reuse | ❌ More broken links | ✅ Less transmit energy |
| Frequent HELLO beacons | ✅ Updated topology | ✅ Faster link-failure detection | ❌ Idle listening overhead |
| Sleep scheduling (Duty cycling) | ❌ Higher latency | ❌ Delayed response | ✅ **Massive energy save** |
| Hierarchical clustering | ✅ Localized routing | ❌ Cluster re-election cost | ✅ Inter-cluster traffic reduced |
| Multipath routing | ✅ Robustness | ✅ Path redundancy | ❌ More Tx + Rx energy |
| Predictive mobility models | ❌ Complex | ✅ Proactive hand-off | ✅ Avoid re-discoveries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do **not** claim a technique improves *all three* challenges simultaneously. Every lever is a trade-off. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for one-sided "this solves everything" answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Taxonomy of Ad Hoc Network Challenges (Mermaid Mind-Map)

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Adhoc Network Challenges))
    Scalability
      ProactiveOverload
      ReactiveFlood
      CachePollution
      HierarchicalClustering
    Mobility
      LinkBreakage
      RouteStaleness
      RandomWaypoint
      RPGM
      VehicularVANET
    EnergyEfficiency
      FirstOrderRadioModel
      SleepScheduling
      DutyCycling
      PowerControl
      NetworkLifetime
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Interaction / Trade-off Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Increased Node Count N] --> B[Routing Overhead Rise]
    B --> C[Bandwidth Saturation]
    C --> D[Throughput per Node Falls]
    D --> E[Scalability Bottleneck]

    F[Node Mobility vmax] --> G[Link Breakage Rate]
    G --> H[Route Re-discovery Floods]
    H --> I[Control Packet Surge]
    I --> E

    J[Battery Capacity E0] --> K[Tx and Rx Energy]
    K --> L[Network Lifetime TL]
    L --> M[First Node Dies FND]
    M --> N[Partition and Coverage Loss]

    E --> O[Reactive Route Discovery]
    O --> P[More Control Packets]
    P --> K
    N --> Q[Network Becomes Inoperable]

    style A fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#add8e6,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333
    style Q fill:#ff6347,color:#fff,stroke:#333
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The three **input stressors** (orange / blue / green) are the challenges.
- They feed into the three **internal pathologies** (pink / red boxes).
- The loop $O \rightarrow P \rightarrow K$ shows the **deadly feedback**: poor scalability triggers more route discoveries, which **drain the battery**, which causes nodes to die, which *worsens* scalability. This is the central reason ad hoc network design is hard.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — State-Transition of a Node's Radio (Energy View)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Sleep
    Sleep --> Idle: WakeUpTimer
    Idle --> Transmit: PacketToSend
    Transmit --> Idle: TxComplete
    Idle --> Receive: CarrierSense
    Receive --> Idle: RxComplete
    Idle --> Sleep: NoActivityTimeout
    Transmit --> Sleep: LowBatteryCutoff
    Receive --> Sleep: LowBatteryCutoff
    Sleep --> [*]: BatteryDepleted

    note right of Sleep
      PSleep approx 0
      Saves 80 to 90 percent energy
    end note

    note right of Idle
      PIdle approx 0.8 PTx
      Hidden energy drain
    end note

    note right of Transmit
      PTx highest
      Function of distance d
    end note
```

**Block-Level Functional Insight:**
- The **Idle state** consumes nearly as much power as transmission — this is the single largest source of energy waste in ad hoc networks.
- The **Sleep-to-Idle transition** is the heart of every energy-saving MAC protocol (S-MAC, B-MAC, ZigBee beacons).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Why is scalability considered a fundamental challenge in Mobile Ad Hoc Networks? Mention the Gupta-Kumar capacity result."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Scalability is a fundamental challenge because as the number of nodes $N$ increases, the number of possible source-destination pairs grows as $O(N^2)$, leading to **routing table explosion**, **control message flooding**, and **MAC-layer contention**. According to the Gupta-Kumar result (2000), the per-node throughput capacity of a flat random ad hoc network scales as $S_{node} = \Theta(W/\sqrt{N})$. This means that **doubling the network size reduces each node's fair throughput by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$**, regardless of protocol design.
