Hence, scalability is bounded by a *physical-layer information-theoretic limit*, not merely a protocol inefficiency. *(3 marks: 1 for challenge identification + 1 for the formula + 1 for the interpretation.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"Briefly explain the first-order radio energy model and the role of the crossover distance $d_0$."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The first-order radio energy model (Heinzelman, 2000) gives:

$$
E_{Tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{\alpha}
$$

with $\alpha = 2$ (free-space) when $d < d_0$ and $\alpha = 4$ (multipath) when $d \ge d_0$. The crossover distance $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}}$ marks the boundary where the dominant loss mechanism changes from free-space propagation to two-ray ground reflection. Beyond $d_0$, energy cost grows as $d^4$, making long-range transmission **prohibitively expensive**. This justifies the use of **multi-hop short-range** transmission in ad hoc networks. *(3 marks: 1 model + 1 crossover + 1 multi-hop justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the three primary challenges — **scalability, mobility, and energy efficiency** — in mobile ad hoc networks. For each, state the **root cause**, **quantitative metric**, and **one representative solution technique**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **Gupta-Kumar per-node throughput ceiling** for a random ad hoc network and explain why flat protocols cannot escape this bound.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Scalability [2.5 marks]**
- **Root cause:** Growth of routing state and MAC contention as $N$ rises.
- **Metric:** Per-node throughput $S_{node}$ and routing overhead $R_{oh}$.
- **Solution:** Hierarchical routing (clustering, ZRP) or geographic routing (GPSR).

**2. Mobility [2.5 marks]**
- **Root cause:** Continuous change in node positions, leading to link breakage.
- **Metric:** Mean link lifetime $\mathbb{E}[T_{link}] \approx R/v_{max}$.
- **Solution:** Predictive / anticipatory routing (PRAODV, AODV with link prediction), or position-aware protocols.

**3. Energy Efficiency [2 marks]**
- **Root cause:** Battery-powered radios; energy consumed in Tx, Rx, Idle, Sleep states.
- **Metric:** Network lifetime $T_L = \min_i \{\text{time until } E_i = 0\}$.
- **Solution:** Sleep scheduling (S-MAC, B-MAC), transmit power control, data aggregation.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1** — Model: $N$ nodes uniform in unit disk; each with range $R$, rate $W$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2** — Network diameter and number of hops: $\bar{h} = \Theta(\sqrt{N})$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3** — Spatial reuse: a disc of area $\pi R^2$ supports at most one transmission; number of concurrent transmissions $\Theta(1/R^2) = \Theta(N)$ in a unit area. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4** — Aggregate throughput: $C_{agg} = \Theta(W \sqrt{N})$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5** — Per-node share: $S_{node} = C_{agg}/N = \Theta(W/\sqrt{N})$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6** — Why flat protocols cannot escape: the bound is **information-theoretic**, derived from interference geometry, not protocol-specific. No flat protocol (proactive, reactive, or hybrid) can change the underlying physics of wireless interference. To beat this, the network must become **non-flat** — i.e., use infrastructure (cellular), directional antennas, or MIMO. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** With the **first-order radio energy model**, derive the **total energy consumed in delivering one bit** over a multi-hop path of $H$ hops with hop distances $\{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_H\}$. State the *counter-intuitive* insight that emerges from this derivation.

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider a sensor field with $N = 100$ nodes, transmission range $R = 50$ m, and maximum node speed $v_{max} = 5$ m/s. Compute (i) the **expected link lifetime** under the random waypoint model, and (ii) the **per-node throughput** assuming channel rate $W = 1$ Mbps. Comment on whether this network is "scalable" by KTU 2024 norms.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1** — Energy to transmit $k$ bits over distance $d_i$ (free-space regime):

$$
E_{Tx}(k, d_i) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d_i^{2}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2** — Energy to receive $k$ bits (no distance term):

$$
E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3** — For a single hop, total energy = $E_{Tx} + E_{Rx} = 2 E_{elec} k + \epsilon_{fs} k d_i^{2}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4** — Sum over $H$ hops (each hop = 1 Tx + 1 Rx, except the source and destination contribute one of each):

$$
E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{H} (E_{Tx,i} + E_{Rx,i}) = 2H \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{H} d_i^{2}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**Step 5 — Counter-intuitive insight:** Each relay node spends energy on **receiving** equal to its electronic transmit cost. Idle listening and receiving often dominate total energy. Therefore, **reducing transmission range alone is insufficient**; we must also **minimize overhearing** via sleep scheduling. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Link Lifetime [3 marks]**

Using the derived formula:

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{link}] \approx \frac{R}{v_{max}} = \frac{50 \text{ m}}{5 \text{ m/s}} = 10 \text{ seconds}
$$

**(ii) Per-node Throughput [3 marks]**

Using the Gupta-Kumar formula:

$$
S_{node} = \Theta\left(\frac{W}{\sqrt{N}}\right) = \frac{1 \text{ Mbps}}{\sqrt{100}} = \frac{1 \text{ Mbps}}{10} = 100 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Comment [1 mark]:** With $S_{node} = 100$ kbps, the network supports a *modest* per-node rate. However, with link lifetime of only 10 s, a reactive protocol will flood the network with RREQ packets at least every 10 s, eating roughly 80% of this 100 kbps. Hence, this network is **only marginally scalable** by KTU 2024 norms; clustering or directional antennas are recommended.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Writing the Gupta-Kumar formula without the $W$ factor or the $\log N$ refinement** — if the question says "as $N$ doubles", you must show the $\sqrt{2}$ reduction explicitly. *(–1 mark)*
> 2. **Confusing per-node throughput with aggregate throughput** — the former *decreases* with $N$, the latter *increases*. Examiners check this distinction strictly. *(–1 mark)*
> 3. **Forgetting that $E_{Rx} = E_{elec} \cdot k$ has NO distance term** — students often wrongly add a $d^2$ or $d^4$ to receive energy. *(–1 mark)*
> 4. **Claiming that clustering solves scalability, mobility, *and* energy simultaneously** — it improves scalability but adds cluster-head bottleneck. Always state the trade-off. *(–1 mark)*
> 5. **Using the wrong $\alpha$** — free-space is $\alpha = 2$, multipath is $\alpha = 4$. Mixing them up loses the 1 mark for the crossover distance. *(–1 mark)*
> 6. **Omitting units in the final answer** — KTU answers must include units (bits/sec, Joules, meters, seconds). Unit-less numbers are penalized 0.5 marks. *(–0.5 marks)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Scalability** is bounded by information theory, not by protocol: $S_{node} = \Theta(W/\sqrt{N})$.
- **Mobility** breaks links at a rate of $\approx v_{max}/R$, so mean link lifetime $\approx R/v_{max}$.
- **Energy** is consumed in **all four** radio states; idle listening is the silent killer (≈ 80% of $P_{Tx}$).
- The **first-order radio model** uses $d^2$ in free-space and $d^4$ in multipath, separated by $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}}$.
- **Receive energy has no distance term** — this is the basis for sleep-scheduling energy savings.
- The three challenges form a **deadly feedback loop**: mobility → re-route → control overhead → energy drain → node death → partition → poor scalability.
- **No single technique solves all three**; always describe the trade-off.
- Quantitative metrics to memorize: **PDR, delay, routing overhead, network lifetime, throughput per node**.
- Mobility models to know: **Random Waypoint, Random Direction, RPGM**.
- For KTU derivations, always show the **assumptions, the formula, the substitution, and the interpretation** — never skip a step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** A 14-mark question on this topic almost always asks for *one* derivation + *one* design discussion. Practice writing the Gupta-Kumar derivation and the energy-per-bit derivation until you can produce them in under 8 minutes each. The remaining 6 minutes should be spent on the design trade-off table.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medium Access Control in Adhoc Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT863 - ADHOC AND WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Medium Access Control in Adhoc Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Medium Access Control (MAC)** in Ad Hoc Networks is the sub-layer of the Data Link Layer (Layer 2 of the OSI/TCP-IP model) responsible for regulating the use of the shared wireless transmission medium among multiple, decentralized, and infrastructure-less mobile nodes. Since Ad Hoc networks lack a central coordinator (e.g., no Access Point or Base Station), the MAC protocol must be *distributed*, *collision-aware*, and *energy-efficient*, enabling nodes to arbitrate channel access in a fair, contention-resolved, and topology-adaptive manner.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the MAC layer in ad hoc networks addresses:
* **Distributed channel access** (no central controller).
* **Collision avoidance** in a broadcast shared medium.
* **Hidden and exposed terminal resolution**.
* **Power conservation** for battery-limited mobile nodes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Round-Table Walkie-Talkie"

Imagine **5 friends** sitting around a round table, each holding a single shared walkie-talkie (only **one frequency channel**, just like wireless medium). No one is the "boss" who can assign the mic.

* If two friends press the **Push-To-Talk (PTT)** button simultaneously, **neither message is heard** → this is a **collision**.
* If Friend A (left side) whispers to Friend B (right side), Friend C (opposite side, *not in direct line of sight*) might *not even know* A is transmitting → C presses PTT and collides anyway. This is the classic **Hidden Terminal Problem**.
* Conversely, when A is talking to B, Friend D (who could safely talk to E nearby) wrongly *stays silent* fearing collision → this is the **Exposed Terminal Problem**.

A **MAC protocol** is the "house rule book" (listen-before-talk, handshakes, timers) that ensures orderly walkie-talkie usage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Why MAC is the Heart of Ad Hoc Networks**
> Unlike cellular networks, ad hoc nodes share the medium **without infrastructure**. Therefore, the MAC protocol *is* the foundation that determines:
> - **Throughput** (successfully delivered bits per second).
> - **Fairness** (equal medium access opportunity).
> - **Energy efficiency** (battery lifetime).
> - **Latency** (delay in packet delivery).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Properties of an Ideal Ad Hoc MAC Protocol**
> 1. **Distributed operation** — no central node.
> 2. **Collision avoidance / detection** — minimize simultaneous transmissions.
> 3. **Fairness** — no node monopolizes the channel.
> 4. **Scalability** — must work for tens to thousands of nodes.
> 5. **Power efficiency** — minimize idle listening and overhearing.
> 6. **QoS support** — differentiate real-time vs. best-effort traffic.

### 1.3 The Wireless Medium — A Hostile Environment

A wireless channel is fundamentally different from a wired channel due to three key physical realities:

| Phenomenon | Description | Impact on MAC |
|---|---|---|
| **Broadcast Nature** | Signal propagates omni-directionally; every neighbor hears it. | Causes hidden/exposed terminal issues. |
| **Half-Duplex Operation** | A node cannot transmit and receive simultaneously on the same frequency. | Forces careful timing (SIFS, DIFS). |
| **High Bit Error Rate (BER)** | Wireless channels suffer fading, noise, and interference. | Requires retransmission, ACK mechanisms. |

These realities make the MAC design for ad hoc networks vastly more complex than the simple CSMA/CD used in Ethernet (wired LANs), which is why **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)** is the cornerstone of wireless MAC.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of MAC Protocols in Ad Hoc Networks

A well-organized KTU board answer must first present the taxonomy. MAC protocols for ad hoc networks are broadly classified into three families:

#### A. Contention-Based Protocols (Random Access)
Nodes compete for the channel using carrier sensing and backoff mechanisms.

* **ALOHA & Slotted ALOHA** — simplest, highest collision rate.
* **CSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access)** — listen before transmit.
* **CSMA/CD (Collision Detection)** — used in Ethernet; **not feasible** in wireless (cannot sense while transmitting due to half-duplex).
* **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)** — **backbone of IEEE 802.11**.
* **MACA (Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)** — introduces RTS/CTS.
* **MACAW (MACA for Wireless)** — adds ACK, RRTS, DS, and fair backoff.

#### B. Contention-Free / Scheduled Protocols
Channel access is deterministically assigned.

* **TDMA, FDMA, CDMA** based schemes.
* **Clustering-based protocols** (e.g., clusterhead assigns slots).
* **Token-based protocols** (e.g., IEEE 802.4 logical ring).

#### C. Hybrid Protocols
Combine the best of both worlds.

* **Hybrid TDMA/CSMA** — schedule for real-time, contention for best-effort.
* **IEEE 802.11e HCF** — uses both contention (EDCA) and contention-free (HCCA).

### 2.2 The IEEE 802.11 DCF — The De-Facto Ad Hoc MAC

The **Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)** of IEEE 802.11 is the most widely examined MAC mechanism in KTU. It is a CSMA/CA protocol with two operating modes:

1. **Basic mode** — two-way handshake (DATA + ACK).
2. **RTS/CTS mode** — four-way handshake (RTS + CTS + DATA + ACK).

#### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Phase 1 — Carrier Sensing & DIFS Wait**
* A node wanting to transmit first performs **Physical Carrier Sensing** (CCA — Clear Channel Assessment).
* If the medium is idle, the node waits for a **DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space)** duration before proceeding.
* DIFS = SIFS + (2 × Slot_Time). SIFS = **10 µs** (Short Inter-Frame Space).

**Phase 2 — Backoff Timer (Binary Exponential)**
* The node selects a **random backoff counter** from the current Contention Window (CW):

  $$CW \in [0, W-1]$$
* Initial contention window: $W_0 = CW_{min} = 32$ (in time slots).
* The timer **decrements by 1** for every idle slot.
* If medium becomes busy → timer **freezes** and resumes after the medium is idle again for DIFS.

**Phase 3 — Transmission (Basic or RTS/CTS Mode)**

* **Basic Mode (Two-Way):** Sender transmits DATA → waits SIFS → Receiver replies ACK.
* **RTS/CTS Mode (Four-Way):**
  - Sender transmits **RTS** (Request-To-Send, 20 bytes) → waits SIFS.
  - Receiver replies **CTS** (Clear-To-Send, 14 bytes) → waits SIFS.
  - Sender transmits **DATA** → waits SIFS.
  - Receiver replies **ACK**.

**Phase 4 — Network Allocation Vector (NAV)**
Every frame (RTS, CTS, DATA, ACK) carries a **Duration field** indicating how long the medium will be reserved. All overhearing nodes set their **NAV timer** and defer transmission — a mechanism called **Virtual Carrier Sensing**.

#### Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)
On each unsuccessful transmission (collision/no ACK), the contention window doubles:

$$W_i = 2^{i} \times W_0 \quad \text{(for retries } i = 0, 1, 2, \dots, m)$$

* $W_{max} = 2^m \times W_0 = 1024$ slots (typical).
* After successful transmission, $W$ resets to $W_0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why RTS/CTS Solves Hidden Terminal:**
> The hidden terminal hears either the RTS or the CTS (both contain duration). It then sets its NAV and **stays silent**, preventing collision with the eventual DATA frame at the receiver.

### 2.3 Other Major Ad Hoc MAC Protocols

* **MACA (Karn, 1990)** — Solves hidden terminal using RTS/CTS only. **No carrier sensing** before RTS, **no ACK**, **no backoff freezing**. Pure handshake.
* **MACAW (Bharghavan et al., 1994)** — Improves MACA with:
  - **ACK frame** for reliability.
  - **RRTS (Request-for-RTS)** from receiver to hidden node.
  - **DS (Data-Sending)** multi-frame stream header.
  - **ML (Multiplicative Increase Linear Decrease)** for fair backoff.
* **PAMAS (Power-Aware Multi-Access with Signaling)** — Uses a **second radio/channel for signaling** so the data radio can be powered off during silence → saves **30-50% battery**.
* **S-MAC (Sensor MAC)** — For WSNs; uses **sleep/wake cycles** (duty cycling) to save energy.
* **B-MAC (Berkeley MAC)** — Uses **LPL (Low Power Listening)** and **CCA** for ultra-low power.
* **Z-MAC (Zebra MAC)** — Hybrid; uses TDMA in high contention, CSMA in low contention.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are *guaranteed high-yield* for KTU board examinations. Memorize units and typical IEEE 802.11 constants.

| # | Formula / Constant | Expression | Typical Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DIFS Duration** | $DIFS = SIFS + 2 \times \sigma$ | **34 µs** (for 802.11b) | Wait time before contention |
| 2 | **SIFS Duration** | Standard constant | **10 µs** | Short wait for response frames |
| 3 | **Slot Time ($\sigma$)** | Standard constant | **20 µs** (for DSSS 802.11) | Unit of backoff time |
| 4 | **Backoff Counter** | $BO = Random(0, CW)$ | Integer slots | Initial deferral time |
| 5 | **Contention Window Growth** | $W_i = \min(2^{i} \times W_0, \ CW_{max})$ | $32 \rightarrow 1024$ | BEB on collision |
| 6 | **Initial CW** | $W_0 = CW_{min}$ | **32 slots** | Starting backoff range |
| 7 | **Max CW** | $CW_{max}$ | **1024 slots** | Upper backoff cap |
| 8 | **NAV Value** | Set from Duration field of RTS/CTS | Time in µs | Virtual carrier sense timer |
| 9 | **Total Reservation Time (4-way)** | $T_{res} = T_{RTS} + T_{CTS} + T_{DATA} + T_{ACK} + 3 \cdot SIFS$ | — | Channel busy period |
| 10 | **Throughput Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{DATA}}{L_{DATA} + L_{OH} + T_{backoff}}$ | $< 1$ | Useful data / total time |
| 11 | **Duty Cycle (WSN MAC)** | $DC = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}$ | 1-10% typical | Energy savings ratio |
| 12 | **Max Retransmit Count** | Standard limit | **7** (short) / **4** (long) | After this → drop frame |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The above formulas drive the design of every Wi-Fi router, ZigBee node, Bluetooth device, and 6LoWPAN sensor on Earth. Industrial applications include vehicular ad hoc networks (VANETs), military MANETs, smart grids, and IoT deployments.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi (Ad Hoc / Mesh Mode)** — uses DCF as described.
* **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)** — use S-MAC, B-MAC for battery life.
* **Vehicular Ad Hoc Networks (VANETs)** — IEEE 802.11p (WAVE) is a modified 802.11 DCF.
* **Underwater Acoustic Networks** — MAC tailored for long propagation delays.
* **Satellite Ad Hoc Networks** — TDMA-based MAC due to long latencies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Channel Reservation Time for a 4-Way RTS/CTS Handshake

**Given Problem (Typical KTU):** Compute the total time required for a successful data transmission using the IEEE 802.11 DCF RTS/CTS mechanism, given:

* DATA frame length = $L$ bits
* PHY header = $H_{phy}$ = 192 bits (preamble + header)
* MAC header = $H_{mac}$ = 272 bits
* ACK = 112 bits + PHY header
* RTS = 160 bits + PHY header
* CTS = 112 bits + PHY header
* Channel bit rate = $R$ bits/sec
* SIFS = 10 µs, DIFS = 34 µs, Slot = 20 µs

**Step 1 — Compute RTS Transmission Time**

The RTS frame duration includes both PHY and MAC overhead:

$$T_{RTS} = \frac{H_{phy} + 160}{R} = \frac{192 + 160}{R} = \frac{352}{R} \ \text{seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Compute CTS Transmission Time**

$$T_{CTS} = \frac{H_{phy} + 112}{R} = \frac{192 + 112}{R} = \frac{304}{R} \ \text{seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Compute DATA Transmission Time**

$$T_{DATA} = \frac{H_{phy} + H_{mac} + L}{R} = \frac{192 + 272 + L}{R} = \frac{464 + L}{R} \ \text{seconds}$$

**Step 4 — Compute ACK Transmission Time**

$$T_{ACK} = \frac{H_{phy} + 112}{R} = \frac{192 + 112}{R} = \frac{304}{R} \ \text{seconds}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Propagation Delay**

Let $T_{prop}$ denote one-way propagation. For a 4-way handshake, propagation occurs 4 times (RTS, CTS, DATA, ACK) — each directionally:

$$T_{prop,total} = 4 \times T_{prop}$$

**Step 6 — Sum the Total Channel Reservation Time**

The sender first waits DIFS, then transmits RTS → SIFS gap → CTS → SIFS gap → DATA → SIFS gap → ACK. After the last bit of ACK, the medium is reserved. Total:

$$T_{total} = T_{DIFS} + T_{RTS} + T_{prop} + T_{SIFS} + T_{CTS} + T_{prop} + T_{SIFS} + T_{DATA} + T_{prop} + T_{SIFS} + T_{ACK} + T_{prop}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute and Simplify**

Substituting $T_{DIFS} = T_{SIFS} + 2\sigma$:

$$T_{total} = (T_{SIFS} + 2\sigma) + T_{RTS} + T_{CTS} + T_{DATA} + T_{ACK} + 3 T_{SIFS} + 4 T_{prop}$$

$$T_{total} = 2\sigma + T_{RTS} + T_{CTS} + T_{DATA} + T_{ACK} + 4 T_{SIFS} + 4 T_{prop}$$

**Step 8 — Final Substituted Form**

$$\boxed{T_{total} = 2\sigma + 4 T_{SIFS} + 4 T_{prop} + \frac{(352 + 304 + 304 + 464 + L)}{R}}$$

$$\boxed{T_{total} = 2\sigma + 4 T_{SIFS} + 4 T_{prop} + \frac{1424 + L}{R}}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Protocol Comparison (High-Yield KTU Table)

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **board-exam favorite**. Drawing a similar table in your answer earns full marks on comparison questions.

| Feature | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA | CSMA | CSMA/CA (802.11 DCF) | MACA | MACAW | PAMAS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Carrier Sensing** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes + Virtual (NAV) | ❌ No (only RTS/CTS) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (data ch.) |
| **Collision Detection** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ (wired only) | ❌ (avoided) | ❌ Avoided | ❌ Avoided | ❌ Avoided |
| **RTS/CTS Handshake** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Optional | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Acknowledgment (ACK)** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Hidden Terminal Solution** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (via NAV) | ✅ (partial) | ✅ (better) | ✅ Yes |
| **Exposed Terminal Solution** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ (still exists) | ✅ (partial) | ✅ (partial) | ✅ Yes |
| **Power Saving** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ Basic | ❌ | ❌ | ✅✅ Excellent |
| **Backoff Mechanism** | ❌ | ❌ | Non-persistent | BEB (Binary Exp.) | None | MILD (Multiplicative Inc. Linear Dec.) | BEB-like |
| **Suitable for** | Reference only | Low load | Low load | Wi-Fi, general MANETs | Basic MANET | Improved MANET | Energy-constrained MANETs |

### 3.3 Derivation: Saturation Throughput of CSMA/CA (Simplified Bianchi Model — KTU Form)

Bianchi's model assumes each node always has a packet to transmit. Let:

* $n$ = number of contending nodes
* $\tau$ = probability a node transmits in a random slot
* $p$ = conditional collision probability
* $W$ = minimum contention window
* $m$ = maximum backoff stage

**Step 1 — Transmission Probability in Steady State**

A node transmits in a slot with probability:

$$\tau = \frac{2(1 - 2p)}{(1 - 2p)(W + 1) + pW(1 - (2p)^m)}$$

**Step 2 — Successful Transmission Probability on the Channel**

A transmission is successful if exactly one node transmits:

$$P_s = n \tau (1 - \tau)^{n-1}$$

**Step 3 — Saturation Throughput**

Let $E[P]$ = average payload size, $T_s$ = time for success, $T_c$ = time for collision, $\sigma$ = slot time:

$$S = \frac{P_s \cdot E[P]}{(1 - P_{tr}) \sigma + P_{tr} P_s T_s + P_{tr}(1 - P_s) T_c}$$

where $P_{tr} = 1 - (1 - \tau)^n$ is the probability that *at least one* node transmits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exam:** As $n$ increases, $S$ initially rises, peaks, then **falls sharply** due to increased collisions — this is the **scalability bottleneck** of contention-based MAC.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — MAC Protocol Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[MAC Protocols for Ad Hoc Networks]
    ROOT --> A[Contention Based]
    ROOT --> B[Contention Free Scheduled]
    ROOT --> C[Hybrid]

    A --> A1[Pure ALOHA]
    A --> A2[Slotted ALOHA]
    A --> A3[CSMA CA]
    A --> A4[MACA]
    A --> A5[MACAW]

    A3 --> A3A[Basic Mode DATA ACK]
    A3 --> A3B[RTS CTS Mode]

    A5 --> A5A[ACK Reliability]
    A5 --> A5B[RRTS for Hidden]
    A5 --> A5C[MILD Fairness]

    B --> B1[TDMA]
    B --> B2[FDMA]
    B --> B3[CDMA]
    B --> B4[Token Ring]

    C --> C1[Z MAC]
    C --> C2[IEEE 80211e HCF]
    C --> C3[Cluster TDMA CSMA]

    classDef rootStyle fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef catStyle fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef leafStyle fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    class ROOT rootStyle
    class A,B,C catStyle
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,B1,B2,B3,B4,C1,C2,C3,A3A,A3B,A5A,A5B,A5C leafStyle
```

### 4.2 Figure 2 — IEEE 802.11 DCF RTS/CTS Handshake Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant NodeA as Sender Node A
    participant NodeB as Receiver Node B
    participant NodeC as Hidden Node C
    participant NodeD as Exposed Node D

    Note over NodeA,NodeD: All nodes share the same wireless medium
    NodeA->>NodeA: Sense medium is idle for DIFS duration
    NodeA->>NodeA: Select random backoff from CW and count down

    Note over NodeA,NodeB: RTS CTS Handshake begins
    NodeA->>NodeB: RTS frame with Duration field
    NodeB->>NodeA: CTS frame with Duration field after SIFS

    Note over NodeC: Node C overhears RTS sets NAV and defers
    Note over NodeD: Node D overhears RTS but sets short NAV
    NodeD-->>NodeD: Can transmit to another node after CTS duration

    NodeA->>NodeB: DATA frame after SIFS
    NodeB->>NodeA: ACK frame after SIFS

    Note over NodeA,NodeD: All NAV timers expire channel becomes free
```

### 4.3 Figure 3 — Hidden Terminal vs. Exposed Terminal Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    SCEN[Wireless Medium Scenarios]
    SCEN --> HT[Hidden Terminal Problem]
    SCEN --> ET[Exposed Terminal Problem]

    HT --> HTD1[Node A transmits to Node B]
    HT --> HTD2[Node C cannot sense A transmission]
    HT --> HTD3[Node C transmits and collides at B]
    HT --> HTS[Solution RTS CTS via B hears CTS]

    ET --> ETD1[Node B transmits to Node A]
    ET --> ETD2[Node C senses busy and defers]
    ET --> ETD3[Node C could safely transmit to Node D]
    ET --> ETS[Solution Virtual carrier sensing with NAV tuning]

    classDef problemStyle fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef detailStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef solutionStyle fill:#00b050,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class SCEN,HT,ET problemStyle
    class HTD1,HTD2,HTD3,ETD1,ETD2,ETD3 detailStyle
    class HTS,ETS solutionStyle
```

### 4.4 Figure 4 — CSMA/CA State Transition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST0[Node in Idle State] --> ST1[New Packet Arrives]
    ST1 --> ST2{Is Medium Idle for DIFS}
    ST2 -->|No| ST3[Defer and start backoff after medium idle]
    ST2 -->|Yes| ST4[Select random BO from CW]
    ST4 --> ST5{Is BO Timer Zero}
    ST5 -->|No| ST6[Decrement BO per idle slot]
    ST6 --> ST5
    ST5 -->|Yes| ST7[Transmit RTS or DATA]
    ST7 --> ST8{CTS or ACK Received}
    ST8 -->|Yes| ST9[Success Reset CW to W0]
    ST8 -->|No| ST10[Collision or Loss Detected]
    ST10 --> ST11[Doubling CW up to CWmax]
    ST11 --> ST12{Retry Count Exceeded}
    ST12 -->|No| ST4
    ST12 -->|Yes| ST13[Packet Dropped and Reported to Upper Layer]

    classDef stateStyle fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef errorStyle fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef successStyle fill:#00b050,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class ST0,ST1,ST3,ST4,ST6,ST7,ST9 stateStyle
    class ST2,ST5,ST8,ST12 decisionStyle
    class ST10,ST13 errorStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure Interpretation Note:** Each Mermaid block follows the **KTU Board Diagram Standard** — left-to-right or top-to-bottom flow, clearly labeled states, and color-coded decision/success/failure paths.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Explain the Hidden Terminal and Exposed Terminal problems in ad hoc networks with a suitable diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Hidden Terminal Problem:** In a wireless ad hoc network, Node A is transmitting to Node B, but Node C (which is *out of radio range* of A) cannot sense A's transmission. If C initiates a transmission to B, the frames **collide at receiver B**, causing data loss. This scenario is called the **hidden terminal problem** because C is "hidden" from A's transmission.

**Exposed Terminal Problem:** When Node B is transmitting to Node A, Node C (within range of B) defers its own transmission to avoid collision, even though C could **safely transmit to Node D** without causing any interference. This unnecessary deferral wastes channel capacity.

**Solution:** The **RTS/CTS handshake** (used in IEEE 802.11 DCF and MACA/MACAW) solves both problems through the **Network Allocation Vector (NAV)** — every overhearing node sets a virtual carrier-sense timer and defers its transmission for the duration indicated in the RTS/CTS frames.

**Diagram:** Draw two pairs of nodes — Hidden: `A -- B` and `C -- B` where A and C are out of range; Exposed: `C -- B` and `C -- D` where B and D are out of range. *(1 mark for problem statements + 1 mark for solution + 1 mark for diagram)*.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**What is CSMA/CA? How is it different from CSMA/CD?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)** is a MAC protocol used in wireless networks where a node *listens* to the medium before transmitting, and if the medium is busy, it *waits* for a randomly computed backoff time before retrying — thus **avoiding** rather than detecting collisions.

**Differences from CSMA/CD:**

| Aspect | CSMA/CD | CSMA/CA |
|---|---|---|
| **Medium** | Wired (Ethernet) | Wireless (Wi-Fi, Ad Hoc) |
| **Collision** | Detected *after* it occurs | Avoided *before* it occurs |
| **Sensing While Transmitting** | Possible (full-duplex capable) | Not possible (half-duplex radio) |
| **Mechanism** | Voltage anomaly detection | Energy detection + NAV + RTS/CTS |
| **Performance** | Efficient in low load, low latency | Slightly lower throughput due to overhead |

**Conclusion:** CSMA/CD is unsuitable for wireless ad hoc networks because wireless nodes cannot transmit and sense the channel simultaneously. Hence, **CSMA/CA** is universally adopted in IEEE 802.11-based ad hoc networks. *(1.5 marks CSMA/CA + 1 mark comparison + 0.5 mark conclusion)*.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Module contains a 14-mark question with internal choice (Attempt any ONE of Q(A) or Q(B)). Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the working of the **IEEE 802.11 Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)** in detail. With a neat timing diagram, explain the **RTS/CTS mechanism** and the role of the **Network Allocation Vector (NAV)**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** algorithm used in DCF. A station has retransmission count $i = 3$ and minimum contention window $W_0 = 32$ slots. Calculate the current contention window size. If transmission still fails, what is the value of $W$ after the next retry?

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Working of IEEE 802.11 DCF (7 Marks)

**1. Overview (1 mark):**
The **Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)** is the fundamental MAC technique of IEEE 802.11, used in both infrastructure (BSS) and ad hoc (IBSS) modes. It is a **CSMA/CA-based** protocol employing both **physical carrier sensing** (CCA) and **virtual carrier sensing** (NAV) to avoid collisions.

**2. Two Operating Modes (1 mark):**
* **Basic Access Mode:** Two-way handshake (DATA + ACK).
* **RTS/CTS Mode:** Four-way handshake (RTS + CTS + DATA + ACK) — used for long frames to mitigate hidden terminal.

**3. Step-by-Step Operation (3 marks):**
* **Step 1 — DIFS Wait:** A node with a frame to send first senses the channel. If idle for **DIFS** = 34 µs, it proceeds.
* **Step 2 — Backoff:** The node selects a random integer in $[0, CW-1]$ and starts decrementing the counter per idle slot. If the channel becomes busy, the counter **freezes**.
* **Step 3 — RTS Transmission:** When the counter reaches 0, the node sends an **RTS** (20 bytes).
* **Step 4 — CTS Reply:** After **SIFS** = 10 µs, the receiver replies with **CTS** (14 bytes).
* **Step 5 — DATA Transmission:** Sender waits SIFS, then transmits DATA.
* **Step 6 — ACK:** Receiver waits SIFS, sends ACK confirming receipt.

**4. NAV Mechanism (2 marks):**
Every 802.11 frame (RTS, CTS, DATA, ACK) contains a **Duration field** specifying the time (in µs) the medium will be reserved. All overhearing nodes extract this value and set their **NAV timer** — during this time, they treat the medium as busy even if their physical CCA says otherwise. This is **virtual carrier sensing** and is the key mechanism for solving the hidden terminal problem.

**5. Timing Diagram (in text form for board exam):**

```
[A] ----DIFS----[BO]-->[RTS]---SIFS--->[CTS]---SIFS--->[DATA]---SIFS--->[ACK]
                          |              |               |
[B]                     (waits SIFS)  (NAV set)       (waits SIFS)
[C] (overhears RTS)   [NAV set for entire duration]
[D] (overhears CTS)   [NAV set for DATA+ACK]
```

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Binary Exponential Backoff (7 Marks)

**1. Concept (2 marks):**
**BEB** is a collision-resolution algorithm where the contention window **doubles** after every failed transmission attempt, up to a maximum. This widens the backoff range, statistically reducing the probability of two nodes selecting the same backoff counter and re-colliding.

**2. Mathematical Formulation (2 marks):**
$$W_i = \min(2^i \cdot W_0, \ CW_{max})$$
where:
* $i$ = retransmission attempt count (starts at 0)
* $W_0 = CW_{min} = 32$ slots
* $CW_{max} = 1024$ slots
* $m$ = maximum backoff stage = 5

**3. Calculation (2 marks):**

Given $i = 3$, $W_0 = 32$:

$$W_3 = 2^3 \times 32 = 8 \times 32 = 256 \ \text{slots}$$

After next retry (i.e., $i = 4$):

$$W_4 = 2^4 \times 32 = 16 \times 32 = 512 \ \text{slots}$$

**4. Numerical Range of Backoff Counter (1 mark):**
* At $i = 3$: BO counter $\in [0, 255]$.
* At $i = 4$: BO counter $\in [0, 511]$.

**Conclusion:** After successful transmission, $W$ resets to $W_0 = 32$.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the following MAC protocols for ad hoc networks: (i) **Pure ALOHA**, (ii) **CSMA**, (iii) **MACA**, and (iv) **MACAW**. State at least three distinguishing features for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **PAMAS (Power-Aware Multi-Access with Signaling)** protocol. How does it achieve energy saving in ad hoc networks? Compare it briefly with the **S-MAC** protocol used in Wireless Sensor Networks.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Comparison of MAC Protocols (7 Marks)

**1. Pure ALOHA (1.5 marks):**
* A node transmits **as soon as** it has a frame — no carrier sensing.
* Maximum theoretical throughput: **18.4%** ($S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$, max at $G = 0.5$).
* Suffered from very high collision probability; primarily a reference baseline.

**2. CSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access) (1.5 marks):**
* A node **listens** to the medium before transmitting.
* **Variants:** 1-persistent, non-persistent, p-persistent.
* Performance: much better than ALOHA under low load, degrades under high load.
* **Limitation:** cannot detect collisions while transmitting (half-duplex) → needs CSMA/CA in wireless.

**3. MACA (Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) (2 marks):**
* Introduces **RTS/CTS handshake** to solve hidden terminal problem.
* **No carrier sensing** before transmitting RTS — relies purely on handshake.
* **No ACK** frame — unreliable (lost frames not recovered at MAC layer).
* **No backoff freezing** — even when channel becomes busy mid-backoff, the timer continues.

**4. MACAW (MACA for Wireless) (2 marks):**
* Improvement over MACA, addressing its weaknesses.
* **Adds ACK** for reliability.
* **Adds RRTS (Request-for-RTS)** to handle the case where hidden terminal hears only CTS, not RTS.
* **Uses DS (Data-Sending) header** to inform neighbors of multi-frame sessions.
* **MILD (Multiplicative Increase, Linear Decrease)** backoff — increases by 1.5× on collision, decreases by 1 on success → better fairness than BEB.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — PAMAS Protocol (7 Marks)

**1. PAMAS Overview (2 marks):**
**PAMAS (Power-Aware Multi-Access with Signaling)** is a MAC protocol proposed by Singh and Raghavendra (1998) specifically for energy-constrained ad hoc networks. Its key innovation is the use of a **separate signaling channel** to control the data channel.

**2. Working Principle (2 marks):**
* The node has **two radios**: one for data, one for control signaling.
* When a node is **not transmitting or receiving** data, but is forced to remain silent (because of a neighbor's transmission indicated by RTS/CTS on the signaling channel), it **powers off its data radio**.
* The signaling radio remains active only to detect when the silence period ends.
* When the signaling channel indicates the medium is free, the data radio is powered back on.

**3. Energy Savings Achieved (1.5 marks):**
PAMAS saves energy by avoiding:
* **Overhearing** — turning off the radio when overhearing others' transmissions (which doesn't help the node).
* **Idle listening** — turning off the radio when there is nothing to send or receive.
* **Collisions** — avoiding the energy wasted in transmitting frames that will collide.
* Reported savings: **30% to 50%** of battery power compared to MACA.

**4. Comparison with S-MAC (1.5 marks):**

| Feature | PAMAS | S-MAC |
|---|---|---|
| **Target Network** | General Ad Hoc (MANET) | Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) |
| **Energy Saving Method** | Two-radio, power off data radio on silence | Sleep/wake duty cycling on single radio |
| **Synchronization** | Not required | Required (SYNC frames) |
| **Hardware Cost** | High (two radios) | Low (single radio) |
| **Overhearing Solution** | Excellent (radio off) | Partial (sleep when not involved) |
| **Latency** | Low | High (multi-hop sleep delay) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing CSMA/CA with CSMA/CD** — A single-line error here costs **2 marks** instantly. Always state *Collision Avoidance* vs. *Collision Detection*.
> 2. **Missing NAV explanation** — Many students describe RTS/CTS but forget to mention the **Duration field** and **virtual carrier sensing** — this loses **2–3 marks** in DCF questions.
> 3. **No units in formulas** — Writing DIFS = 34 without "µs" loses 0.5 mark; examiners are strict about units.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** — A question asking for "neat diagram" without one → **deduction of 1–2 marks**. Always draw **even a rough sketch**.
> 5. **Wrong backoff formula** — Writing $W_i = 2^{i+1} \times W_0$ instead of $2^i \times W_0$ is a **fatal 2-mark error**. Remember: $i$ starts at 0, not 1.
> 6. **Conflating ALOHA throughputs** — Pure ALOHA max = 18.4%, Slotted ALOHA max = 36.8%. Mixing them up = lost mark.
> 7. **Not stating the problem-solution pair** in hidden/exposed questions — Examiner expects **problem + cause + solution** in three crisp points.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: MAC in Ad Hoc Networks**

**🔑 Core Concepts to Memorize**
* **MAC** = Layer 2 sublayer managing shared wireless medium.
* **CSMA/CA** is the *de facto* wireless MAC; CSMA/CD is *wired only*.
* **Hidden Terminal** = out-of-range sender collides at receiver → solved by **RTS/CTS + NAV**.
* **Exposed Terminal** = unnecessarily defers → partially solved by virtual carrier sensing.
* **NAV** = Virtual Carrier Sense timer set from RTS/CTS Duration field.
* **DIFS = SIFS + 2σ**; SIFS = **10 µs**; σ (slot) = **20 µs** (IEEE 802.11b DSSS).
* **BEB Formula:** $W_i = \min(2^i W_0, CW_{max})$, $W_0 = 32$, $CW_{max} = 1024$.

**🔑 Protocols to Know (Minimum for KTU)**
* **Pure/Slotted ALOHA** — baseline, no sensing.
* **CSMA, CSMA/CA, CSMA/CD** — sensing-based.
* **MACA** — first to use RTS/CTS.
* **MACAW** — adds ACK, RRTS, DS, MILD.
* **PAMAS** — two-radio energy saving.
* **S-MAC, B-MAC, Z-MAC** — WSN-targeted protocols.
* **IEEE 802.11 DCF** — industry standard.
* **IEEE 802.11e HCF** — QoS-enhanced hybrid.

**🔑 Numerical Constants to Commit**
* SIFS = **10 µs**, DIFS = **34 µs**, Slot = **20 µs**
* $W_0$ = **32 slots**, $CW_{max}$ = **1024 slots**
* ALOHA max throughput = **18.4%** (pure), **36.8%** (slotted)

**🔑 Common KTU Question Patterns**
* "Explain hidden/exposed terminal with diagram" → **3 marks** (definition + diagram + solution).
* "Compare CSMA/CA vs CSMA/CD" → **3-7 marks** (table + explanation).
* "Describe DCF / RTS-CTS" → **7-14 marks** (steps + NAV + timing diagram).
* "BEB calculation" → **3-7 marks** (formula + substitution).
* "Power-aware MAC protocols" → **7 marks** (PAMAS, S-MAC, B-MAC).
* "Pure ALOHA throughput derivation" → **7 marks** ($S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$, max at $G = 0.5$).

**🔑 Last-Minute Mnemonics**
* **"R-C-D-A"** = **R**TS → **C**TS → **D**ATA → **A**CK (4-way handshake).
* **"D-S-S"** = **D**IFS > **S**IFS > Slot — wait times in decreasing order.
* **"MACAW has 4 extras"** = ACK, RRTS, DS, MILD.

**🔑 Diagram Essentials to Draw Quickly**
* **RTS/CTS timing diagram** — vertical lines for A, B, C nodes showing DIFS, SIFS, RTS, CTS, DATA, ACK.
* **Hidden terminal** — 4 nodes in a rhombus: A-B and C-B with A-C out of range.
* **Exposed terminal** — 4 nodes linear: A — B — C — D where B-C and C-D are in range but A-D are not.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies and Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT863 - ADHOC AND WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Studies and Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Case Study** in the context of Ad-hoc and Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) is a structured, in-depth empirical investigation of a real-world deployment that documents the complete lifecycle of the network — from problem statement and design constraints, through hardware selection and protocol stack, to field deployment, operational challenges, and performance evaluation. In KTU 2024 parlance, a case study must demonstrate the application of the **fundamental principles of WSNs** (energy efficiency, self-organization, multi-hop communication, data-centric routing, and constrained resources) to a specific application domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Case studies are *not* theoretical exercises. They are expected to be presented with a **Problem → Design → Implementation → Evaluation** narrative. Examiner credit is awarded for explicitly linking each design decision to a fundamental WSN principle (e.g., "Duty cycling was chosen because of the energy-constrained nature of motes").

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a medical student learning surgery. You can read all the textbooks about how to perform an appendectomy, but until you study **a real surgery case** — with the patient profile, the incision choice, the complications faced, and the recovery observed — you do not truly *understand* the procedure. **Case studies in WSNs serve the exact same purpose.**

A textbook tells you *how* LEACH clustering works. A case study of the **Great Duck Island habitat monitoring deployment** tells you *why* a 3-level hierarchy of micamotes, gateway motes, and a base station was needed, *how* the Berkeley motes were programmed with TinyOS, and *what* real environmental challenges (rain, wind, bird interference) broke the link assumptions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Definition:** A WSN case study is a "post-mortem with a purpose" — it is a published, peer-reviewed account of how someone actually built, deployed, and ran a sensor network in the real world, and what the engineering community learned from it.

## 1.3 Why KTU Includes This Topic

The KTU 2024 Scheme includes "Case Studies and Examples" in Module 1 because:

1. **Industry Readiness:** KTU's NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) mandate requires students to appreciate *real engineering*, not just algorithms.
2. **Cross-Course Linkage:** Case studies tie together concepts from Module 1 (architecture, applications, challenges) and prepare the student for later modules on routing, MAC, and security.
3. **Examination Relevance:** KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) frequently ask 14-mark descriptive questions on classic case studies (e.g., ZebraNet, Great Duck Island, SensIT).

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Bolded Standard Metrics)

- **Mote:** A single sensor node (also called a "smart dust" node). Standard reference: **Berkeley/Crossbow MICAz** and **TelosB**.
- **Base Station (BS):** The data sink, typically with **unlimited power, storage, and computation**.
- **Energy Budget:** Typically a mote has **~2 AA batteries** yielding approximately **2,850 mAh @ 3V**, i.e., roughly **30 kJ** total.
- **Network Lifetime:** Usually defined as the time until the **first mote dies (FND)** or the **last mote dies (LND)**.
- **Duty Cycle:** The fraction of time a node's radio is active. Typical WSNs run at **1–5 % duty cycle**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of WSN Case Studies by Application Domain

KTU 2024 Module 1 categorizes case studies along two axes: **Domain** (application area) and **Architecture** (deployment topology).

### 2.1.1 By Application Domain

- **Environmental Monitoring:** Great Duck Island (seabird habitat), Zebranet (wildlife tracking), LOFAR-agro (precision agriculture), Volcano monitoring (Pinatubo / Reventador).
- **Military / Defense:** SensIT program (DARPA), Counter-Sniper systems (Vanderbilt University), battlefield surveillance.
- **Healthcare / Medical:** CodeBlue (Harvard), wearable Body Area Networks (BANs), patient monitoring in hospitals.
- **Structural / Civil Engineering:** Golden Gate Bridge structural monitoring, smart buildings, dam and pipeline monitoring.
- **Industrial / Process Control:** Monitoring of rotating machinery, oil refineries, smart grid AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure).
- **Underwater / Harsh Environments:** Underwater acoustic sensor networks, glacial monitoring.
- **Vehicular / Mobile:** VANETs for traffic safety, fleet management, disaster recovery MANETs.

### 2.1.2 By Architectural Topology

- **Flat / Homogeneous:** All nodes identical (e.g., early MICA deployments).
- **Hierarchical / Clustered:** Two-tier (e.g., LEACH) or three-tier (e.g., Great Duck Island).
- **Heterogeneous:** Mix of motes, gateways, base stations, and powerful relays.
- **Mobile:** Nodes move (e.g., Zebranet, VANETs).

## 2.2 The Universal "Five-Question" Template for Analysing a WSN Case Study

Every KTU 2024 examiner expects a case study answer to address these five questions:

1. **WHO is the user / What is the problem?** (Application context)
2. **WHAT is sensed, at WHAT rate, and with WHAT precision?** (Sampling parameters)
3. **HOW is data routed and aggregated?** (Network protocol — typically TinyOS + multi-hop + clustering)
4. **WHAT are the energy and lifetime constraints?** (Power budget, duty cycle, solar harvesting?)
5. **WHAT were the lessons learned?** (Failure modes, scalability, calibration drift, etc.)

## 2.3 The "Why and How" Behind Each Design Pillar

### 2.3.1 Energy Efficiency
- **Why?** Motes are battery-powered and often inaccessible (e.g., on a volcano or inside a wild animal). Replacing batteries is expensive or impossible.
- **How?** The case study must describe **duty cycling**, **in-network aggregation**, **data compression**, and **energy-aware routing**.

### 2.3.2 Self-Organization
- **Why?** Motes are deployed in large numbers (often by aircraft over hostile terrain). Manual configuration of thousands of nodes is infeasible.
- **How?** The case study should reference **neighbor discovery**, **cluster-head election** (LEACH-like), and **localized algorithms** (e.g., GAF, SPAN, geographic routing).

### 2.3.3 Multi-Hop Communication
- **Why?** Radio transmission power scales as $d^{\alpha}$ where $\alpha \ge 2$. Long-range direct transmission wastes energy.
- **How?** Short hops from neighbor to neighbor; data funnels toward a sink.

### 2.3.4 Data-Centricity
- **Why?** Traditional IP addressing is meaningless when queries are "any node that sensed temperature > 30 °C in region R".
- **How?** **Attribute-based naming** (e.g., Directed Diffusion, SPIN), in-network aggregation, and content-based addressing.

### 2.3.5 Constrained Resources
- **Why?** Motes have 8–16-bit CPUs, **4–10 KB RAM**, and tiny flash. TCP/IP is overkill.
- **How?** Lightweight protocols (TinyOS / nesC), custom MAC (S-MAC, B-MAC, T-MAC), and embedded OS.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **mathematical backbone** of any quantitative case-study analysis you will be asked to perform in the ESE. Memorize the **form** of each, the **boundary conditions**, and the **units**.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Boundary Condition / Typical Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **First-Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman)** | $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{\,\alpha}$ | $\alpha = 2$ (free space) or $4$ (multipath); $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit; $\epsilon_{amp} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² or $0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ | Estimate energy to transmit $k$ bits over distance $d$ |
| 2 | **Receive Energy** | $E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit | Energy to receive $k$ bits |
| 3 | **Network Lifetime (FND)** | $T_{FND} = \min_i \left\{ t \;\vert\; E_i(t) = 0 \right\}$ | Earliest death of any node | Used to compare protocol designs |
| 4 | **Duty Cycle** | $\delta = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}$ | Typical WSN: $\delta \in [0.01, 0.05]$ | Radio sleep scheduling |
| 5 | **Energy per Useful Bit (EPUB)** | $EPUB = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i}{N_{useful}}$ | $N_{useful}$ = bits reaching sink | Aggregate energy efficiency metric |
| 6 | **Coverage (Boolean Sensing)** | $C(x,y) = 1 \;\text{if}\; d((x,y), s) \le r_s \;\text{else}\; 0$ | $r_s$ = sensing radius | Field coverage by a node $s$ |
| 7 | **Network Coverage** | $C_{net} = \dfrac{\vert A_{covered} \vert}{\vert A_{total} \vert}$ | $0 \le C_{net} \le 1$ | Fraction of area covered |
| 8 | **Multi-hop Energy Gain** | $\dfrac{E_{TX}^{direct}}{E_{TX}^{multi-hop}} = \dfrac{d^{4}}{4 \cdot (d/2)^{4}} = 4$ (for $\alpha=4$) | For $d > d_{cross-over}$ | Justifies multi-hop over direct |
| 9 | **Cross-over Distance** | $d_{cross} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}$ | Usually $d_{cross} \approx 87$ m | Threshold to choose FS vs MP model |
| 10 | **Data Aggregation Gain** | $G_{agg} = \dfrac{N_{raw}}{N_{aggregated}}$ | $G_{agg} \ge 1$ | Number of raw readings collapsed per output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Notice:** All vertical bars in this table (e.g., $\vert A_{covered} \vert$) use `\vert` in LaTeX so that the markdown table is **not** broken. This is the **KTU 2024 formatting standard** for clean rendering.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The case-study analysis framework is not academic fluff. Industry uses it directly:

- **IoT startups** build reference architectures (e.g., AWS IoT Greengrass) by distilling patterns from earlier WSN deployments.
- **Smart city planners** cite Great Duck Island and Smart Santander when justifying deployment budgets.
- **Disaster-management agencies** reference the post-tsunami and post-hurricane MANET case studies to spec their emergency communication kits.
- **Healthcare IoT companies** use CodeBlue as the intellectual foundation of their wearable platforms.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Multi-Hop vs Direct Transmission Energy Trade-off

This is the **single most common quantitative question** in KTU ESE. The examiner will ask: *"In a case study, a node is at distance $d$ from the base station. Justify the use of multi-hop transmission using the first-order radio model."*

### 3.1.1 Given / Assumed

- $k$ bits to be transmitted to the base station.
- Distance from source to base station $= d$ meters.
- Energy per bit for electronics $= E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit.
- Amplifier energy (multipath) $= \epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ (i.e., $1.3 \times 10^{-15}$ J/bit/m⁴).
- Path loss exponent $\alpha = 4$ (multipath, dense urban / indoor).
- Multi-hop path is $n = 2$ equal hops, each of length $d/2$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Direct Transmission Energy.**

A single transmission of $k$ bits over distance $d$ with $\alpha=4$:

$$E_{direct} = E_{TX}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4}$$

**Step 2: Multi-Hop Transmission Energy (n = 2 equal hops).**

Each hop is of length $d/2$. The source transmits $k$ bits over $d/2$, and the intermediate node receives $k$ bits and re-transmits $k$ bits over the next $d/2$.

$$E_{hop1}^{TX} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{4}$$

$$E_{hop2}^{RX} = E_{elec} \cdot k$$

$$E_{hop2}^{TX} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{4}$$

**Step 3: Total Multi-Hop Energy.**

$$E_{multi} = E_{hop1}^{TX} + E_{hop2}^{RX} + E_{hop2}^{TX}$$

$$E_{multi} = \left[E_{elec} k + \epsilon_{mp} k \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{4}\right] + E_{elec} k + \left[E_{elec} k + \epsilon_{mp} k \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{4}\right]$$

$$E_{multi} = 3 E_{elec} k + 2 \epsilon_{mp} k \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{4}$$

**Step 4: Expand the multi-hop amplifier term.**

$$2 \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{4} = 2 \cdot \frac{d^{4}}{16} = \frac{d^{4}}{8}$$

$$E_{multi} = 3 E_{elec} k + \epsilon_{mp} k \cdot \frac{d^{4}}{8}$$

**Step 5: Take the ratio $\dfrac{E_{direct}}{E_{multi}}$ ignoring the $E_{elec}$ terms for the long-range regime.**

For large $d$, the amplifier term dominates. Then:

$$\frac{E_{direct}}{E_{multi}} \approx \frac{\epsilon_{mp} k d^{4}}{\epsilon_{mp} k d^{4}/8} = 8$$

Wait — recall the canonical textbook result states a gain of **4** for $\alpha = 4$ with $n=2$ hops. The factor of 2 difference comes from the $E_{elec}$ term in the multi-hop case. Let us redo the ratio more carefully:

**Step 6: Exact ratio.**

$$\frac{E_{direct}}{E_{multi}} = \frac{E_{elec} k + \epsilon_{mp} k d^{4}}{3 E_{elec} k + \epsilon_{mp} k d^{4}/8} = \frac{E_{elec} + \epsilon_{mp} d^{4}}{3 E_{elec} + \epsilon_{mp} d^{4}/8}$$

**Step 7: For very large $d$**, $\epsilon_{mp} d^{4} \gg E_{elec}$, so:

$$\frac{E_{direct}}{E_{multi}} \approx \frac{\epsilon_{mp} d^{4}}{\epsilon_{mp} d^{4}/8} = 8$$

This is the **asymptotic gain**. For the **small per-hop transmission energy regime** (i.e., when the inter-node distance is small but the direct distance is large enough that $d^{4}$ term dominates over the $E_{elec}$ term per hop), and ignoring the receive cost, the classical textbook **Heinzelman result** is:

$$\frac{E_{direct}}{E_{multi}} = \frac{d^{4}}{n \cdot (d/n)^{4}} = n^{\alpha - 1} = 2^{4-1} = 8$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU 2024 Valuation:** Different textbooks present this derivation slightly differently. The Heinzelman canonical result is $\dfrac{d^{4}}{4 (d/2)^{4}} = 4$ when **receive energy is ignored and the path is a single straight-line of $n$ equal segments with no receive cost on intermediate nodes**. If you include receive cost, the asymptotic gain is $n^{3} = 8$ for $n=2$. **Always state your assumption explicitly** — the KTU examiner awards marks for the correct assumption statement.

### 3.1.3 Engineering Interpretation

- For $\alpha = 4$ and 2 equal hops, multi-hop is **at least 4× more energy-efficient** (and asymptotically 8×) than direct transmission.
- This is precisely why *every* WSN case study from Great Duck Island to Zebranet uses multi-hop.
- General formula: optimal number of equal hops $n_{opt} = d \cdot \sqrt[\alpha-1]{\epsilon_{mp}/E_{elec}}$ (a closed-form result from calculus).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Network Lifetime Estimation under Uniform Energy Drain

This derivation is asked in the context of comparing two clustering protocols (e.g., LEACH vs static clustering) in a case study.

### 3.2.1 Given / Assumed

- $N = 100$ sensor nodes uniformly distributed in a $100 \text{ m} \times 100 \text{ m}$ field.
- Each node has initial energy $E_0 = 2$ J (2 AA batteries).
- Each round, a node generates $k = 2000$ bits.
- Each node transmits once per round to a cluster head at average distance $d_{toCH}$.
- Cluster head transmits aggregated data to BS at distance $d_{toBS} = 75$ m.
- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² (free space), $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ (multipath).
- Use LEACH assumptions: average $d_{toCH} = \dfrac{M}{\sqrt{\pi k_{cluster}}}$ for $M$ field side, $k_{cluster}$ clusters per round.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Energy spent by a non-cluster-head node in one round.**

$$E_{nonCH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Energy spent by a cluster head in one round (receives from all members, aggregates, transmits to BS).**

$$E_{CH} = k \cdot \frac{N}{k_{cluster}} \cdot E_{DA} + k \cdot \frac{N}{k_{cluster}} \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$$

where $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit is data aggregation cost.

**Step 3: Total energy per round.**

$$E_{round} = (N - k_{cluster}) \cdot E_{nonCH} + k_{cluster} \cdot E_{CH}$$

**Step 4: Number of rounds until first node dies.**

This requires solving:

$$T_{FND} = \dfrac{N \cdot E_0}{E_{round}}$$

**Step 5: Numerical example (typical LEACH textbook values).**

- $M = 100$ m, $N = 100$, $k_{cluster} = 5$, $d_{toBS} = 75$ m.
- $d_{toCH} = \dfrac{100}{\sqrt{\pi \cdot 5}} \approx 25.23$ m.
- $E_{nonCH} = 2000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} + 2000 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (25.23)^{2}$.
- $E_{nonCH} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.27 \times 10^{-5} = 1.13 \times 10^{-4}$ J.
- $E_{CH}$ computation gives (after substitution) $\approx 0.025$ J per round.
- $E_{round} \approx 95 \cdot 1.13 \times 10^{-4} + 5 \cdot 0.025 = 0.0107 + 0.125 = 0.136$ J per round.
- $T_{FND} = \dfrac{100 \cdot 2}{0.136} \approx 1470$ rounds.

### 3.2.3 Engineering Interpretation

- The cluster heads consume **10× more energy** than non-CH nodes per round → rotation of CH role (LEACH) is essential.
- If CH role is **not rotated**, $T_{FND}$ drops by an order of magnitude.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Two Canonical Case Studies

This matrix is what an examiner expects when the question says *"Compare LEACH and Directed Diffusion" or "Compare Great Duck Island and Zebranet architectures."*

| Feature | Great Duck Island (Habitat Monitoring) | Zebranet (Wildlife Tracking) |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | Seabird nesting behaviour | Zebra herd movement |
| **Sensed Quantity** | Temperature, humidity, pressure, light | GPS location (collar) |
| **Node Type** | Berkeley MICA / MICA2 motes | Custom collar-mounted nodes |
| **Topology** | 3-tier hierarchical (sensor → gateway → base) | Peer-to-peer mobile MANET |
| **Routing** | Multi-hop through sensor motes, gateway hop to BS | Peer-to-peer flooding, store-and-forward, ZebraNet's *History-based* protocol |
| **Mobility** | Static (sensor deployed on burrows) | Highly mobile (animal-mounted) |
| **Energy Source** | Battery (some with solar) | Battery only, collar weight constrained |
| **Data Model** | Periodic samples + threshold-triggered events | Sporadic, mobility-induced encounters |
| **Challenge Highlighted** | Hardware failure in harsh weather; RF attenuation by rocks; mote calibration drift | Disconnection, intermittent links, energy-aware peer selection |
| **Lesson Learned** | Need for tiered architecture; need for software re-tasking via TinyOS | Peer-to-peer "encounter" protocols outperform classical routing when mobility is high |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Two More Canonical Case Studies

| Feature | SensIT / DARPA (Military Surveillance) | CodeBlue (Medical BAN) |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | Battlefield target tracking | Patient vital-sign monitoring |
| **Sensed Quantity** | Acoustic, seismic, magnetic | ECG, SpO2, blood pressure, motion |
| **Node Type** | Heterogeneous (mica motes + PDA-class relays) | Tiny wearable motes + PDAs |
| **Topology** | Multi-hop with gateway | Star / multi-hop BAN |
| **Routing** | Directed Diffusion (attribute-based) | Publish/subscribe, priority-based |
| **Mobility** | Vehicles, soldiers, sensors | Patients (limited mobility) |
| **Energy Source** | Battery (with scavenging attempts) | Battery + wall-powered relays |
| **Data Model** | Event-driven, query-driven | Continuous vital signs + alarms |
| **Challenge** | False positives, node compromise, secure localization | Data integrity, regulatory (FDA), patient privacy |
| **Lesson Learned** | Need for multi-modal sensor fusion; secure time-sync | Body-area networks demand ultra-low-power radios (e.g., Bluetooth LE) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal Block-Level Functional Architecture of a WSN Case Study

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **universal functional architecture** common to nearly every WSN case study. It abstracts away the physical details and emphasises the *information flow* and *control flow* between tiers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    field[("PHYSICAL PHENOMENON\n(temperature, vibration, gas)")]
    sens["SENSING LAYER\n(motes with MEMS sensors, ADC)"]
    proc["PROCESSING LAYER\n(microcontroller, TinyOS, nesC)"]
    rad["RADIO LAYER\n(low-power transceiver, MAC)"]
    rout["ROUTING / AGGREGATION LAYER\n(multi-hop, clustering)"]
    gw["GATEWAY / SINK\n(field-laptop or base station)"]
    iot["INFRASTRUCTURE LAYER\n(Internet / cloud server)"]
    user(["APPLICATION / USER\n(decisions, dashboards)"])
    batt[("ENERGY SOURCE\n(battery, solar, scavenger)")]

    field --> sens
    sens --> proc
    proc --> rad
    rad --> rout
    rout --> gw
    gw --> iot
    iot --> user
    batt -. powers .-> sens
    batt -. powers .-> proc
    batt -. powers .-> rad
    rout -. queries .-> proc
    user -. tasks .-> iot
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Guide for the Examiner:** Arrows are *information flow*. Dotted arrows are *power supply* and *tasking/control flow*. Every case study you study in KTU Module 1 will map onto this template — only the **labels** change.

## 4.2 Comparative Architecture: Hierarchical vs Flat vs Mobile (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HIER["HIERARCHICAL (e.g., Great Duck Island)"]
        H1[("Sensor Mote")]
        H2[("Sensor Mote")]
        H3[("Sensor Mote")]
        HG[("Gateway Mote")]
        HB[("Base Station")]
        H1 --> HG
        H2 --> HG
        H3 --> HG
        HG --> HB
    end

    subgraph FLAT["FLAT (e.g., early MICA farm deployment)"]
        F1[("Mote")]
        F2[("Mote")]
        F3[("Mote")]
        FB[("Base Station")]
        F1 --> FB
        F2 --> FB
        F3 --> FB
    end

    subgraph MOBILE["MOBILE (e.g., ZebraNet)"]
        M1[("Collar Node")]
        M2[("Collar Node")]
        M3[("Collar Node")]
        M4[("Mobile Base / Vehicle")]
        M1 -. opportunistic link .- M2
        M2 -. opportunistic link .- M3
        M3 -. encounter-based .- M4
    end
```

## 4.3 Energy State Machine of a Mote (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SLEEP
    SLEEP --> WAKE: timer / external interrupt
    WAKE --> SENSE: sample ADC
    SENSE --> PROCESS: run application code
    PROCESS --> TRANSMIT: radio TX with payload
    TRANSMIT --> RECEIVE: listen for ACK
    RECEIVE --> SLEEP: ACK ok, duty cycle done
    RECEIVE --> RETRY: ACK fail, increment counter
    RETRY --> TRANSMIT: retry less than max
    RETRY --> SLEEP: retry exhausted
    SLEEP --> [*]: battery depleted
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical note:** Every case study implicitly or explicitly describes a slightly different version of this state machine. Great Duck Island had a *very* long SLEEP state. Zebranet had a *very* active TRANSMIT/RECEIVE pair during encounters. SensIT emphasized RETRY transitions due to battlefield interference.

## 4.4 Data-Centric vs Address-Centric Naming (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DCN["DATA-CENTRIC (WSN case studies)"]
        D1["Sink query: 'temperature in region R > 30 C'"]
        D2["Mote with matching attribute replies"]
        D3["No IP addresses used"]
        D1 --> D2 --> D3
    end

    subgraph ACN["ADDRESS-CENTRIC (Internet)"]
        A1["Application: 'GET /node/192.168.0.42/temp'"]
        A2["HTTP routed by IP address"]
        A3["Standard TCP/IP stack"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3
    end
```
