# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Distribution Note (2024 Scheme):** Part A carries 3 marks per question. The expected answer length is 80–120 words. Students must present a *defining sentence* + a *factual expansion* + (where relevant) a *one-line example or formula*. No diagrams needed in Part A; the examiner checks factual recall and structure.

### Question 1: Define a WSN case study. List three classic examples with one-line descriptions. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A WSN case study is an empirical, in-depth account of a real-world sensor network deployment that documents the problem context, design choices, protocols, hardware, and operational outcomes.
Three classic examples:

1. **Great Duck Island (2002):** Habitat monitoring of Leach's Storm Petrel using Berkeley motes in a 3-tier hierarchical topology.
2. **Zebranet (2004):** Mobile peer-to-peer collar-mounted nodes tracking zebra herds in Kenya.
3. **SensIT (DARPA, early 2000s):** Military battlefield surveillance with multi-modal sensors and Directed Diffusion routing.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between data-centric and address-centric naming with one WSN example for each. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Data-Centric (WSN) | Address-Centric (Internet) |
|---|---|---|
| Query basis | By attribute (e.g., "temperature > 30 °C") | By IP address |
| Identity | Attribute-value pairs | Unique global ID |
| Example | Directed Diffusion in SensIT | HTTP GET on a server |

Data-centric naming suits WSNs because nodes are queried by *what they sense* rather than *who they are*, reducing the cost of maintaining unique addresses across thousands of motes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Distribution (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks. Conventionally split as **7 + 7** marks across sub-parts. Students answer **ONE** out of **TWO** choices. The answer should include **at least one diagram** and at least **one quantitative worked example** to score full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Great Duck Island Case Study in Depth

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the Great Duck Island habitat monitoring case study. Highlight the application, the three-tier architecture, the hardware used, and the energy/communication challenges faced.

**Model Answer:**

Great Duck Island is a small island off the coast of Maine, USA, used since 2002 by researchers (Mainwaring, Polastre, Szewczyk, Culler) to monitor the nesting behaviour of **Leach's Storm Petrel**.

**Application & Sensed Quantities:** Temperature, humidity, barometric pressure, and infrared (bird presence). Sampling rate: 1 sample per 2–5 minutes.

**Hardware:**
- **Sensor motes:** Berkeley MICA / MICA2DOT platforms with MTS310 sensor boards.
- **Gateway motes:** MICA2 with higher transmit power, acting as cluster heads.
- **Base station:** Stargate-class single-board computer with Internet uplink (satellite or 802.11b).

**Three-Tier Architecture:**
- **Tier 1 (Sensor motes):** Placed inside / near burrows. Multi-hop routes data to Tier 2.
- **Tier 2 (Gateway motes):** Aggregate Tier 1 traffic and forward to Tier 3.
- **Tier 3 (Base station):** Permanent, wall-powered, uploads to a server in Berkeley.

**Protocols:** TinyOS, B-MAC, multi-hop tree routing, in-network aggregation.

**Challenges Faced:**
1. **Hardware failure in harsh weather:** Salt spray, wind, and bird interference damaged sensor motes.
2. **RF attenuation:** Wet leaves and rock attenuated radio signals unexpectedly, breaking assumptions about transmission range.
3. **Calibration drift:** MEMS sensors drifted over weeks, requiring in-field recalibration.
4. **Energy:** Battery life limited deployment to weeks without replacement.
5. **Software re-tasking:** Required redesigning TinyOS to allow remote code updates.

**Lesson Learned:** Real WSNs need **tiered, fault-tolerant, and remotely re-taskable** architectures — pure flat topologies do not scale in the wild.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Using the first-order radio model, show that multi-hop transmission is more energy-efficient than direct transmission for a node 100 m from the sink, with $k = 2000$ bits, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, $\alpha = 4$, and a 2-hop path.

**Model Solution:**

Given: $k = 2000$ bits, $d = 100$ m, $E_{elec} = 50 \times 10^{-9}$ J/bit, $\epsilon_{mp} = 1.3 \times 10^{-15}$ J/bit/m⁴, $\alpha = 4$.

**Step 1: Direct transmission energy.**

$$E_{direct} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4}$$

$$E_{direct} = (50 \times 10^{-9})(2000) + (1.3 \times 10^{-15})(2000)(100)^{4}$$

$$E_{direct} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + (1.3 \times 10^{-15})(2000)(10^{8})$$

$$E_{direct} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 2.6 \times 10^{-4} = 3.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}$$

**Step 2: Multi-hop transmission energy (2 equal hops of 50 m).**

For each hop, since the distance is 50 m, we use the free-space model ($\alpha = 2$, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10 \times 10^{-12}$ J/bit/m²) — but for consistency with the problem we keep the multipath model.

$$E_{hop1}^{TX} = (50 \times 10^{-9})(2000) + (1.3 \times 10^{-15})(2000)(50)^{4}$$

$$E_{hop1}^{TX} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + (1.3 \times 10^{-15})(2000)(6.25 \times 10^{6})$$

$$E_{hop1}^{TX} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.625 \times 10^{-5} = 1.1625 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}$$

**Step 3: Receive energy at intermediate node.**

$$E_{RX} = (50 \times 10^{-9})(2000) = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}$$

**Step 4: Second-hop transmission (same as hop 1).**

$$E_{hop2}^{TX} = 1.1625 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}$$

**Step 5: Total multi-hop energy.**

$$E_{multi} = 1.1625 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.1625 \times 10^{-4} = 3.325 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}$$

**Step 6: Ratio.**

$$\frac{E_{direct}}{E_{multi}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-4}}{3.325 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1.083$$

The gain is modest in this small example because the receive cost of the intermediate node is non-trivial. For larger $d$ (e.g., 200 m), the ratio grows to $\approx 3$ and asymptotically approaches **4** under the canonical Heinzelman assumption (ignoring receive cost and using a single straight-line path divided into $n$ equal segments with $n=2$).

**Conclusion:** Multi-hop is energy-efficient for WSNs, and the benefit grows as the source-to-sink distance increases.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** A very common mistake is to compute $E_{direct}$ correctly but **forget the receive energy of the intermediate node** in $E_{multi}$. The student then writes $E_{multi} = 2 \cdot E_{hop}^{TX}$ instead of $E_{multi} = 2 \cdot E_{hop}^{TX} + E_{RX}$. This costs 2 of the 7 marks. **Always** write down the receive term explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Zebranet Case Study in Depth

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the Zebranet case study. Explain why classical multi-hop routing fails in this scenario and describe the encounter-based protocol used.

**Model Answer:**

**Background:** Zebranet is a wildlife-tracking WSN deployed by Princeton researchers (Juang, Oki, Wang, Martonosi, Rubenstein) in 2004 to track zebra movement in the Sweetwaters Game Reserve, Kenya.

**Application:** Mobility-based monitoring of wildlife. Each zebra wears a collar-mounted sensor node with a **GPS receiver** and a short-range radio.

**Why Classical Multi-Hop Fails:**

1. **High mobility:** Zebras move continuously; routes are valid for seconds, not minutes.
2. **Sparsity:** The herd is dispersed over a wide area; finding a 2-hop neighbor is uncertain.
3. **Disconnection tolerance:** The network is *expected* to be partitioned. Store-and-forward at every node is mandatory.
4. **Heterogeneous link quality:** Some zebras are within range for a few minutes, others never.

**Encounter-Based Protocol (ZebraNet's History-based Routing):**

- When two nodes meet, they exchange **summary information** about the data they hold.
- Each node maintains a **history** of past encounters and ranks other nodes by a **"level of interest"** function (e.g., a node seen frequently is a better forwarder).
- Data is forwarded to whichever encountered node has the *highest* level of interest, biasing delivery toward nodes likely to meet the mobile base station (a research vehicle that periodically drives through the reserve).
- The mobile base station is the only persistent sink.

**Hardware:** Custom collar with a GPS, 8-bit microcontroller, 900 MHz radio, and battery.

**Lessons Learned:**
- Disconnection-tolerant networking (DTN) is essential for mobile WSNs.
- Encounter history is a *better* routing metric than minimum hop count.
- Wildlife tracking demands ultra-light, low-power, rugged hardware.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare the architecture of Great Duck Island and Zebranet across at least six dimensions. Which design lessons from these case studies are applicable to a smart agriculture deployment in Kerala?

**Model Answer:**

**Comparative Table:**

| Dimension | Great Duck Island | Zebranet |
|---|---|---|
| Topology | Hierarchical (3-tier) | Mobile, peer-to-peer |
| Mobility | Static | Highly mobile |
| Routing | Multi-hop tree | Encounter-based, DTN-style |
| Energy source | Battery + occasional solar | Battery only |
| Application class | Environmental monitoring | Wildlife tracking |
| Data model | Periodic + event | Sporadic, encounter-triggered |
| Hardware | Off-the-shelf Berkeley motes | Custom collar |
| Lesson | Tiered, fault-tolerant design | Store-and-forward with mobility |

**Applicability to Kerala Smart Agriculture:**

1. **Tiered architecture from Great Duck Island** is directly applicable — Kerala farms can use a 3-tier setup: field motes (soil moisture, pH) → gateway in the farm → cloud server.
2. **Energy harvesting** (solar) lessons from GDI apply to Kerala's high-insolation conditions.
3. **Adaptive duty cycling** is needed because Kerala's humidity stresses MEMS sensors.
4. **Zebranet's encounter-based protocol** is *not* directly applicable (plants don't move), but the **store-and-forward principle** helps when farm nodes are disconnected due to monsoon flooding.
5. **TinyOS-based remote re-tasking** is critical so farmers don't need to physically access every node.
6. **Calibration drift lessons** from GDI → periodic re-calibration routines must be built in.

**Conclusion:** Kerala smart agriculture should adopt a **Great Duck Island tiered architecture** with **Zebranet-inspired DTN principles** as a fallback during monsoon-induced disconnections.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part a):** Many students answer "Zebranet is a wireless network for zebras" and stop. This scores 0. The examiner expects **why classical routing fails** (the *design lesson*) and the **encounter-based protocol's mechanism** (the *engineering solution*). A one-line answer without the protocol's name and principle loses 4 of 7 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** The comparison table alone is **not** sufficient. The examiner awards full 7 marks only if the student also provides the **applicability analysis** (Kerala smart-agri context). A table with no follow-up paragraph loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **last-page cheat sheet** for the ESE. Read it 5 minutes before you enter the exam hall.

- ✅ **Definition:** A WSN case study is an empirical, in-depth account of a real-world deployment documenting problem → design → implementation → evaluation.
- ✅ **Five-Question Template:** WHO, WHAT, HOW (routing), WHAT (energy), WHAT (lessons).
- ✅ **Three-Tier Architecture** (Great Duck Island): sensor motes → gateway motes → base station.
- ✅ **Encounter-Based Routing** (Zebranet): routing decision is based on level-of-interest from past encounters; classical multi-hop fails when mobility is high.
- ✅ **First-Order Radio Model:** $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} k + \epsilon_{amp} k d^{\alpha}$. **Always** state the receive term in multi-hop derivations.
- ✅ **Multi-Hop Gain (asymptotic, $\alpha=4$, $n=2$ hops, no RX cost):** 4× (Heinzelman) or 8× (with RX cost). The gain grows with $d$.
- ✅ **Duty cycle** is the fundamental energy-saving knob: $\delta = T_{active} / (T_{active} + T_{sleep})$.
- ✅ **Cross-over distance:** $d_{cross} = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}} \approx 87$ m for canonical values.
- ✅ **Data-Centric vs Address-Centric:** WSNs use attribute-based naming; the Internet uses IP-based naming.
- ✅ **TinyOS / nesC** is the dominant OS for mote-class nodes.
- ✅ **Data aggregation gain** $G_{agg} = N_{raw}/N_{aggregated}$ is the single most important efficiency metric in cluster-based case studies.
- ✅ **Network lifetime** is reported as FND (First Node Dies) and LND (Last Node Dies); FND is the conservative KTU standard.
- ✅ **Common KTU ESE mistakes to avoid:**
  - Forgetting the receive term in multi-hop energy derivations.
  - Describing a case study without naming the *protocol* used.
  - Confusing SensIT (military) with Sensoria / SensoNet (academic prototypes).
  - Writing "Zebranet uses LEACH" — it does not; it uses encounter-based history routing.
  - Quoting voltage without a reference node (always say "**per Berkeley MICA**" or "**per TelosB**").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Routing in Adhoc Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT863 - ADHOC AND WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Routing in Adhoc Networks # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Routing in Mobile Ad hoc Networks (MANETs)** is defined as the process of selecting optimal paths in a network along which data packets can be forwarded from a source node to a destination node, where the network topology is dynamic, decentralized, and infrastructure-less. The Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) MANET Working Group characterizes an ad hoc routing protocol as a *convention*, or *standard*, that controls how nodes decide which way to route packets between computing devices in a mobile ad hoc network. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, routing in ad hoc networks refers to the set of algorithms, protocols, and mechanisms that enable **multi-hop communication** among mobile nodes acting simultaneously as hosts and routers, without relying on any pre-existing fixed infrastructure such as base stations or access points. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The fundamental premise of ad hoc routing is the absence of any dedicated router or central administrator. Every node is a potential router, and routing decisions must be made collectively and dynamically in response to frequent topology changes caused by node mobility, link failures, battery exhaustion, and environmental obstructions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a group of **50 friends scattered across a huge dark forest** at night, with no map, no phones with GPS, and no central guide. Alice wants to pass a secret message to Bob, who is 2 kilometers away. She cannot shout that far. So she whispers it to Carol, who is closest to her. Carol whispers it to David, and so on, until it reaches Bob. Now imagine that the friends are **constantly walking around randomly**. The friend who was closest a second ago might now be far away. So Alice and her friends must constantly **re-discover** who is nearby, who can hear whom, and figure out a fresh chain of whispers every few seconds. This is exactly what an ad hoc network is: - The **friends** = mobile nodes (laptops, phones, sensors). - The **whispering** = wireless transmission over a limited radio range. - The **chain of whispers** = a multi-hop route. - The **constant walking** = node mobility causing topology change. - The **rediscovery** = the routing protocol's job. Routing in ad hoc networks, therefore, is the **continuously self-updating "whisper relay map"** that allows messages to traverse a moving crowd of wireless devices. ## 1.3 Core Terminology and Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Essential KTU 2024 Definitions to Memorize:** | Term | Formal Meaning | |------|----------------| | **Node** | A mobile wireless device participating in the ad hoc network. | | **Hop** | A single forward transmission of a packet from one node to the next. | | **Hop Count** | The number of intermediate nodes a packet traverses. | | **Path** | A sequence of nodes and links forming a route from source to destination. | | **Route Discovery** | The process of finding a valid path to a destination. | | **Route Maintenance** | The process of repairing or replacing a broken route. | | **Topology** | The instantaneous pattern of links connecting all active nodes. | | **Convergence** | The time taken by all nodes to update and agree on routing information. | | **End-to-End Delay** | Total time a packet takes from source to destination. | | **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** | Ratio of packets successfully delivered to packets sent. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric Units in Ad Hoc Routing:** > - **Hop Count:** dimensionless integer (≥ 1). > - **End-to-End Delay:** **milliseconds (ms)**. > - **Throughput:** **bits per second (bps)** or **kilobits per second (kbps)**. > - **Routing Overhead:** bytes or number of control packets. > - **Jitter:** variation in delay, measured in **ms**. > - **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR):** a percentage (%). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Traditional Routing Fails in Ad Hoc Networks Conventional routing protocols such as **RIP (Routing Information Protocol)**, **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)**, and **BGP (Border Gateway Protocol)** were designed for **fixed, wired networks** with relatively stable topologies. They fail in ad hoc environments for the following structured reasons: 1. **Dynamic Topology:** Traditional protocols assume that link changes are infrequent. In ad hoc networks, links break and form continuously due to mobility, causing routing tables to become stale rapidly. 2. **No Centralized Infrastructure:** Traditional protocols often rely on hierarchical structures (area border routers, designated routers) which do not exist in ad hoc networks. 3. **Asymmetric / Unreliable Links:** Wireless links are not only lossy but also often asymmetric. Many traditional protocols assume bidirectional symmetric links. 4. **Resource Constraints:** Mobile nodes operate on limited battery power and bandwidth. Periodic table updates of traditional protocols waste precious energy. 5. **Scalability Issues:** Proactive flooding of link-state information across thousands of mobile nodes creates excessive control overhead (often called the *broadcast storm problem*). 6. **Hidden and Exposed Terminal Problems:** Unique to wireless shared-medium networks, these problems are absent in wired routing assumptions. ## 2.2 Fundamental Classification of Ad Hoc Routing Protocols The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2) requires a clear classification. The protocols are broadly classified along **four orthogonal axes**: ### Axis 1: By Route Discovery Timing - **Proactive (Table-Driven) Protocols:** Maintain up-to-date routing information for **all** destinations at **all times**, regardless of traffic. Example: **DSDV (Destination-Sequenced Distance-Vector)**. - **Reactive (On-Demand) Protocols:** Discover a route **only when** a node actually needs to send data to a destination. Example: **DSR (Dynamic Source Routing)**, **AODV (Ad hoc On-demand Distance Vector)**. - **Hybrid Protocols:** Combine the best of both; use proactive routing within a local zone and reactive routing across zones. Example: **ZRP (Zone Routing Protocol)**. ### Axis 2: By Network Organization - **Flat Routing:** All nodes are equal; no hierarchy. Example: AODV, DSR. - **Hierarchical Routing:** Nodes are organized into clusters or zones, with a cluster-head or zone leader. Example: **CBRP (Cluster Based Routing Protocol)**, ZRP. ### Axis 3: By Path Information - **Source Routing:** The complete route is encoded in the packet header at the source. Example: **DSR**. - **Hop-by-Hop Routing:** Each intermediate node decides the next hop independently using its own routing table. Example: **AODV, DSDV**. ### Axis 4: By Link Property - **Symmetric-link protocols:** Assume bidirectional equal-quality links. - **Asymmetric-link protocols:** Tolerate differing uplink/downlink qualities. ## 2.3 Operational Phases of Any Ad Hoc Routing Protocol Regardless of being proactive or reactive, every ad hoc routing protocol exhibits three operational phases: - **Phase 1 — Neighbor Discovery (Hello Mechanism):** A node broadcasts a small "HELLO" packet at periodic intervals. All nodes within radio range reply, building a one-hop neighbor table. This is the foundational input for any routing decision. - **Phase 2 — Route Discovery / Update:** Based on the protocol type: - Proactive → Periodic broadcast of full or incremental routing tables. - Reactive → Broadcast of a Route Request (RREQ) only when needed. - **Phase 3 — Route Maintenance:** When a link is detected broken (via missing link-layer ACKs or hello-timeout), a Route Error (RERR) is propagated. The route is either repaired locally, or a fresh discovery is initiated. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Ad Hoc Routing Metrics & Equations for KTU 2024 Exams** | Metric / Formula | Symbol | Definition / Engineering Meaning | |------------------|--------|----------------------------------| | **Hop Count** | $H$ | Number of intermediate nodes in a path; lower is preferred. | | **End-to-End Delay** | $D_{e2e}$ | Sum of queuing, transmission, propagation, and processing delays across all hops. | | **Average End-to-End Delay** | $\bar{D}$ | $$\bar{D} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{recv,i} - t_{sent,i})$$ where $N$ = number of successfully delivered packets. | | **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** | $\text{PDR}$ | $$\text{PDR} = \frac{P_{received}}{P_{sent}} \times 100\%$$ | | **Normalized Routing Load (NRL)** | $\text{NRL}$ | $$\text{NRL} = \frac{C_{packets}}{P_{delivered}}$$ where $C_{packets}$ = total control packets transmitted per data packet delivered. | | **Throughput** | $T$ | $$T = \frac{\text{Total data bits received}}{\text{Total time (s)}} \quad [\text{bps}]$$ | | **Path Optimality (Shortest Path Metric)** | $L_{opt}$ | $$L_{opt} = \min_{P \in \Pi(s,d)} \sum_{i \in P} w(i)$$ where $\Pi(s,d)$ is the set of all paths from $s$ to $d$ and $w(i)$ is the link weight. | | **Control Overhead Ratio** | $\eta$ | $$\eta = \frac{\text{Control bytes transmitted}}{\text{Total bytes transmitted}} \times 100\%$$ | | **Route Discovery Latency** | $t_{disc}$ | Time between sending an RREQ and receiving the first corresponding RREP. | | **Route Lifetime** | $t_{life}$ | Mean time a route remains valid before breaking due to mobility. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Note:** When $|x|$ (absolute value) is needed in any formula inside a table, write it as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to preserve the markdown table structure. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Ad Hoc Routing The principles studied in this module directly enable: - **Military battlefield networks** (soldiers, vehicles, UAVs). - **Disaster recovery communications** when cellular towers are destroyed. - **Vehicle Ad hoc Networks (VANETs)** for intelligent transportation. - **Internet of Things (IoT)** and **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)**. - **Underwater acoustic networks** for oceanographic sensing. - **Pocket-switched networks** for opportunistic communication. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Average End-to-End Delay in a Multi-Hop Ad Hoc Path **Problem Setup:** A packet travels from source $S$ to destination $D$ through $H$ intermediate hops. At each hop $i$, four delay components occur. Derive the total end-to-end delay and its average. **Step 1 — Define the four delay components at hop $i$:** $$d_{q,i} = \text{queuing delay at node } i$$ $$d_{t,i} = \text{transmission delay at node } i$$ $$d_{p,i} = \text{propagation delay on link } i$$ $$d_{pr,i} = \text{processing delay at node } i$$ **Step 2 — Express the per-hop delay $d_i$ as the algebraic sum of the four components:** $$d_i = d_{q,i} + d_{t,i} + d_{p,i} + d_{pr,i}$$ **Step 3 — Sum across all $H$ hops to obtain total end-to-end delay $D_{e2e}$:** $$D_{e2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{H} d_i = \sum_{i=1}^{H} (d_{q,i} + d_{t,i} + d_{p,i} + d_{pr,i})$$ **Step 4 — For a homogeneous scenario (identical delay at every hop), apply the simplification $d_i = d$ for all $i$:** $$D_{e2e} = H \cdot d$$ **Step 5 — If $N$ packets are observed and each takes $H_k$ hops with delay $d_k$, derive the average end-to-end delay $\bar{D}$:** $$\bar{D} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} D_{e2e,k} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \sum_{i=1}^{H_k} d_{i,k}$$ **Conversion logic:** The first equation is the formal definition; the second step groups the four physical delay contributors at a single hop; the third step applies superposition across the route; the fourth step uses the uniform-hop assumption to obtain the closed form $D_{e2e} = H \cdot d$; the fifth step generalizes to the empirical average computed by KTU lab evaluations (typically using NS-2/NS-3 trace files). ## 3.2 Derivation: Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR) for Reactive Protocols **Problem Setup:** In a reactive protocol like AODV, a Route Request (RREQ) is flooded; some RREQs collide and are lost. Derive PDR in terms of RREQ success probability. **Step 1 — Let $p$ be the per-hop successful delivery probability of a control packet (RREQ/RREP) over the wireless medium.** **Step 2 — For a route requiring $H$ hops, the probability that the RREQ successfully reaches the destination is $p^H$ (independent Bernoulli trials):** $$P_{RREQ} = p^H$$ **Step 3 — Similarly, the RREP must traverse the reverse $H$ hops. Assuming channel symmetry, the success probability is also $p^H$:** $$P_{RREP} = p^H$$ **Step 4 — The route is successfully established only if both RREQ and RREP succeed, which is the joint probability under independence:** $$P_{route} = P_{RREQ} \times P_{RREP} = p^H \cdot p^H = p^{2H}$$ **Step 5 — If the source sends $P_{sent}$ data packets and a fraction $P_{route}$ of them find a usable path, the delivered packets approximate:** $$P_{received} \approx P_{sent} \cdot P_{route} = P_{sent} \cdot p^{2H}$$ **Step 6 — Therefore, the Packet Delivery Ratio becomes:** $$\text{PDR} = \frac{P_{received}}{P_{sent}} \times 100\% = p^{2H} \times 100\%$$ **Conversion logic:** Step 1 introduces the channel model; Step 2 uses the chain rule for independent hops; Step 3 mirrors the return trip; Step 4 multiplies the two probabilities for a successful two-way handshake (essential for symmetric protocols); Step 5 conditions data delivery on route establishment; Step 6 expresses PDR in closed form, showing its **exponential decay with hop count $H$** — a critical result justifying why ad hoc networks are sensitive to network diameter. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Proactive vs Reactive vs Hybrid > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Comparison Matrix — High-Yield for 14-Mark Questions** | Parameter | Proactive (DSDV) | Reactive (AODV / DSR) | Hybrid (ZRP) | |-----------|------------------|------------------------|---------------| | **Route Availability** | Always available | Available only after discovery | Available within zone immediately, on-demand across zones | | **Latency for First Packet** | Low (route pre-computed) | High (discovery needed) | Moderate (zone-local is fast) | | **Control Overhead** | High (periodic updates) | Low (only on demand) | Moderate (depends on zone radius) | | **Scalability** | Poor (floods tables) | Better (limited flooding) | Good (tunable zone radius) | | **Storage at Nodes** | Large routing tables | Route cache (small) | Zone routing table + cache | | **Bandwidth Consumption** | Continuous | Burst during discovery | Mixed | | **Battery Drain** | High (always updating) | Low when idle | Moderate | | **Loop Freedom** | Yes (sequence numbers) | Yes (sequence numbers) | Yes (zone-based) | | **Mobility Tolerance** | Low–Medium | High | Medium–High | | **Best Suited For** | Small, low-mobility networks | Medium, high-mobility networks | Large, hierarchical networks | | **Example Protocols** | DSDV, OLSR | AODV, DSR, TORA | ZRP, CBRP, ZHLS | ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Source Routing vs Hop-by-Hop Routing | Feature | Source Routing (DSR) | Hop-by-Hop Routing (AODV) | |---------|----------------------|----------------------------| | **Route Stored In** | Packet header | Routing table at each node | | **Header Size** | Large (grows with $H$) | Small (constant) | | **Intermediate Node Processing** | Minimal (just forward) | Routing table lookup | | **Route Change Reaction** | Source must discover new route | Local table update | | **Security Vulnerability** | Higher (route in header) | Lower | | **Scalability to Large $H$** | Poor | Good | | **Channel Overhead** | Lower control packets, larger data packets | Higher control packets, smaller data packets | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of an Ad Hoc Routing Protocol The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a generic ad hoc routing protocol**, showing how the five key modules interact. This is a direct adaptation of the standard MANET protocol stack model taught in KTU Module 2. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Application Layer
Data Packet Source] --> B[Routing Agent
Decides Path] B --> C{Need New Route?} C -- Yes --> D[Route Discovery Module
RREQ Flooding] C -- No --> E[Route Cache or Table
Lookup Module] D --> F[Route Reply Handler
RREP Processing] F --> G[Route Maintenance Module
RERR and Link Monitoring] E --> G G --> H[Neighbor Discovery Module
HELLO Beacon Exchange] H --> I[MAC Layer
IEEE 802.11 DCF] I --> J[Physical Wireless
Radio Interface] J --> K[Other Mobile Nodes
in Ad Hoc Network] K --> I I --> L[Route Maintenance Module
Route Maintenance Module] L --> B ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Classification Tree of Ad Hoc Routing Protocols ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ad Hoc Routing Protocols] --> B[Proactive
Table Driven] A --> C[Reactive
On Demand] A --> D[Hybrid] B --> B1[DSDV] B --> B2[OLSR] B --> B3[CGSR] C --> C1[AODV] C --> C2[DSR] C --> C3[TORA] C --> C4[ABR] D --> D1[ZRP] D --> D2[CBRP] D --> D3[ZHLS] A --> E[Other Classifications] E --> F[Flat Routing] E --> G[Hierarchical Routing] E --> H[Source Routing] E --> I[Hop by Hop Routing] E --> J[Geographic Position Based] J --> J1[GPSR] J --> J2[GEAR] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Operational Phases of a Reactive (On-Demand) Protocol ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Phase 1: Neighbor Discovery
HELLO Beacon Exchange] --> P2[Phase 2: Route Discovery
Source Floods RREQ] P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Route Reply
Destination Sends RREP] P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Data Transmission
Forwarding Using Route] P4 --> P5{Link Broken?} P5 -- Yes --> P6[Phase 5: Route Maintenance
RERR Propagated] P5 -- No --> P4 P6 --> P2 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Multi-Hop Route in a MANET ```mermaid flowchart LR S((Source Node S)) -- hop 1 --> N1((Intermediate N1)) N1 -- hop 2 --> N2((Intermediate N2)) N2 -- hop 3 --> N3((Intermediate N3)) N3 -- hop 4 --> D((Destination D)) ``` **Interpretation:** The packet traverses $H = 4$ hops. Each intermediate node (N1, N2, N3) acts as a router. If any single link breaks due to mobility, the entire route fails and route maintenance is triggered. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define ad hoc routing. Why are traditional Internet routing protocols unsuitable for Mobile Ad Hoc Networks? **Model Answer:** **Ad hoc routing** is the process of discovering and maintaining paths for packet delivery in a network where mobile nodes communicate wirelessly without any fixed infrastructure, with each node acting as both a host and a potential router. Traditional Internet routing protocols (RIP, OSPF, BGP) are unsuitable because: 1. **Frequent topology changes:** They assume static or slowly changing topologies, but ad hoc networks experience rapid, unpredictable link changes due to node mobility. 2. **Centralized infrastructure assumption:** They rely on designated routers and hierarchical structures, which do not exist in decentralized ad hoc networks. 3. **High control overhead:** Periodic table updates consume excessive bandwidth and battery power in resource-constrained mobile devices. 4. **Symmetric link assumption:** Wired links are reliable and symmetric, but wireless links are often lossy and asymmetric. 5. **Lack of scalability:** Flooding link-state updates in a dynamic MANET triggers broadcast storms. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only "mobility" as the reason. You must list **at least three specific limitations** to score full marks. Mentioning *bandwidth*, *battery*, *symmetric links*, and *broadcast storm* is highly appreciated. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Differentiate between proactive and reactive routing protocols. Give one example of each. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Proactive (Table-Driven) | Reactive (On-Demand) | |---------|--------------------------|----------------------| | **Route Discovery** | Continuous, periodic | Only when needed | | **Latency** | Low (route pre-computed) | High (first-packet delay) | | **Overhead** | High control traffic | Low when idle | | **Storage** | Large routing tables | Smaller route cache | | **Example** | **DSDV** (Destination-Sequenced Distance-Vector) | **AODV** (Ad hoc On-demand Distance Vector) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** write only one-line definitions. Always use a **comparison table** to earn full marks in KTU valuation. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the classification of ad hoc routing protocols with a neat diagram. Compare proactive and reactive approaches. **(b)** [7 Marks] A reactive ad hoc routing protocol uses a per-hop success probability of $p = 0.85$. For a route of $H = 4$ hops, compute the route establishment probability and the resulting Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR). Comment on how PDR changes as $H$ doubles. **Model Solution for (a):** The classification follows the structure illustrated in the Mermaid diagram of Section 4.2. The two principal families are: 1. **Proactive (Table-Driven):** Maintain routes to all destinations continuously. Example: DSDV. 2. **Reactive (On-Demand):** Discover routes only when needed. Examples: AODV, DSR. 3. **Hybrid:** Combine both. Example: ZRP. A comparison of proactive vs reactive must be given in a **table format** covering: - Route availability (always vs on-demand). - Latency (low vs high). - Control overhead (high vs low). - Storage requirement (large table vs small cache). - Suitability (small/low-mobility vs medium/high-mobility). - Power consumption (high vs low). **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** Per-hop success probability $p = 0.85$, number of hops $H = 4$. **Step 1 — Route establishment probability (RREQ × RREP):** $$P_{route} = p^{2H} = (0.85)^{2 \times 4} = (0.85)^{8}$$ **Step 2 — Numerical evaluation:** $$(0.85)^2 = 0.7225$$ $$(0.85)^4 = (0.7225)^2 = 0.52200625$$ $$(0.85)^8 = (0.52200625)^2 \approx 0.27249$$ **Step 3 — Route establishment probability:** $$P_{route} \approx 0.2725 \; (\text{or } 27.25\%)$$ **Step 4 — Packet Delivery Ratio:** $$\text{PDR} = P_{route} \times 100\% \approx 27.25\%$$ **Step 5 — When $H$ doubles to $8$:** $$\text{PDR}_{H=8} = (0.85)^{2 \times 8} = (0.85)^{16} = ((0.85)^8)^2 \approx (0.2725)^2 \approx 0.0743 \; \text{or} \; 7.43\%$$ **Step 6 — Comment:** Doubling the hop count causes an **exponential collapse** of PDR (from ~27% down to ~7%), confirming that ad hoc networks are highly sensitive to network diameter. This justifies the design of cluster-based and hierarchical protocols for large-scale deployments. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that the RREP must also be transmitted successfully and write $p^H$ instead of $p^{2H}$. Always state the *joint probability* of both RREQ and RREP succeeding for symmetric protocols. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]** **(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the operational phases of a generic ad hoc routing protocol. What are the key challenges in ad hoc routing? **(b)** [7 Marks] An ad hoc network with $H = 5$ hops observes the following one-way delays at each hop (in ms): $d_1 = 12$, $d_2 = 15$, $d_3 = 10$, $d_4 = 18$, $d_5 = 8$. Calculate the total end-to-end delay $D_{e2e}$ and the average delay per hop. **Model Solution for (a):** The block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram) must be sketched to show: - Application Layer → Routing Agent → (Route Discovery / Route Cache) → Route Maintenance → Neighbor Discovery → MAC → Physical → Wireless Medium. - A feedback loop from the wireless medium back to Route Maintenance (for handling link breaks). **Key challenges in ad hoc routing (list at least 5):** 1. **Mobility-induced topology changes.** 2. **Limited bandwidth and shared medium.** 3. **Energy constraints (battery).** 4. **Hidden and exposed terminal problems.** 5. **Asymmetric links.** 6. **Security vulnerabilities** (no central authentication). 7. **Scalability** in large networks. 8. **Broadcast storm problem** due to flooding. **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** $H = 5$ hops, with delays $d_1 = 12$, $d_2 = 15$, $d_3 = 10$, $d_4 = 18$, $d_5 = 8$ ms. **Step 1 — Total end-to-end delay:** $$D_{e2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} d_i = 12 + 15 + 10 + 18 + 8$$ **Step 2 — Arithmetic evaluation:** $$D_{e2e} = 27 + 10 + 18 + 8 = 37 + 26 = 63 \; \text{ms}$$ **Step 3 — Average delay per hop:** $$\bar{d} = \frac{D_{e2e}}{H} = \frac{63}{5} = 12.6 \; \text{ms per hop}$$ **Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:** The average per-hop delay of 12.6 ms is well above the 1 ms ideal, indicating congestion or retransmissions at intermediate nodes. Optimization techniques include shortest-path routing, congestion-aware metrics, and load balancing. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Always include the **units (ms)** in the final answer. A correct numerical value without the unit is penalized **0.5 marks** in KTU valuation. Also, when sketching the block diagram, use clear arrow labels and avoid cross-over lines. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 — Module 2 Rapid Revision Checklist** - **Definition:** Ad hoc routing is the multi-hop, infrastructure-less path selection process in MANETs. - **Why Traditional Fails:** Dynamic topology, no central router, asymmetric links, resource constraints, scalability, broadcast storm. - **Three Major Categories:** Proactive (DSDV), Reactive (AODV, DSR), Hybrid (ZRP). - **Operational Phases:** Neighbor Discovery → Route Discovery → Route Maintenance. - **Source vs Hop-by-Hop:** DSR uses source routing; AODV uses hop-by-hop. - **PDR formula:** $\text{PDR} = p^{2H} \times 100\%$ for symmetric reactive protocols. - **End-to-End Delay:** $D_{e2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{H} (d_{q,i} + d_{t,i} + d_{p,i} + d_{pr,i})$. - **Proactive Pros:** Low latency, route always available. - **Proactive Cons:** High control overhead, large tables, high power drain. - **Reactive Pros:** Low overhead when idle, scalable. - **Reactive Cons:** High first-packet delay, route discovery latency. - **Hybrid Trade-off:** Tunable via zone radius — too small = behaves reactively; too large = behaves proactively. - **Key Challenges:** Mobility, bandwidth, energy, hidden/exposed terminals, asymmetry, security, scalability, broadcast storm. - **Engineering Applications:** Battlefield, disaster recovery, VANET, IoT/WSN, underwater, pocket-switched. - **Common Exam Keywords:** Hop count, convergence, PDR, NRL, throughput, jitter, hello mechanism, RREQ, RREP, RERR.

topics.title: Proactive, Reactive, and Hybrid Routing Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT863 - ADHOC AND WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Proactive, Reactive, and Hybrid Routing Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Routing in Ad-hoc Wireless Networks

In **Mobile Ad-hoc Networks (MANETs)**, there is no fixed infrastructure such as routers, base stations, or access points. Every node acts both as a host (end-system) and a router (forwarding device). Because of this dual role, every node must participate in the **routing process** to deliver packets from a source to a destination across a multi-hop wireless topology that changes continuously due to node mobility.

A **Routing Protocol** in this context is the set of rules, message exchanges, and algorithms used by nodes to discover, maintain, and select paths across the dynamic ad-hoc topology. KTU Module 2 classifies these protocols into three broad families based on *when* and *how* routing information is acquired.

### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proactive Routing Protocol (Table-Driven)**
> A routing approach in which every node continuously maintains **up-to-date routes to ALL possible destinations** in the network by periodically exchanging routing tables, regardless of whether a particular route is currently needed. Examples: **DSDV**, **OLSR**, **TBRPF**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reactive Routing Protocol (On-Demand)**
> A routing approach in which a node discovers and maintains a route to a destination **only when it actually has data to send**. Route discovery is invoked on demand and routes are kept alive as long as the data session requires them. Examples: **AODV**, **DSR**, **TORA**, **ABR**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid Routing Protocol**
> A routing approach that combines the **proactive maintenance of routes within a local neighbourhood/zone** with the **reactive discovery of routes across larger network regions**, thereby balancing control overhead and route acquisition latency. Examples: **ZRP**, **CBRP**, **ZHLS**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of three different tourist navigation systems for a city:

- **Proactive** is like a taxi driver who keeps a *complete printed map of the entire country* in his glove box. He knows every road, every shortcut, every closure *right now*. He wastes ink and paper updating it daily, but the moment you ask for a route, he answers instantly.

- **Reactive** is like a backpacker who carries *no map at all*. The instant he decides to go somewhere, he pulls over and starts asking locals: "Excuse me, how do I get to Kochi?" He uses little ink, but every journey begins with a delay.

- **Hybrid** is like a regional guide who carries a *detailed map of his own city* (where he works proactively) but, for destinations outside his city, he *calls colleagues in other cities* and asks on demand. Best of both worlds.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold-Formatted Standard Metrics)

The KTU syllabus evaluates these three protocol families on the following standard performance metrics:

- **Route Acquisition Latency** — Time delay before a usable route is obtained.
- **Control Overhead** — Number of routing packets transmitted per data byte delivered.
- **Bandwidth Consumption** — Radio bandwidth consumed by routing traffic.
- **Storage Cost** — Memory required to store routing tables.
- **Route Optimality** — Closeness of chosen path to shortest possible path.
- **Scalability** — Ability to maintain performance as the number of nodes grows.
- **Mobility Adaptability** — Robustness under high node-speed conditions.
- **Power Consumption** — Energy expended in routing-related transmissions.

### 1.5 Classification Overview

> [!NOTE]
> KTU classifies ad-hoc routing protocols along two primary axes:
> 1. **Topology-based vs. Position-based** (covered separately in Module 2).
> 2. **Time of route discovery** — Proactive (before need) vs. Reactive (on need) vs. Hybrid (mixed).

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Proactive (Table-Driven) Routing Protocols — Operational Logic

The proactive family follows a *push* model of routing information. The following structured sequence describes the operational flow:

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Each node constructs a routing table entry for every other node in the network, typically setting the cost (hop count or metric) to infinity for unknown destinations.
- **Step 2 — Periodic Exchange:** At fixed intervals $T_{hello}$ (e.g., every 1 to 15 seconds), each node broadcasts a routing update message to its neighbours. The update may be a *full dump* (entire table) or a *differential/incremental* update (only changed entries).
- **Step 3 — Triggered Updates:** In addition to periodic updates, topology-change events (link break, new neighbour) trigger an *event-driven* update to propagate information faster.
- **Step 4 — Route Computation:** Each node independently runs a shortest-path algorithm (Bellman-Ford for distance-vector, Dijkstra for link-state) on the topology it has learned.
- **Step 5 — Forwarding:** When a packet must be sent, the node consults its already-populated table — *zero route-acquisition latency*.

**Why it works:** Because tables are continuously refreshed, any packet can be forwarded immediately. **How it costs:** The radio channel is consumed by constant updates even when no user data flows.

### 2.2 Reactive (On-Demand) Routing Protocols — Operational Logic

The reactive family follows a *pull* model. The structured sequence is:

- **Step 1 — Route Cache State:** Initially, every node's route cache is empty (or contains only previously used routes that may now be stale).
- **Step 2 — Route Request (RREQ):** When a source $S$ needs a route to destination $D$, it floods a *Route Request* packet across the network. Each intermediate node rebroadcasts the RREQ exactly once.
- **Step 3 — Route Reply (RREP):** When the RREQ reaches $D$ (or an intermediate node with a fresh route to $D$), that node unicasts a *Route Reply* back along the reverse path recorded in the RREQ.
- **Step 4 — Route Maintenance:** As long as the session is active, intermediate nodes monitor the link. A link break triggers a *Route Error* (RERR) message to inform the source.
- **Step 5 — Route Repair or Rediscovery:** The source may attempt local repair, initiate a new RREQ, or simply drop the session.

**Why it works:** The network only spends energy on routing for sessions that actually exist. **How it costs:** The first packet of every new session waits for discovery — a measurable latency penalty.

### 2.3 Hybrid Routing Protocols — Operational Logic

Hybrid protocols exploit the observation that *most traffic is local* (a well-documented empirical property of MANETs). The structure is:

- **Step 1 — Zone/Cluster Definition:** Each node defines a *local neighbourhood* of radius $r$ (in hops) — its proactive zone.
- **Step 2 — Intra-Zone Proactive Maintenance:** Inside the zone, routes are kept up-to-date exactly like a proactive protocol.
- **Step 3 — Inter-Zone Reactive Discovery:** For destinations outside the zone, the protocol reverts to an on-demand RREQ/RREP exchange.
- **Step 4 — Bordercasting:** RREQs are not blindly flooded; they are sent only to *peripheral nodes* (nodes at the zone boundary), a technique called bordercasting.
- **Step 5 — Route Caching:** Successfully used inter-zone routes are cached to accelerate future requests.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the analytical expressions commonly examined in KTU 2024 ESE for this topic. All symbols are formally defined inside the same table cell.

| # | Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Boundary / Typical Value | Engineering Unit |
|---|----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Number of proactive control packets per node per unit time | $C_{pro} = \dfrac{N \cdot k}{T_{hello}}$ | $N$ = total nodes; $k$ = table-update size factor | packets/sec |
| 2 | Total proactive network overhead | $O_{pro} = N \cdot C_{pro} = \dfrac{N^2 \cdot k}{T_{hello}}$ | Scales as $O(N^2)$ | packets/sec |
| 3 | RREQ flooding overhead (reactive) | $O_{req} = N - 1$ (worst-case flooded nodes) | Bounded by $N$ | packets |
| 4 | Reactive route-acquisition latency | $L_{req} = 2 \cdot d_{hops} \cdot t_{prop} + t_{proc}$ | $d_{hops}$ = hop count of discovered path; $t_{prop}$ = per-hop propagation; $t_{proc}$ = processing delay | seconds |
| 5 | Proactive route-acquisition latency | $L_{pro} \approx t_{lookup}$ (table read time) | $L_{pro} \ll L_{req}$ | seconds |
| 6 | Zone radius (hybrid) | $r = $ constant chosen by protocol designer (e.g., ZRP default $= 2$ hops) | Trade-off knob | hops |
| 7 | Intra-zone node count | $N_{zone} \approx \rho \cdot \pi r^2$ | $\rho$ = node density (nodes per unit area) | nodes |
| 8 | Effective overhead ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{ctrl}}{C_{ctrl} + C_{data}}$ | Lower is better | dimensionless |
| 9 | Average end-to-end delay (E2E) | $D_{E2E} = \sum_{i=1}^{d_{hops}} \left(t_{prop,i} + t_{trans,i} + t_{queue,i}\right)$ | Sum across every hop $i$ | seconds |
| 10 | Throughput degradation factor | $T_{deg} = 1 - \eta$ | $0 \le T_{deg} \le 1$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table-Cell Pipe Rule:** Throughout this note, vertical bar symbols inside table cells are rendered using the LaTeX constructs `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Proactive protocols** are deployed in **tactical military mesh networks** (e.g., soldier-to-soldier communication) where every second of latency is critical and bandwidth is reserved.
- **Reactive protocols** are favoured in **disaster-recovery ad-hoc deployments** where the network is sparse, traffic is bursty, and energy conservation is paramount (e.g., battlefield rescue robots, IoT-style emergency mesh).
- **Hybrid protocols** are chosen for **vehicular ad-hoc networks (VANETs)** and **community Wi-Fi meshes** where traffic is geographically clustered and node density is high.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is the single most-tested comparison in KTU 2024 ESE for this topic. Memorize the columns; they appear verbatim in past university question papers.

| Property | Proactive (DSDV, OLSR) | Reactive (AODV, DSR) | Hybrid (ZRP, CBRP) |
|----------|------------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Route availability | Always available | Only after discovery | Intra-zone immediate, inter-zone on-demand |
| Routing philosophy | Table-driven, periodic | On-demand, source-initiated | Zone-based, mixed |
| Control overhead | High (continuous updates) | Low (only when needed) | Medium (proportional to zone size) |
| Latency for first packet | Very low (lookup only) | High (RREQ/RREP round trip) | Low for local, high for remote |
| Storage requirement | High (full network table) | Low (only active routes cached) | Medium (zone table + cache) |
| Scalability | Poor in large networks | Good in large, sparse networks | Good in clustered networks |
| Bandwidth efficiency | Low (idle updates waste BW) | High (no idle updates) | Medium |
| Power consumption | High (always on transmitter) | Low (transmit only when needed) | Medium |
| Mobility handling | Periodic updates mask mobility | Reactive rediscovery handles mobility well | Handles mobility within zone proactively |
| Loop-free guarantee | Yes (sequence numbers / link state) | Yes (sequence numbers) | Yes (depends on sub-protocol) |
| Best suited for | Small, high-traffic MANETs | Large, low-traffic MANETs | Hierarchical / clustered MANETs |
| Example protocol | DSDV, OLSR, TBRPF | AODV, DSR, TORA, ABR | ZRP, CBRP, ZHLS |

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Proactive Overhead Scaling

**Problem (KTU-style):** A MANET contains $N$ nodes. Each node broadcasts a routing update every $T_{hello}$ seconds, and the average update packet contains $k$ entries per neighbour. Derive the total control-packet rate generated by the network.

**Step 1 — Per-Node Rate:** A single node generates one update packet every $T_{hello}$ seconds.

$$
R_{node} = \frac{1}{T_{hello}}
$$

This is the per-node packet generation frequency in units of packets per second.

**Step 2 — Per-Node Bandwidth Cost:** Each packet carries $k$ routing entries; therefore, in a small interval $\Delta t$, the node contributes $k$ entries of routing information.

**Step 3 — Total Network Rate:** With $N$ independent nodes each broadcasting, the total control-packet rate across the network is the linear superposition of all per-node rates.

$$
R_{total} = N \cdot R_{node} = N \cdot \frac{1}{T_{hello}} = \frac{N}{T_{hello}}
$$

**Step 4 — Quadratic Scaling Identification:** Because the update must, in the worst case, propagate to all other nodes (via flooding or link-state dissemination), the **information propagation cost** scales as $N(N-1) \approx N^2$.

$$
O_{pro} = \frac{N^2 \cdot k}{T_{hello}}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:** Doubling the number of nodes quadruples the proactive overhead. This is the fundamental scalability bottleneck that motivates reactive and hybrid designs.

### 3.3 Worked Derivation — Reactive Latency Budget

**Problem (KTU-style):** A reactive protocol (AODV) discovers a route from $S$ to $D$ across a path of $d_{hops} = 6$ hops. Each hop introduces propagation delay $t_{prop} = 2$ ms and processing delay $t_{proc} = 1$ ms. Transmission delay per hop is $t_{trans} = 0.5$ ms. Compute the total route-acquisition latency for the RREQ to reach $D$ and the RREP to return to $S$.

**Step 1 — Forward Path Delay (RREQ from $S$ to $D$):** The RREQ traverses all $d_{hops}$ links, and at each intermediate node it incurs the per-hop delay.

$$
D_{fwd} = d_{hops} \cdot (t_{prop} + t_{trans} + t_{proc})
$$

Substitute the numerical values.

$$
D_{fwd} = 6 \cdot (2 + 0.5 + 1) = 6 \cdot 3.5 = 21 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Reverse Path Delay (RREP from $D$ back to $S$):** The RREP traverses the same number of hops in the opposite direction.

$$
D_{rev} = d_{hops} \cdot (t_{prop} + t_{trans} + t_{proc}) = 6 \cdot 3.5 = 21 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Route-Acquisition Latency:** Sum the two directional components.

$$
L_{req} = D_{fwd} + D_{rev} = 21 + 21 = 42 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:** A 42 ms acquisition delay is the price paid *once* per session. Subsequent packets incur only the per-packet forwarding delay (e.g., $D_{fwd} = 21$ ms for a data packet, or half of $L_{req}$).

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Tree for Protocol Selection

A KTU examiner may ask: *"When would you choose proactive over reactive?"* The decision flow:

- **If the network size $N$ is small (e.g., $N \le 20$):** Proactive is acceptable because $N^2$ overhead remains bounded.
- **If node mobility is high (frequent topology changes):** Proactive's continuous updates may still outperform reactive's repeated rediscovery.
- **If traffic is bursty with long idle intervals:** Reactive wins by conserving energy during idle periods.
- **If traffic is geographically clustered:** Hybrid wins because intra-cluster proactive maintenance is cheap, and inter-cluster reactive discovery is rare.
- **If latency on the first packet is critical (e.g., real-time voice):** Proactive wins; reactive must be avoided.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q:** Differentiate between proactive and reactive routing protocols in MANETs. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Proactive (table-driven) protocols maintain routing information to *all* destinations continuously, exchanging periodic updates among all nodes. This results in high control overhead but **zero route-acquisition latency** because routes are pre-computed. Example: **DSDV** (Destination-Sequenced Distance-Vector).

Reactive (on-demand) protocols create and maintain a route to a destination *only when a source has data to send*, using RREQ/RREP exchanges. This results in low control overhead but introduces a measurable **route-acquisition latency** for the first packet. Example: **AODV** (Ad-hoc On-demand Distance Vector).

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q:** What is a hybrid routing protocol? Name two hybrid protocols and state one key design idea used by them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A hybrid routing protocol combines the proactive maintenance of routes in a local neighbourhood with the reactive discovery of routes to remote destinations, balancing the control overhead of proactive protocols and the latency of reactive protocols. Two examples are **ZRP (Zone Routing Protocol)** and **CBRP (Cluster Based Routing Protocol)**. The key design idea is the use of a *zone* (or *cluster*) of radius $r$ hops, within which routes are kept proactively up-to-date, and across which routes are discovered reactively using a *bordercasting* mechanism.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> (a) Explain the architecture and operation of the DSDV proactive routing protocol with a suitable example. State how it avoids the count-to-infinity problem. **(7 marks)**
> (b) Describe the route discovery and route maintenance procedures of the AODV reactive routing protocol with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**DSDV Architecture:** Destination-Sequenced Distance-Vector (DSDV) is a table-driven proactive protocol in which every node maintains a routing table with one entry per known destination. Each entry contains:
- Destination address
- Number of hops (metric)
- Next-hop address
- Sequence number (issued by the destination)
- Installation time

**Operation:** Each mobile node periodically advertises its routing table to its immediate neighbours. Updates may be *full dump* (entire table) or *incremental* (only entries whose metric has changed). Two types of routes exist in the table: *stable* (infrequently changing) and *volatile* (frequently changing).

**Avoidance of Count-to-Infinity:** DSDV assigns a monotonically increasing *sequence number* to every entry, originated by the destination itself. When two routes to the same destination are compared, the route with the **higher sequence number is always preferred**, regardless of hop count. If the sequence numbers are equal, the route with the **lower metric is preferred**. This ensures that stale information can never overwrite fresh information, eliminating the count-to-infinity pathology of classical distance-vector protocols.

**Sketch of Routing Table Update Flow:** Suppose node A wants to reach node D. A's neighbour B advertises a route (D, metric=2, seq=10, next-hop=B). If A receives a later advertisement (D, metric=3, seq=12, next-hop=C), A accepts the second one because seq=12 > seq=10. The higher sequence number overrides even a shorter path.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**AODV Route Discovery:** When a source $S$ needs a route to destination $D$ and has no fresh entry in its route table, it broadcasts a **RREQ (Route Request)** packet containing: source address, destination address, broadcast ID, source sequence number, destination sequence number, and hop count. Each intermediate node that receives the RREQ:
1. Records a *reverse route* to $S$ (used later to send RREP back).
2. Increments the hop count.
3. Rebroadcasts the RREQ if it does not already know a fresh route to $D$.

When the RREQ reaches $D$ (or a node with a fresh enough route), that node generates a **RREP (Route Reply)** containing its current sequence number for $D$, the hop count, and lifetime. The RREP is unicast back along the reverse path, and intermediate nodes install a *forward route* to $D$ as the RREP traverses them.

**AODV Route Maintenance:** Each node monitors the link to its next-hop neighbour using periodic *Hello* messages (a special RREP with TTL=1). If a node fails to receive Hello messages from a neighbour for a threshold period, it declares the link broken. The node then generates a **RERR (Route Error)** message listing all destinations that become unreachable due to the break, and the RERR is propagated to active upstream neighbours. The source, upon receiving the RERR, may re-initiate route discovery or locally repair the route.

**Sketch:**

```
S --RREQ--> N1 --RREQ--> N2 --RREQ--> D
D --RREP--> N2 --RREP--> N1 --RREP--> S
N1 --Hello--N2   (link monitoring)
N1 --RERR--> S   (on link break)
```

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**
> **Question B (14 Marks):**
> (a) With a neat diagram, explain the Zone Routing Protocol (ZRP) architecture. How does it achieve a balance between proactive and reactive paradigms? **(7 marks)**
> (b) Compare the performance of DSDV, AODV, and ZRP under the following metrics: control overhead, end-to-end delay, throughput, and mobility handling. Tabulate your answer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**ZRP Architecture:** ZRP divides the network into overlapping *zones* of radius $r$ hops, where $r$ is a design parameter (commonly $r = 2$). Each node $S$ is the *centre* of its own zone, and the zone contains all nodes within $r$ hops of $S$. ZRP consists of three sub-protocols:
- **IARP (Intrazone Routing Protocol):** Proactive; maintains routes to all nodes inside the zone.
- **IERP (Interzone Routing Protocol):** Reactive; discovers routes to nodes outside the zone using bordercasting.
- **BRP (Bordercast Resolution Protocol):** A mechanism that delivers RREQs to *peripheral nodes* (zone boundary nodes) rather than flooding to all nodes.

**Operational Flow:** For a destination $D$:
1. If $D$ lies inside $S$'s zone, $S$ uses the proactive IARP table to forward the packet directly.
2. If $D$ lies outside $S$'s zone, $S$ uses IERP to bordercast an RREQ to its peripheral nodes. Each peripheral node checks its own IARP table; if $D$ is in its zone, it responds with an RREP, otherwise it propagates the RREQ outward to its own peripheral nodes.
3. The RREP travels back, establishing a route that is cached for future use.

**Balance Achieved:** Within the zone (where most traffic is local), routes are *immediately available* (proactive benefit). Across zones (where traffic is less frequent), routes are discovered *on demand* (reactive benefit), avoiding the cost of maintaining long-distance routes continuously.

**Diagram (already shown in Section 4.3):** Node $S$ has zone $\{S, N1, N2, N3, N4\}$. Destination $D$ is outside. $S$ bordercasts RREQ to peripheral node $N4$, which queries its IARP and replies.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Performance Comparison Table:**

| Metric | DSDV (Proactive) | AODV (Reactive) | ZRP (Hybrid) |
|--------|------------------|-----------------|--------------|
| **Control Overhead** | High — continuous periodic updates, $O(N^2)$ scaling | Low — only on demand, scales with traffic | Medium — proportional to zone radius $r$ and traffic |
| **End-to-End Delay** | Low first-packet delay (table lookup) | High first-packet delay (RREQ/RREP); low subsequent | Low for intra-zone, high for inter-zone first packet |
| **Throughput** | High for stable topologies; degrades with high mobility | Lower under bursty traffic; better under high mobility | Balanced; competitive in clustered topologies |
| **Mobility Handling** | Periodic updates mask changes; triggered updates add cost | Rediscovery absorbs mobility gracefully | Intra-zone proactive absorbs local mobility; inter-zone rediscovery for remote |
| **Storage Cost** | High (full table) | Low (active routes only) | Medium (zone table + cache) |
| **Scalability** | Poor for large $N$ | Good for large $N$ | Good for clustered/large $N$ |
| **Power Consumption** | High | Low | Medium |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) confusing *DSDV* (proactive) with *DSR* (reactive) — the names look similar but the philosophies are opposite; (2) writing "ZRP is proactive" or "ZRP is reactive" — ZRP is *hybrid*, and the answer must explicitly state the intra-zone proactive / inter-zone reactive split; (3) omitting the *sequence number* mechanism when explaining count-to-infinity avoidance in DSDV; (4) forgetting to mention *bordercasting* in ZRP and writing a generic RREQ/RREP description.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Three families** of ad-hoc routing protocols: Proactive, Reactive, Hybrid — classified by *when* routes are acquired.
- **Proactive** = continuous updates, zero latency, high overhead, $O(N^2)$ scaling. Examples: **DSDV, OLSR, TBRPF**.
- **Reactive** = on-demand discovery, first-packet latency, low overhead, scales with traffic. Examples: **AODV, DSR, TORA, ABR**.
- **Hybrid** = proactive inside zone, reactive across zones, balance overhead vs. latency. Examples: **ZRP, CBRP, ZHLS**.
- **DSDV uses sequence numbers** to avoid count-to-infinity — the foundation of proactive correctness.
- **AODV uses RREQ/RREP/RERR** as its three core control messages and Hello messages for link monitoring.
- **DSR uses source routing** — the entire path is included in the packet header (memory vs. state trade-off).
- **ZRP uses a zone radius $r$** (commonly 2 hops) and **bordercasting** to limit RREQ propagation.
- **CBRP clusters** the network and uses cluster heads for hierarchical routing.
- **The control-overhead formula for proactive protocols** is $O_{pro} = \dfrac{N^2 \cdot k}{T_{hello}}$ — a derivation frequently tested.
- **The reactive latency formula** is $L_{req} = 2 \cdot d_{hops} \cdot (t_{prop} + t_{trans} + t_{proc})$ — also testable.
- **Memorize the comparison matrix** in Section 3.1 — it is the single most reused KTU 2024 answer template for this topic.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** Small $N$ + low latency need $\rightarrow$ Proactive. Large $N$ + bursty traffic $\rightarrow$ Reactive. Clustered topology + mixed traffic $\rightarrow$ Hybrid.
- **Energy implication:** Proactive drains battery fastest; Reactive is the most energy-efficient; Hybrid is in between.
- **Loop-freeness** is guaranteed in all three families through different mechanisms — sequence numbers (DSDV/AODV), source routing (DSR), or DAGs (TORA).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AODV, OLSR, and TORA Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT863 - ADHOC AND WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** AODV, OLSR, and TORA Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to MANET Routing Protocols

In Mobile Ad Hoc Networks (MANETs), there is **no fixed infrastructure** (no routers, no base stations). Every node acts as both a **host** and a **router**, forwarding packets for other nodes. Because the topology changes dynamically, routing is the hardest problem in MANETs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Routing protocols in MANETs are classified based on how and when routes are established. They are categorized as **Proactive (Table-Driven)**, **Reactive (On-Demand)**, and **Hybrid** protocols. AODV and TORA are **Reactive**, while OLSR is **Proactive**.*

## 1.2 The Three Protocols at a Glance

| Protocol | Full Name | Type | Standard | Key Idea |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AODV** | Ad hoc On-demand Distance Vector | Reactive | RFC 3561 | Discovers route only when needed |
| **OLSR** | Optimized Link State Routing | Proactive | RFC 3626 | Maintains tables continuously using MPRs |
| **TORA** | Temporally Ordered Routing Algorithm | Reactive | RFC draft (ietf-manet-tora) | Uses link reversal, creates DAG |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Three Taxi Drivers"**
>
> - **AODV** is like a taxi driver who **doesn't know the city map**. He only asks for directions (broadcasts a "RREQ" – *where is the airport?*) when a passenger wants to go somewhere, then memorizes the returned path.
>
> - **OLSR** is like a taxi driver who **has the entire city map updated every minute** on his dashboard. He knows every route always, but wastes fuel (bandwidth) keeping it fresh.
>
> - **TORA** is like a taxi driver who **follows downhill water flow** toward the destination. If a road is blocked, water finds a new downhill path automatically — this is *link reversal*.

## 1.4 Why These Three?

KTU specifically tests these three because they represent **three fundamentally different design philosophies**:
1. **AODV** → Sequence-numbered distance vector
2. **OLSR** → Optimized link-state flooding
3. **TORA** → Reversible graph-based, multi-path

Each is a 14-mark favorite in KTU End Semester Examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 AODV — Ad Hoc On-Demand Distance Vector

### 2.1.1 Core Concept

AODV is a **reactive protocol** — it does **not** maintain a route to every destination. Routes are created **only when a source wants to send data**. AODV uses **destination sequence numbers** to guarantee loop-free paths and to determine the *freshness* of routing information.

### 2.1.2 Routing Tables in AODV

Each AODV node maintains a routing table with the following fields:

- **Destination IP Address**
- **Destination Sequence Number**
- **Valid Destination Sequence Number Flag** (a boolean)
- **Network Interface**
- **Hop Count** (number of hops to destination)
- **Next Hop** (the IP of the relay)
- **Precursor List** (neighbors using this route)
- **Lifetime** (expiry timer in ms, default = 3000 ms)

### 2.1.3 Three Control Message Types

| Message | Purpose | Key Fields |
|---|---|---|
| **RREQ** (Route Request) | Initiates route discovery | Type, J, R, G, D, U, Reserved, Hop Count, RREQ ID, Destination IP, Dest Seq No, Originator IP, Originator Seq No |
| **RREP** (Route Reply) | Confirms route back to source | Type, R, A, Reserved, Prefix Size, Hop Count, Dest IP, Dest Seq No, Originator IP, Lifetime |
| **RERR** (Route Error) | Reports link breakage | Type, N, Reserved, DestCount, Unreachable Dest IP + Dest Seq No pairs |

### 2.1.4 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

**Step 1 — Route Discovery (when source S wants to send to D):**
- S checks its routing table. If a fresh route exists (Dest Seq No of route ≥ Last known Dest Seq No of D), S forwards data immediately.
- If no fresh route, S creates a **RREQ** packet and **broadcasts** it.

**Step 2 — RREQ Forwarding:**
- Intermediate nodes update their routing table (reverse path to S, with Originator Seq No).
- They check if they have a fresh route to D. If yes, they send **RREP** back to S via the reverse path. If no, they rebroadcast the RREQ with incremented hop count.

**Step 3 — RREP Propagation:**
- D (or any node with a fresh route) unicasts **RREP** back to S along the reverse path.
- Each intermediate node updates the forward route (toward D) with the **Dest Seq No** and **Lifetime**.

**Step 4 — Data Transmission:**
- S starts sending data using the established forward route.

**Step 5 — Route Maintenance:**
- Each node periodically sends **HELLO** messages (a special RREP with TTL=1) to its active neighbors.
- If a node does not receive 3 consecutive HELLO messages (HELLO_INTERVAL = 1000 ms, ALLOWED_HELLO_LOSS = 3), the link is considered broken.
- A **RERR** is generated and sent to all **precursors** (active upstream neighbors).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Sequence Numbers?** Distance Vector algorithms suffer from the *count-to-infinity* problem. Sequence numbers act like a *timestamp* — a node always prefers a route with a **higher** sequence number, ensuring loop-freedom.

### 2.1.5 AODV — Pros and Cons

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Low control overhead when traffic is sparse | High latency for first packet (route discovery delay) |
| Loop-free (due to sequence numbers) | Scalability issues in large networks (flooding of RREQ) |
| Scales well in mobile scenarios | Only one route per destination (no multipath) |

---

## 2.2 OLSR — Optimized Link State Routing

### 2.2.1 Core Concept

OLSR is a **proactive / table-driven** protocol. Every node **continuously** maintains a routing table to **all** destinations in the network. The "optimization" lies in reducing flooding overhead using **Multipoint Relays (MPRs)**.

### 2.2.2 Two Key Message Types

| Message | Frequency | Purpose | TTL |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HELLO** | Every HELLO_INTERVAL (default 2 s) | Neighbor discovery + MPR selection | 1 (local only) |
| **TC** (Topology Control) | Every TC_INTERVAL (default 5 s) | Distribute topology using MPRs | Network-wide |

### 2.2.3 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

**Step 1 — Neighbor Sensing (HELLO):**
- Every node broadcasts **HELLO** messages with TTL=1 (so they are not forwarded).
- HELLO contains: *own address, list of neighbors, list of MPR selectors*.
- Based on received HELLOs, each node classifies neighbors into:
  - **Symmetric** (bidirectional link confirmed)
  - **Asymmetric** (only heard)
  - **MPR** (chosen relays)

**Step 2 — MPR Selection (the key optimization):**
- A node selects a **subset** of its 1-hop symmetric neighbors as **MPRs**.
- Rule: Every 2-hop neighbor must be reachable via at least one selected MPR.
- Goal: **Minimize the number of MPRs** to reduce TC flooding.

**Step 3 — Topology Dissemination (TC):**
- An **MPR node** periodically broadcasts **TC messages** announcing its **MPR Selector set** (the nodes that chose it as MPR).
- TC messages are **flooded throughout the network**, but **only MPR nodes retransmit** them.

**Step 4 — Route Calculation:**
- Each node now has full topology (from TC messages) + link state.
- Uses **Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm** to compute routes to all destinations.
- Routing table is updated continuously.

### 2.2.4 MPR Selection Algorithm — Step-by-Step

1. Start with N = set of 2-hop neighbors not reachable via symmetric 1-hop neighbors.
2. For each 1-hop neighbor *x*, compute its *reachability* = number of nodes in N covered by *x*.
3. Select the node with the **highest reachability** as MPR. Add all its 2-hop neighbors to covered set.
4. Remove covered nodes from N.
5. Repeat until N is empty.

### 2.2.5 OLSR — Pros and Cons

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Low latency (route is always ready) | High control overhead even when no traffic |
| Loop-free (link-state nature) | Requires significant memory and CPU (Dijkstra) |
| Multipoint Relay reduces flooding | MPR selection is non-trivial |

---

## 2.3 TORA — Temporally Ordered Routing Algorithm

### 2.3.1 Core Concept

TORA is a **reactive, source-initiated, multi-path** protocol designed for **highly dynamic** mobile networks. It uses a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** rooted at the destination. The destination has **height = 0**, and all other nodes have a *height* greater than the minimum of their neighbors. Routes flow *downhill* toward the destination.

### 2.3.2 The Five Routing States of a Node (Height Levels)

A node can be in one of five heights, and these are modified during link reversal:

1. **Undetected** — Initial state, no route.
2. **Established** — Has a valid path (height level 0, 1, 2, ...).
3. **Listening — Reflected** — Lost last downstream link, waiting for CLR.
4. **Listening — Not Reflected** — Lost last downstream link, will reflect UPD.
5. **Lost** — No route to destination.

### 2.3.3 Three Control Packets

| Packet | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **QRY** (Query) | Downstream → Upstream | "Do you have a route to D?" |
| **UPD** (Update) | Upstream → Downstream | Contains new height reference |
| **CLR** (Clear) | Any | Erases invalid routes after partition |

### 2.3.4 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

**Step 1 — Route Creation:**
- Source S needs a route to D. S broadcasts a **QRY** with the destination's ID.
- QRY propagates outward. When D (or a node with a height to D) is reached, a **UPD** is generated containing the destination's reference level + offset.

**Step 2 — DAG Construction:**
- Heights are assigned such that they decrease monotonically from any node to the destination.
- This forms a **DAG** rooted at D.

**Step 3 — Route Maintenance (Link Reversal):**
- When a link between A and B breaks:
  - The upstream node (A) sets its height to be **higher** than all its remaining neighbors.
  - This is called **partial / full link reversal**.
- This may cascade — adjacent upstream nodes also reverse.
- Eventually, a new DAG rooted at D is formed.

**Step 4 — Route Erasure (when partition occurs):**
- If a node becomes *Lost*, it broadcasts a **CLR** to clear all invalid routes in the partition.

### 2.3.5 TORA — Pros and Cons

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Multiple loop-free paths (multipath) | High control overhead during link reversals |
| Fast reaction to topology changes | Not scalable (each node maintains 5 state variables) |
| Supports partition detection (CLR) | Possible oscillations if not synchronized |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Default Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AODV HELLO Interval | $T_{HELLO}$ | 1000 ms |
| 2 | AODV Allowed Hello Loss | $N_{loss}$ | 3 packets |
| 3 | AODV Route Lifetime | $L_{route}$ | 3000 ms |
| 4 | AODV Path Selection | $R_{selected} = \arg\max_{R_i}\left(\text{SeqNo}(D, R_i)\right)$ | — |
| 5 | OLSR HELLO Interval | $T_{H}^{OLSR}$ | 2 s |
| 6 | OLSR TC Interval | $T_{TC}^{OLSR}$ | 5 s |
| 7 | OLSR Reachability | $R(x) = \vert N_2 \cap \text{coverage}(x) \vert$ | nodes covered |
| 8 | OLSR Route Cost (Dijkstra) | $C(d) = \min_{j \in N(i)}\left[ C(j) + w_{ij} \right]$ | hops |
| 9 | TORA Height Tuple | $H_i = (\tau_i, oid_i, r_i, \delta_i)$ | integer levels |
| 10 | TORA Link Reversal Condition | If no downstream $\Rightarrow$ reverse links | — |
| 11 | MANET Bandwidth Model | $BW_{eff} = BW_{raw} \cdot (1 - \text{Overhead Factor})$ | bps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** When asked to compare, always use a **tabular comparison** (pros, cons, type, message types). This is the valuation scheme. Always mention **sequence numbers** for AODV, **MPRs** for OLSR, and **link reversal** for TORA — these three keywords guarantee marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 AODV — Detailed Route Discovery Example

Consider a MANET with 5 nodes: **S (source), A, B, C, D (destination)**.

**Topology:**
- S — A (link)
- S — B (link)
- A — C (link)
- B — C (link)
- C — D (link)

S has no route to D. We trace the protocol step-by-step.

### Step 1: Source Creates RREQ

S creates an RREQ packet:
