# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR) and explain its significance in evaluating MANET routing protocols.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** is defined as the ratio of the total number of data packets successfully delivered to the destination to the total number of data packets sent by the source, expressed as a percentage.

**Formula:**
$$PDR = \dfrac{P_{rcvd}}{P_{sent}} \times 100\,\%$$

**Significance:**
1. It measures the **reliability** of a routing protocol in delivering data despite topology changes, link breakages, and node mobility.
2. A high PDR indicates the protocol's route maintenance and recovery mechanisms are effective.
3. It is the most widely reported metric in MANET research (along with delay) and is mandatory in KTU-evaluated performance comparison studies.
4. Protocols like AODV and DSR typically achieve higher PDR under high mobility compared to DSDV.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - July 2023, Supplementary Exam]`
**Differentiate between Proactive and Reactive routing protocols with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Proactive (Table-Driven) | Reactive (On-Demand) |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Route Discovery | Maintained continuously | Initiated only when needed |
| Latency | Low (route ready) | High (route discovery delay) |
| Control Overhead | High (periodic updates) | Low (only on demand) |
| Bandwidth Usage | Wastes bandwidth on updates | Conserves bandwidth |
| Suitability | Low mobility networks | High mobility networks |
| Example 1 | DSDV | AODV |
| Example 2 | OLSR | DSR |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain any **five major performance metrics** used for evaluating MANET routing protocols with their mathematical formulas. Discuss how mobility affects each metric. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The five major performance metrics used in MANET evaluation are:

**1. Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR):**
$$PDR = \dfrac{P_{rcvd}}{P_{sent}} \times 100\,\%$$
PDR generally **decreases** with increasing mobility because link breakages become more frequent, and the routing protocol may take time to discover alternative paths.

**2. Average End-to-End Delay:**
$$D_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{recv,i} - T_{sent,i})$$
Delay **increases** with mobility due to frequent route discoveries, MAC-layer retransmissions, and queue buildup at intermediate nodes.

**3. Throughput (kbps):**
$$Th = \dfrac{N_{rcvd} \times L \times 8}{T_{sim} \times 1000}$$
Throughput **drops** at high mobility because PDR drops and channel contention increases.

**4. Normalized Routing Load (NRL):**
$$NRL = \dfrac{P_{routing}}{P_{delivered}}$$
NRL **increases** with mobility in reactive protocols (more RREQ/RERR packets) but remains high in proactive protocols regardless.

**5. Routing Overhead (bytes):**
$$OH = \sum_{i=1}^{M} C_i$$
Overhead grows with both mobility and network size, as more control packets must be exchanged to maintain connectivity.

**Conclusion:** Mobility is a primary stressor; reactive protocols (AODV) generally outperform proactive ones (DSDV) at high mobility in terms of PDR and overhead.

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the **AODV routing protocol**. Discuss its advantages and limitations compared to DSDV. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working of AODV (Ad-hoc On-Demand Distance Vector):**

AODV discovers a route only when a source node has data to send.

**Phase 1 — Route Discovery:**
1. Source $S$ broadcasts a **Route Request (RREQ)** packet containing source ID, destination ID, sequence numbers, and hop count.
2. Intermediate nodes forward RREQ if they don't have a fresh route to the destination.
3. Destination $D$ (or an intermediate node with a fresh route) unicasts a **Route Reply (RREP)** back to $S$.
4. $S$ installs the reverse path; data transmission begins.

**Phase 2 — Route Maintenance:**
5. If a link breaks, the upstream node broadcasts a **Route Error (RERR)** packet.
6. Source initiates a new route discovery if still needed.

**Advantages over DSDV:**
- **Lower control overhead** — no periodic table updates.
- **Better scalability** for large, mobile networks.
- **Higher PDR** under high mobility.
- **Less memory consumption** (no full topology table).

**Limitations compared to DSDV:**
- **Higher initial delay** due to route discovery latency.
- **More complex** with three message types (RREQ, RREP, RERR).
- **Single route reply** may select a non-optimal path.

**Comparison Diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Source S] -->|RREQ Broadcast| N1[Node 1]
    S -->|RREQ Broadcast| N2[Node 2]
    N1 -->|Forward RREQ| N3[Node 3]
    N2 -->|Forward RREQ| N3
    N3 -->|RREQ| D[Destination D]
    D -->|RREP Unicast| N3
    N3 -->|RREP| N1
    N1 -->|RREP| S
    S -->|Data Packets| N1
    N1 -->|Data| N3
    N3 -->|Data| D
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** Compare the performance of **proactive (DSDV), reactive (DSR), and hybrid (ZRP)** routing protocols in terms of PDR, end-to-end delay, routing overhead, and throughput under varying mobility conditions. Use a comparative table and justify the observations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparative Table:**

| Metric / Condition | DSDV (Proactive) | DSR (Reactive) | ZRP (Hybrid) |
|-------------------|------------------|----------------|--------------|
| PDR @ Low Mobility (0–5 m/s) | High (95%+) | High (95%+) | High (95%+) |
| PDR @ High Mobility (20+ m/s) | Low (60–75%) | Medium-High (80–90%) | Medium (75–85%) |
| End-to-End Delay | Low (route ready) | High (discovery overhead) | Medium |
| Routing Overhead | Very High (periodic) | Low (on-demand) | Tunable (zone radius) |
| Throughput @ Low Load | High | High | High |
| Throughput @ High Load | Degrades fast | Stable | Stable |
| Scalability | Poor | Good | Good |
| Storage Requirement | High (full table) | Low (route cache) | Medium |

**Justifications:**

- **DSDV** maintains up-to-date tables through periodic updates, leading to high overhead and rapid degradation when mobility increases because tables become stale.
- **DSR** uses route caching and source routing, so it generates overhead only when needed. It scales well but suffers from initial discovery delay.
- **ZRP** uses a zone-based approach: proactive within the zone, reactive outside. It offers a balanced trade-off and is best for moderately mobile, large networks.

**Conclusion:** No single protocol is universally best. DSDV suits low-mobility, small networks; DSR suits high-mobility, low-traffic MANETs; ZRP is a balanced hybrid choice.

---

**(b)** Explain the **first-order radio energy model** used in WSN performance analysis. A sensor node transmits 4000 bits over 3 hops, each hop of 50 m. Using $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{amp} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², path-loss exponent $n=2$, compute the total energy consumed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**First-Order Radio Energy Model:**

The model assumes the radio dissipates $E_{elec}$ (electronics energy) to run the transmitter/receiver circuitry, and $\epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$ (amplifier energy) for transmission over distance $d$.

$$E_{TX}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$$

$$E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$$

**Given:**
- $k = 4000$ bits, $d = 50$ m, $H = 3$ hops, $n = 2$
- $E_{elec} = 50 \times 10^{-9}$ J/bit
- $\epsilon_{amp} = 10 \times 10^{-12}$ J/bit/m²

**Step 1: Energy per Transmission**
$$E_{TX} = (50 \times 10^{-9})(4000) + (10 \times 10^{-12})(4000)(50^2)$$
$$E_{TX} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + (10 \times 10^{-12})(4000)(2500)$$
$$E_{TX} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 1 \times 10^{-4} = 3 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{J}$$

**Step 2: Energy per Reception**
$$E_{RX} = (50 \times 10^{-9})(4000) = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{J}$$

**Step 3: Total Energy for 3 Hops (no aggregation)**
$$E_{total} = H \cdot E_{TX} + (H-1) \cdot E_{RX}$$
$$E_{total} = 3 \times 3 \times 10^{-4} + 2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$E_{total} = 9 \times 10^{-4} + 4 \times 10^{-4} = 13 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{J}$$

$$\boxed{E_{total} = 1.3\,\text{mJ}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** If this were a single long-distance hop of 150 m with $n=4$ (multi-path fading), the amplifier term alone would be:
$$(10 \times 10^{-12})(4000)(150^4) = 2.025\,\text{J}$$
This is **>1500× more energy** — proving why multi-hop routing is essential in WSNs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Units Mistake:** Students often mix up $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit with $\epsilon_{amp} = 10$ pJ/bit/m². Note the prefix: nJ = $10^{-9}$ J, pJ = $10^{-12}$ J. Off-by-three orders of magnitude is the most common error.
> 2. **Forgot Receiver Energy:** Many students compute only $E_{TX}$ and forget $E_{RX}$ at intermediate hops. For $H$ hops, you have $H$ transmissions and $(H-1)$ receptions.
> 3. **Path-Loss Exponent:** $n=2$ is for free-space (line of sight); $n=4$ is for multi-path (urban/obstructed). Always check the problem statement.
> 4. **PDR Formula:** Do not write $PDR = \dfrac{P_{sent}}{P_{rcvd}}$. It is always *received* over *sent*.
> 5. **Delay Formula:** Always include route discovery latency for reactive protocols; do not only sum transmission delays.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 2 — Performance Analysis**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Performance analysis evaluates routing protocols using quantitative **QoS metrics**.
- ☐ The **5 primary metrics** are: **PDR, E2E Delay, Throughput, NRL, Energy Consumption**.
- ☐ **Mobility** is the most significant stressor; it reduces PDR and increases delay.
- ☐ **Traffic load** impacts throughput and queueing delay.
- ☐ **Node density** affects contention and routing overhead.

### Protocol Behavior
- ☐ **DSDV (Proactive):** Best for low mobility; high overhead, low delay.
- ☐ **DSR (Reactive):** Best for high mobility, low traffic; uses route cache + source routing.
- ☐ **AODV (Reactive):** Most balanced; uses sequence numbers to avoid loops.
- ☐ **ZRP (Hybrid):** Zone-based; proactive inside zone, reactive outside.
- ☐ **LEACH (WSN):** Cluster-based; rotational CH selection saves energy.

### Formulae to Memorize
- ☐ $PDR = \dfrac{P_{rcvd}}{P_{sent}} \times 100\,\%$
- ☐ $D_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{recv,i} - T_{sent,i})$
- ☐ $Th = \dfrac{N_{rcvd} \times L \times 8}{T_{sim} \times 1000}$ (kbps)
- ☐ $NRL = \dfrac{P_{routing}}{P_{delivered}}$
- ☐ $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$
- ☐ $E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$
- ☐ $E_{total} = H \cdot E_{TX} + (H-1) \cdot E_{RX}$

### Standard Constants (Heinzelman Model)
- ☐ $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit
- ☐ $\epsilon_{amp} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² (free space, $n=2$)
- ☐ $\epsilon_{amp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ (multi-path, $n=4$)
- ☐ $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit (data aggregation)

### Key Trade-offs
- ☐ **Proactive vs Reactive:** Proactive = low delay, high overhead. Reactive = low overhead, high delay.
- ☐ **Single-hop vs Multi-hop:** Single-hop = low latency, high energy. Multi-hop = high latency, low energy.
- ☐ **Aggregation vs No Aggregation:** Aggregation saves energy but adds processing delay.

### Exam Strategy
- ☐ Always write the **formula** before substituting values.
- ☐ Carry **units** through every calculation.
- ☐ For 14-mark questions, provide **tables, diagrams, and justifications**—not just numbers.
- ☐ Mention **mobility model** (Random Waypoint is the default) in any simulation-based answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) — Formal Definition

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed autonomous devices called **sensor nodes** (or motes) that use sensors to cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions, such as temperature, sound, vibration, pressure, motion, or pollutants, at different locations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A WSN consists of hundreds to thousands of low-power, low-cost, multi-functional sensor nodes that are densely deployed in a region of interest. Each node has onboard sensors, a processor, a radio transceiver, and a limited power source (typically a battery). Nodes communicate wirelessly and collaborate to forward sensed data hop-by-hop to a central node called the **Sink** or **Base Station (BS)**.

## 1.2 Sensor Node Architecture — The Four Subsystems

Each sensor node is typically composed of **four fundamental subsystems**, as standardized in the KTU curriculum:

| Subsystem | Function | Example Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing Unit** | Acquires data from the physical environment | Sensors (temperature, humidity, chemical), ADC |
| **Processing Unit** | Stores and processes locally sensed data | Microcontroller / Embedded CPU, Local Memory |
| **Transceiver Unit** | Provides wireless communication | Radio (RF), Antenna |
| **Power Unit** | Supplies energy to all subsystems | Battery (AA/coin cell), Solar scavenger |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Ant Colony" View:** Imagine a colony of ants scattered across a forest floor. Each ant (sensor node) can:
> - **Sense** the smell/sound around it (Sensing Unit)
> - **Remember** a tiny bit of information (Processing Unit)
> - **Whisper** to nearby ants through chemical trails / antenna (Transceiver Unit)
> - Has a **limited sugar reserve** that runs out (Power Unit)
> - Ants don't know the whole map; they pass messages hop-by-hop back to the anthill (Base Station). This is exactly how a WSN operates!

## 1.3 WSN Protocol Stack (5-Layer + 3 Management Planes)

The KTU 2024 syllabus follows the **Crossbow / ZigBee-style 5-layer protocol stack with three cross-cutting management planes**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical Layers (5):**
> 1. **Application Layer** — User-defined application software
> 2. **Transport Layer** — Reliable end-to-end data delivery
> 3. **Network Layer** — Routing of data from source to sink
> 4. **Data Link Layer** — MAC protocol, error control, framing
> 5. **Physical Layer** — Modulation, encoding, radio transmission
>
> **Cross-Cutting Management Planes (3):**
> - **Power Management Plane** — Manages energy consumption
> - **Mobility Management Plane** — Detects and registers node movement
> - **Task Management Plane** — Balances and schedules sensing tasks

## 1.4 Design Goals of a WSN (KTU High-Yield)

The primary design goals — heavily tested in KTU exams — are:

- **Energy Efficiency** (the single most critical metric in WSNs)
- **Scalability** (must support hundreds/thousands of nodes)
- **Self-Organization / Self-Configuration**
- **Fault Tolerance & Reliability**
- **Real-Time / Latency-Aware Operation**
- **Data-Centric Routing** (queries by attribute, not by node ID)
- **Security** (lightweight cryptographic primitives)

## 1.5 Classification of WSN Protocols

WSN protocols are broadly classified as follows (this is the KTU standard classification):

**A. Based on Network Structure:**
- **Flat (Homogeneous)** — All nodes have equal role (e.g., SPIN, Directed Diffusion)
- **Hierarchical (Cluster-based)** — Nodes grouped into clusters with a Cluster Head (e.g., LEACH, PEGASIS, TEEN)
- **Location-based** — Routing decisions use node position (e.g., GEAR, GPSR)

**B. Based on Protocol Function:**
- **MAC Protocols** — S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4
- **Routing Protocols** — SPIN, DD, LEACH, PEGASIS, TEEN, APTEEN
- **Transport Protocols** — PSFQ, RMST, CODA
- **Application Protocols** — SQDDP, MQPs
- **Cross-Layer Protocols** — Designed to break the strict OSI layering
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Subsystem Analysis of a Sensor Node

### 2.1.1 Sensing Unit
Comprises two sub-units:
- **Sensor Device** — Produces a continuous analog signal proportional to the physical phenomenon.
- **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Converts the analog signal to digital form for the processing unit.

Energy consumed in sensing: $E_{sense} = V_{dd} \cdot I_{sensor} \cdot t_{active}$

### 2.1.2 Processing Unit
Typically a low-power microcontroller (e.g., MSP430, ARM Cortex-M). Performs:
- Data aggregation
- Local decision making
- Queue management
- Power management (sleep/wake cycles)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Processing Unit important?** The processing unit can run **data fusion / aggregation** algorithms locally. This means *less data* is transmitted, and transmission is the most energy-expensive operation in a node. Thus, local processing saves network-wide energy.

### 2.1.3 Transceiver Unit
Operates in one of four states: **Transmit, Receive, Idle, Sleep**.
- Sleep consumes the least power.
- Idle listening can be a major energy waste — this is the rationale behind duty-cycled MAC protocols (S-MAC, B-MAC).

### 2.1.4 Power Unit
A non-rechargeable battery is the most common source. **Energy harvesting** (solar, vibration, thermal) is increasingly used.

## 2.2 The First-Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman et al. — KTU High-Yield)

This is the **single most important mathematical model** in WSN energy analysis. It is used in nearly every KTU question on LEACH, PEGASIS, and clustering protocols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions of the First-Order Radio Model:**
> 1. All nodes are symmetric: a node's transmission energy is the same in all directions.
> 2. The radio dissipates $E_{elec}$ (Joules/bit) to run the transmitter or receiver circuitry.
> 3. A transmit amplifier dissipates $E_{fs}$ (free-space) or $E_{mp}$ (multipath) depending on distance.
> 4. Cross-over distance $d_0 = \sqrt{E_{fs}/E_{mp}}$ determines the model.

### 2.2.1 Transmit Energy

$$E_{TX}(k, d) = E_{TX-elec}(k) + E_{TX-amp}(k, d)$$

$$E_{TX}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2} \quad \text{if } d < d_0$$

$$E_{TX}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4} \quad \text{if } d \geq d_0$$

### 2.2.2 Receive Energy

$$E_{RX}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

### 2.2.3 Data Aggregation Energy

$$E_{DA}(k) = k \cdot E_{DA}$$

Where:
- $k$ = number of bits in the message
- $d$ = distance between transmitter and receiver
- $E_{elec}$ = energy dissipated per bit by the electronics (typical: $50 \text{ nJ/bit}$)
- $\varepsilon_{fs}$ = free-space amplifier coefficient (typical: $10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}$)
- $\varepsilon_{mp}$ = multipath amplifier coefficient (typical: $0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{4}$)
- $E_{DA}$ = data aggregation energy (typical: $5 \text{ nJ/bit/message}$)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{TX} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$ | Free-space transmission energy | Short-distance transmission |
| 2 | $E_{TX} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$ | Multipath transmission energy | Long-distance transmission |
| 3 | $E_{RX} = k \cdot E_{elec}$ | Reception energy (no amplifier term) | Any reception cost |
| 4 | $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs} / \varepsilon_{mp}}$ | Crossover distance | Threshold between models |
| 5 | $T(n) = \frac{P}{1 - P \cdot (r \bmod 1/P)}$ | LEACH cluster-head threshold | Cluster head selection |
| 6 | $E_{total} = N \cdot E_{sense} + N \cdot E_{proc} + N \cdot E_{TX/RX}$ | Network total energy | Lifetime estimation |
| 7 | $k_{opt} = \sqrt{N / (2\pi)} \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \cdot \sqrt{M/d_{toBS}^{2}}$ | Optimal # of clusters | LEACH optimization |
| 8 | $E_{chain} \approx (2N - 1) \cdot E_{elec} + N \cdot E_{DA} + \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$ | PEGASIS chain energy | PEGASIS analysis |
| 9 | Network lifetime = Time until FND / HND / LND | First/Half/Last Node Dies | Performance metric |
| 10 | $E_{sleep} \ll E_{idle} \ll E_{TX}$ | Energy hierarchy | MAC design choice |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for energy hierarchy:** "Sleep, Idle, Transmit" — a sleeping node consumes ~1000× less energy than a transmitting node. This is why MAC protocols (S-MAC, B-MAC) focus on maximizing the **sleep time** through duty cycling.

## 2.4 The Three Management Planes — Why They Are Cross-Cutting

- **Power Management Plane:** Monitors battery level and powers down subsystems (radio, sensors) when not needed. *Affects every layer.*
- **Mobility Management Plane:** Detects node movement and keeps track of node location so routing tables can be updated. *Affects Network and Data Link layers.*
- **Task Management Plane:** Balances the sensing workload across nodes in a region to extend network lifetime. *Affects Application and Network layers.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are they called "planes" and not "layers"?** Because they **cut across** the layered protocol stack horizontally, like the wings of an airplane, influencing decisions at every vertical layer.

## 2.5 Protocol Layer Deep-Dive (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.5.1 Application Layer Protocols
- **SQDDP (Sensor Query and Data Dissemination Protocol)** — Issues queries and disseminates data
- **MQPs (Management Query Protocols)** — Network management queries
- **Sensor Management Protocol (SMP)** — Administrative tasks

### 2.5.2 Transport Layer Protocols
The classical TCP is unsuitable for WSNs (overhead, energy cost). Specialized protocols include:
- **PSFQ (Pump Slowly, Fetch Quickly)** — Reliable data delivery
- **RMST (Reliable Multi-Segment Transport)** — Adds reliability to Directed Diffusion
- **CODA (Congestion Detection and Avoidance)** — Handles congestion
- **ESRT (Event-to-Sink Reliable Transport)** — Event reliability

### 2.5.3 Network Layer (Routing) Protocols
Detailed in Section 2.6 below.

### 2.5.4 Data Link / MAC Layer Protocols
- **S-MAC (Sensor-MAC):** Uses listen/sleep cycles, RTS/CTS, virtual clusters
- **B-MAC (Berkeley-MAC):** Uses LPL (Low-Power Listening) and CCA (Clear Channel Assessment)
- **IEEE 802.15.4:** Standard for low-rate WPAN (basis of ZigBee)
- **T-MAC, D-MAC, WiseMAC** — Variants optimizing S-MAC

### 2.5.5 Physical Layer
- Radio frequencies: 433 MHz, 868/915 MHz, 2.4 GHz (ISM bands)
- Modulation: O-QPSK, BPSK, GFSK
- Encoding: DSSS, Manchester

## 2.6 Routing Protocol Taxonomy (KTU Most-Asked)

### A. Data-Centric / Flat Routing
- **SPIN (Sensor Protocols for Information via Negotiation):**
  - Uses 3-way handshake: **ADV** (advertisement) → **REQ** (request) → **DATA**
  - Solves **implosion**, **overlap**, and **resource blindness** problems of flooding.
- **Directed Diffusion (DD):**
  - Sink broadcasts **Interest** messages with attribute-value pairs.
  - **Gradients** are set up back to the sink.
  - **Reinforcement** path becomes the primary path.

### B. Hierarchical / Cluster-Based Routing
- **LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)** — See Section 3 for derivation.
- **PEGASIS (Power-Efficient Gathering in Sensor Information Systems):**
  - Forms a **chain** of nodes (greedy algorithm).
  - Only **one node per round** transmits to the BS.
  - Energy saving: $\sim 100\%$ over LEACH in many scenarios.
- **TEEN (Threshold-sensitive Energy Efficient sensor Network):**
  - Reactive protocol — nodes send data only when attribute crosses a **hard threshold** or changes significantly (**soft threshold**).
- **APTEEN (Adaptive TEEN):**
  - Hybrid — combines periodic + threshold-based transmission.
- **HEED (Hybrid Energy-Efficient Distributed clustering):**
  - Cluster head selection based on residual energy and communication cost.

### C. Location-Based Routing
- **GEAR (Geographic and Energy Aware Routing):**
  - Uses GPS for location; routes to a target region instead of flooding.
  - Energy-aware neighbor selection heuristic.
- **GPSR (Greedy Perimeter Stateless Routing):**
  - Greedy forwarding + perimeter mode (right-hand rule) for voids.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Number of Clusters ($k_{opt}$) in LEACH

This is the most important analytical derivation in KTU Module 3. The objective is to **minimize the total energy dissipated per round** in a clustered network.

### 3.1.1 Setup
- $N$ sensor nodes uniformly distributed in an $M \times M$ region
- 1 Base Station located at $(x_{BS}, y_{BS})$
- $k$ clusters, each cluster has $N/k$ nodes (1 CH + $(N/k) - 1$ non-CH)
- Each CH aggregates data and sends to BS
- All non-CHs send data to their CH

### 3.1.2 Total Energy Per Round

The total energy $E_{total}$ is the sum of three components:

$$E_{total} = E_{CH} + E_{non-CH} + E_{agg}$$

**Step 1: Energy of non-CH transmission to CH**

Distance from non-CH to CH in cluster of area $M^{2}/k$. The expected squared distance:

$$E[d_{toCH}^{2}] = \int_{0}^{x} \int_{0}^{y} (x^{2} + y^{2}) \rho(x,y) \, dx \, dy = \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}$$

Assuming a uniform distribution of nodes within the cluster.

Energy dissipated by all non-CHs per round:

$$E_{non-CH} = k \cdot \left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) \cdot E_{elec} \cdot l + k \cdot \left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot l \cdot E[d_{toCH}^{2}]$$

Simplifying for $N \gg k$:

$$E_{non-CH} \approx N \cdot E_{elec} \cdot l + N \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot l \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}$$

**Step 2: Energy of CH transmission to BS**

$$E_{CH} = k \cdot E_{elec} \cdot l \cdot \frac{N}{k} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot l \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$$

The first term is reception of $N/k$ messages (each $l$ bits), the second is long-haul transmission to BS (multipath model assumed).

Simplifying:

$$E_{CH} = N \cdot E_{elec} \cdot l + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot l \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$$

**Step 3: Data aggregation energy at CHs**

$$E_{agg} = N \cdot E_{DA} \cdot l$$

### 3.1.3 Total Energy Equation

Summing all three:

$$E_{total} = N \cdot l \cdot \left( 2 E_{elec} + E_{DA} \right) + N \cdot l \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k} + k \cdot l \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$$

### 3.1.4 Differentiate to Find $k_{opt}$

To find the value of $k$ that minimizes $E_{total}$, take the derivative with respect to $k$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{d E_{total}}{d k} = -N \cdot l \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k^{2}} + l \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$N \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k^{2}} = \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$$

Solving for $k$:

$$k^{2} = \frac{N \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot M^{2}}{2\pi \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}}$$

Taking the square root:

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Result:**
>
> $$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}$$
>
> This is the **optimal number of clusters** that minimizes total energy dissipation per round. In KTU exams, write this in **bold** in the answer sheet and **box it** for full marks.

### 3.1.5 Interpretation of $k_{opt}$
- If $d_{toBS}$ is large (BS far away), $k_{opt}$ is small → fewer clusters with longer intra-cluster distances are energy-efficient.
- If $N$ is large (more nodes), $k_{opt}$ is larger → can afford more clusters.
- $k_{opt}$ is **independent of $l$** (packet size) and $E_{elec}$ — a beautiful engineering result.

## 3.2 Derivation: LEACH Cluster-Head Selection Threshold

Each node decides **independently** to become a CH in round $r$ by generating a random number $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ and comparing it to a threshold $T(n)$.

### 3.2.1 The Threshold Formula

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{P}{1 - P \cdot \left(r \bmod \dfrac{1}{P}\right)} & \text{if } n \in G \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Where:
- $P$ = desired probability of becoming a CH (typically $P = 0.05$ i.e., 5%)
- $r$ = current round number
- $G$ = set of nodes that have **not** been CHs in the last $1/P$ rounds

### 3.2.2 Why This Threshold?

**Step 1:** We need exactly $k = N \cdot P$ CHs per round on average.

**Step 2:** Each node should be a CH once every $1/P$ rounds (uniform rotation).

**Step 3:** Without rotation, set $T(n) = P$. Then probability of being a CH in any round is $P$, and the **expected** number of CHs is $N \cdot P$. Good — but this can lead to the same node being CH repeatedly and dying quickly.

**Step 4:** To ensure fairness, we restrict CHs to those in $G$ (haven't been CHs recently). The number of eligible nodes at round $r$ (assuming uniform rotation) is approximately $N \cdot (1 - P \cdot (r \bmod 1/P))$.

**Step 5:** The probability $P$ of being a CH must now be applied only to the $G$ set. So:

$$T(n) = \frac{P}{1 - P \cdot (r \bmod 1/P)}$$

This is greater than $P$ and increases as the round progresses within a $1/P$ cycle, guaranteeing that exactly $P$ fraction of nodes are CHs by the end of the cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Summing $T(n)$ over the $N \cdot (1 - P \cdot (r \bmod 1/P))$ eligible nodes gives exactly $N \cdot P$ — the expected number of CHs per round. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of WSN Routing Protocols

| Protocol | Type | Energy | Data Delivery | Scalability | Pros | Cons | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SPIN** | Flat | Moderate | Event-driven | High | Solves flooding issues | Not guaranteed delivery | High |
| **Directed Diffusion** | Flat | Moderate | Query-driven | High | Energy-efficient via gradients | Setup overhead | High |
| **LEACH** | Hierarchical | High saving | Periodic | Medium | Self-organizing, rotating CHs | Random CH selection | **Very High** |
| **PEGASIS** | Hierarchical | Highest saving | Periodic | Medium | Only 1 node to BS/round | Chain construction overhead | Very High |
| **TEEN** | Hierarchical | High | Reactive (threshold) | High | Good for time-critical apps | Data loss if CH fails | High |
| **APTEEN** | Hierarchical | Moderate | Hybrid | High | Combines periodic + reactive | Higher complexity | Medium |
| **HEED** | Hierarchical | High | Periodic | High | Residual energy + cost | Multi-iteration | Medium |
| **GEAR** | Location | High | Geographic | High | Energy-aware | Needs GPS | Medium |
| **GPSR** | Location | High | Geographic | High | Stateless | Void routing issues | Low |

## 3.4 Numerical Example — A KTU 2024 Typical Problem

**Problem:** A WSN has 100 nodes uniformly distributed in a $100 \times 100 \text{ m}^{2}$ field. The BS is located 75 m from the field center. Compute $k_{opt}$ using the standard parameters:
$E_{elec} = 50 \text{ nJ/bit}$, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}$, $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{4}$.

**Solution:**

Apply the formula derived in Section 3.1.4:

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}$$

Compute each factor:

$$\sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{2 \cdot 3.1416}} = \sqrt{15.9155} \approx 3.989$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} = \sqrt{\frac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{\frac{10}{0.0013}} = \sqrt{7692.31} \approx 87.71$$

$$\frac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}} = \frac{100}{75^{2}} = \frac{100}{5625} \approx 0.01778$$

Therefore:

$$k_{opt} = 3.989 \times 87.71 \times 0.01778 \approx 6.22$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $k_{opt} \approx 6$ clusters. Since $k$ must be an integer and a fraction of $N$, we round to $k = 5$ or $k = 6$. The LEACH protocol typically takes $P = 0.05$ (5 CHs out of 100) which is close to the optimal.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — LEACH Operational Phases (Per Round)

```mermaid
graph TB
    START["Round r Begins"]
    ADS["Advertisement Phase - Each Node picks random alpha - If alpha less than Tn then becomes CH - CH broadcasts ADV msg"]
    SCH["Schedule Setup Phase - CHs publish TDMA schedule - Non CHs send JOIN-REQ - CH assigns time slots"]
    STB["Steady State Phase - Non CHs sense and send to CH in their slot - CH aggregates data - CH sends aggregated packet to BS"]
    EOD["End of Round - All nodes sleep until next round"]
    START --> ADS --> SCH --> STB --> EOD
    EOD -->|Next round| START
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — SPIN Three-Way Handshake

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Node A has data X"]
    B["Node B is neighbor"]
    C["Node C is neighbor"]
    A -->|ADV with meta data X| B
    A -->|ADV with meta data X| C
    B -->|REQ for X| A
    C -->|REQ for X| A
    A -->|DATA X to B| B
    A -->|DATA X to C| C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the SPIN diagram:** Node A advertises *metadata* (not raw data) describing what it has. Interested neighbors send a request. Then A sends the actual data. This is why SPIN saves energy — it only transmits to **interested** nodes, eliminating the implosion and overlap problems of classical flooding.
