# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]

**Define Wireless Sensor Network. List the four main components of a sensor node.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-organizing network of spatially distributed, autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions and forward the sensed data to a central sink via multi-hop wireless communication.

The four main components of a sensor node are:

1. **Sensing Unit** — Comprises sensors and ADC for data acquisition.
2. **Processing Unit** — Microcontroller with local memory for computation and data aggregation.
3. **Transceiver Unit** — Radio and antenna for wireless communication.
4. **Power Unit** — Battery (or energy harvester) supplying energy to all subsystems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Definition — 1 mark | Four components — 0.5 mark each = 2 marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]

**What are the three cross-cutting management planes in the WSN protocol stack? Briefly explain any one.**

**Model Answer:**

The three cross-cutting management planes that span across the layered protocol stack are:

1. **Power Management Plane** — Manages how a node uses its energy resources; decides when to power down subsystems.
2. **Mobility Management Plane** — Detects and registers node movement; updates routes based on location changes.
3. **Task Management Plane** — Balances the sensing and processing tasks among nodes in a region to conserve energy.

**Explanation of Power Management Plane:**
The power management plane monitors the residual battery level of each node. When the battery is low or when a node is not actively sensing/communicating, this plane switches off the radio, sensors, or processor to enter a low-power sleep state. This extends the operational lifetime of the network. The plane interacts with all five layers — e.g., at the MAC layer it triggers sleep cycles, at the network layer it influences routing decisions to avoid depleted nodes, and at the application layer it may reduce the sampling rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Listing 3 planes — 1.5 marks | Explanation of one — 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option I [14 Marks]

#### **(a)** Explain the architecture of a WSN with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of each layer in the protocol stack. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A Wireless Sensor Network consists of three architectural components:
1. **Sensor Nodes (Motes):** Distributed in the field to sense data.
2. **Base Station (Sink):** Collects all data; has unlimited power and processing.
3. **Gateway / User Network:** Connects the WSN to the outside world (Internet, control center).

**Protocol Stack — Role of Each Layer:**

**Physical Layer:** Handles frequency selection, carrier frequency generation, signal modulation (O-QPSK, BPSK), and data encryption at the bit level. It is responsible for converting digital bits into radio signals and vice versa.

**Data Link Layer:** Responsible for medium access control (MAC), error detection/correction, and framing. In WSNs, MAC is critical for energy efficiency (e.g., S-MAC uses duty cycling to keep the radio in sleep mode most of the time). It also handles collision avoidance.

**Network Layer:** Performs routing of data from source nodes to the sink. WSN routing is data-centric, energy-aware, and often multi-hop. Examples: LEACH, PEGASIS, SPIN, Directed Diffusion.

**Transport Layer:** Provides reliable end-to-end communication between the sink and sensor nodes. Standard TCP is unsuitable; protocols like PSFQ, RMST, CODA, and ESRT are used.

**Application Layer:** Provides software for specific sensing applications (temperature monitoring, intrusion detection, etc.). It interfaces with the user and translates high-level queries into network-level operations. Protocols include SQDDP and SMP.

**Cross-Cutting Management Planes:** The three planes (Power, Mobility, Task) operate across all layers to optimize energy, handle mobility, and balance the sensing workload.

[Include a labeled diagram as in Section 4.2 for full marks.]

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (7 marks):** Architecture description — 2 marks | Layer-wise role — 4 marks (1 mark per layer × 4 prominent layers) | Diagram — 1 mark.

---

#### **(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the three categories of WSN routing protocols. Compare SPIN and Directed Diffusion. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Categories of WSN Routing Protocols:**

**1. Flat / Data-Centric Routing**
- All nodes have equal roles.
- Routing is based on data attributes, not node IDs.
- Examples: SPIN, Directed Diffusion (DD).
- Pros: Low overhead, no hierarchy to maintain.
- Cons: Higher latency, not energy-efficient for large networks.

**2. Hierarchical / Cluster-Based Routing**
- Nodes are organized into clusters; each cluster has a Cluster Head (CH).
- CHs aggregate data and forward to sink.
- Examples: LEACH, PEGASIS, TEEN, APTEEN, HEED.
- Pros: Excellent energy efficiency, scalable.
- Cons: Cluster formation overhead, CH failure risk.

**3. Location-Based Routing**
- Routing decisions use position information (GPS).
- Nodes forward data to geographically closer neighbors.
- Examples: GEAR, GPSR.
- Pros: Energy-efficient if positions are known.
- Cons: Requires GPS hardware, void handling needed.

[Include a hierarchical routing diagram as in Section 4.3 for full marks.]

**Comparison: SPIN vs. Directed Diffusion**

| Parameter | SPIN | Directed Diffusion |
|---|---|---|
| **Communication Model** | Push-based (source initiates) | Pull-based (sink initiates) |
| **Negotiation** | Yes (ADV/REQ/DATA) | No negotiation; uses gradients |
| **Data Naming** | Meta-data (application-specific) | Attribute-Value pairs |
| **Path Setup** | On-demand by source | Pre-established by sink |
| **Energy Efficiency** | High (avoids flooding) | High (uses single reinforced path) |
| **Delivery Guarantee** | Best-effort (no guarantee) | Best-effort (no guarantee) |
| **Suitability** | Event-driven, periodic apps | Query-driven apps |
| **Complexity** | Low | Higher (gradient setup) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (7 marks):** Three categories — 3 marks (1 mark each) | SPIN vs DD table — 3 marks | Diagrams — 1 mark.

---

### Question B — Option II [14 Marks]

#### **(a)** Derive the expression for the optimal number of clusters ($k_{opt}$) in the LEACH protocol. State the assumptions. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions of the First-Order Radio Model (Heinzelman):**
1. There are $N$ sensor nodes uniformly distributed in an $M \times M$ region.
2. The Base Station (BS) is located at distance $d_{toBS}$ from the field.
3. All nodes are symmetric: energy dissipation is identical in all directions.
4. Each node has $k$ bits of data to send per round.
5. The radio dissipates $E_{elec}$ (Joules/bit) to run transmitter/receiver circuitry.
6. The transmit amplifier uses free-space model ($\varepsilon_{fs} d^{2}$) for $d < d_0$ and multipath model ($\varepsilon_{mp} d^{4}$) for $d \geq d_0$, where $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$.
7. CHs always use multipath model (long-haul to BS).
8. Cluster area is $M^{2}/k$ for $k$ clusters.

**Derivation:**

[Follow the full derivation in Section 3.1.2 to 3.1.4 step by step. Write the three component energies, sum them, differentiate, and solve for $k_{opt}$.]

**Final Result (Box This):**

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (7 marks):** Assumptions — 2 marks | Total energy equation — 2 marks | Differentiation step — 2 marks | Final boxed $k_{opt}$ expression — 1 mark.

---

#### **(b)** Explain the working of the LEACH protocol with its operational phases. How does it achieve energy efficiency? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)** is a hierarchical, cluster-based, self-organizing routing protocol proposed by Heinzelman et al. The key idea is **rotational cluster head selection** to evenly distribute the energy burden across all nodes.

**Operational Phases:**

**Phase 1 — Advertisement Phase (Setup)**
- At the start of round $r$, each node $n$ picks a random number $\alpha \in [0,1]$.
- If $\alpha < T(n)$, the node becomes a CH. The threshold $T(n)$ is:
  $$T(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{P}{1 - P \cdot (r \bmod 1/P)} & \text{if } n \in G \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
- Each CH broadcasts an **ADV** (Advertisement) message using CSMA MAC.

**Phase 2 — Cluster Set-Up Phase**
- Non-CH nodes listen to all ADV messages and choose the CH with the **strongest received signal** (i.e., closest CH).
- They send a **JOIN-REQ** message back to the chosen CH.
- Each CH creates a **TDMA schedule** and broadcasts it to its cluster members.
- Each non-CH gets a unique time slot and can sleep during others (energy saving).

**Phase 3 — Steady-State Phase (Data Transmission)**
- In its assigned slot, each non-CH sends sensed data to its CH using the **free-space model** (short distance).
- The CH aggregates the received data (data fusion), compresses it, and sends it to the BS using the **multipath model** (long distance, often with spread-spectrum and CDMA codes to avoid inter-cluster interference).
- After a fixed duration, the round ends and the cycle repeats.

[Include the LEACH operational diagram as in Section 4.4 for full marks.]

**How LEACH Achieves Energy Efficiency:**

1. **Rotational CH selection:** Energy load is distributed uniformly — no node dies prematurely.
2. **Data aggregation at CH:** Reduces data volume transmitted to BS by 5×–10×.
3. **TDMA scheduling:** Non-CHs sleep when not transmitting — saves up to 90% of idle listening energy.
4. **Short-distance intra-cluster communication:** Uses free-space model ($d^{2}$) which is more efficient than multipath ($d^{4}$).
5. **Single-hop to BS from CHs:** Although it is long-range, it occurs infrequently.
6. **Localized coordination:** No global routing table is needed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **forget to mention the threshold $T(n)$ formula** in LEACH. This costs **1–2 marks**.
> - Students often **miss the data aggregation step** at the CH. This costs **1 mark**.
> - Students often **confuse LEACH phases** — "Steady State" comes after "Setup", not before. Wrong order costs **1 mark**.
> - Students often write the multipath model for intra-cluster communication. **Wrong** — intra-cluster uses the free-space model.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST (Must Memorize for KTU)**

### ⭐ Architectural Concepts
- [x] A WSN has **4 subsystem components per node**: Sensing, Processing, Transceiver, Power.
- [x] The protocol stack is **5 layers + 3 management planes**.
- [x] The 3 management planes are: **Power, Mobility, Task** management — they cut across all layers horizontally.
- [x] Design goals: **Energy efficiency, scalability, self-organization, fault tolerance**.

### ⭐ Energy Model Formulas (Memorize Exactly)
- [x] $E_{TX}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$ (free space, $d < d_0$)
- [x] $E_{TX}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$ (multipath, $d \geq d_0$)
- [x] $E_{RX}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$ (no amplifier term)
- [x] $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$ (crossover distance)

### ⭐ LEACH (MOST IMPORTANT PROTOCOL)
- [x] $T(n) = \dfrac{P}{1 - P \cdot (r \bmod 1/P)}$ for $n \in G$, else $0$.
- [x] $k_{opt} = \sqrt{N/(2\pi)} \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \cdot M/d_{toBS}^{2}$
- [x] Two operational phases: **Setup** and **Steady State**.
- [x] CHs rotate every round — fairness over $1/P$ rounds.
- [x] Typical $P = 0.05$ (5% of nodes are CHs per round).

### ⭐ Routing Protocol Comparison
- [x] **SPIN** = 3-way handshake: ADV → REQ → DATA. Solves implosion, overlap, resource-blindness.
- [x] **Directed Diffusion** = Sink broadcasts Interest, gradients set up, reinforcement picks the best path.
- [x] **PEGASIS** = Greedy chain, only 1 node transmits to BS per round → highest energy saving.
- [x] **TEEN** = Hard threshold + soft threshold; reactive (good for time-critical).
- [x] **APTEEN** = Periodic + threshold (hybrid of LEACH + TEEN).
- [x] **HEED** = Residual energy + communication cost for CH selection.
- [x] **GEAR** = GPS-based, energy-aware neighbor selection.

### ⭐ MAC Protocol Highlights
- [x] **S-MAC**: Fixed duty cycle, listen/sleep periods, virtual clusters for synchronization.
- [x] **B-MAC**: Low-Power Listening (LPL) + Clear Channel Assessment (CCA); adaptive to traffic.
- [x] **IEEE 802.15.4**: Foundation of ZigBee; uses CSMA/CA, GTS, 2.4 GHz.

### ⭐ Transport Layer
- [x] **PSFQ** = Pump Slowly, Fetch Quickly — for reliable downstream.
- [x] **CODA** = Congestion Detection and Avoidance.
- [x] **ESRT** = Event-to-Sink Reliable Transport.

### ⭐ Cross-Layer Design
- [x] Breaks the **strict OSI layering** to allow direct information sharing between layers for energy optimization.

### ⭐ Lifetime Metrics
- [x] **FND** = First Node Dies (stability period ends).
- [x] **HND** = Half Nodes Die.
- [x] **LND** = Last Node Dies (network lifetime ends).

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** If a KTU question asks to "explain WSN architecture with a diagram", **always draw a labeled block diagram of the protocol stack** as in Section 4.2. It is worth **1–2 marks** by itself, and students who skip diagrams typically lose those easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor Node Architecture and Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT863 - ADHOC AND WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensor Node Architecture and Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensor Node Architecture** refers to the **systematic hardware and software organization** of a resource-constrained, battery-powered embedded device deployed in a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) to perform **sensing**, **data processing**, **wireless communication**, and **energy management** functions autonomously within a physical environment.

A **sensor node** (also called a *mote*) is the fundamental building block of a WSN. According to the KTU 2024 scheme, its architecture is a **layered integration** of four functional subsystems:

1. **Sensing Subsystem** – Acquires physical/environmental data.
2. **Processing Subsystem** – Manages data, executes algorithms, controls node behavior.
3. **Communication (Radio) Subsystem** – Enables wireless data exchange.
4. **Power Subsystem** – Supplies and regulates energy for all other blocks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Smart Village Watchman**
> Imagine a tiny robotic **watchman** stationed inside a remote village:
> - His **eyes and ears** (camera, microphone, temperature sensor) = **Sensing Unit**
> - His **brain and notebook** (microcontroller + memory) = **Processing Unit**
> - His **walkie-talkie** (radio transceiver) = **Communication Unit**
> - His **packed lunch and water bottle** (battery + power circuit) = **Power Unit**
>
> Just like the watchman must ration his lunch carefully because resupply is impossible, a sensor node must **strictly manage its energy budget** because battery replacement in a forest, ocean, or battlefield is infeasible. Every subsystem trades off performance against energy.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Emphasis)

- Typical operating voltage: **3.3 V DC**
- Typical radio frequency bands: **2.4 GHz (ISM)**, **433/868/915 MHz**
- Standard battery energy density: **~2000 J/g (Lithium AA)**
- Node form factor: **Matchbox-sized** (MicaZ: 58 × 32 × 7 mm³)
- Microcontroller clock: **8 MHz to 32 MHz** (typical)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture – The Four Subsystems

### A. Sensing Subsystem
- Comprises **two physical layers**:
  - **Sensor (Transducer)**: Converts physical phenomena → electrical signals.
    - Examples: **Mica2 temperature sensor**, **photodiode (light)**, **accelerometer (motion)**, **humidity (SHT11)**.
  - **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**: Converts analog signals → digital data for the processor.
- **Key metrics**: *Accuracy*, *Precision*, *Dynamic Range*, *Sampling Rate*, *Power Draw*.

### B. Processing Subsystem
- Built around a **microcontroller unit (MCU)** or **System on Chip (SoC)**.
- Components:
  - **CPU**: Executes instructions (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, Atmel AVR).
  - **Memory hierarchy**:
    - **Program Flash**: 32 KB – 1 MB (e.g., MicaZ: 128 KB)
    - **RAM**: 4 KB – 64 KB (e.g., MicaZ: 4 KB)
    - **External Storage (EEPROM/Flash)**: Used for bulk data logging.
- **Why critical?** Performs *data aggregation*, *compression*, *encryption*, and *MAC protocol execution*.

### C. Communication Subsystem
- Centered on a **radio transceiver** (e.g., CC2420, CC1000).
- Operates over short range (typically **10 m – 100 m**).
- Three operational states:
  - **Transmit (TX)**: Highest power draw (e.g., **~17.4 mA at 0 dBm**).
  - **Receive (RX)**: Comparable to TX (e.g., **~19.7 mA**).
  - **Sleep/Idle**: Lowest power (e.g., **~1 µA**).
- The transceiver typically consumes **more energy than sensing + processing combined**.

### D. Power Subsystem
- Energy source: **Batteries (alkaline/Li-ion)**, **Solar cells**, **Vibration harvesters**.
- Includes **DC-DC converters** and **voltage regulators** to provide stable rails (1.8 V, 3.3 V).
- A **power management unit (PMU)** controls duty cycling.

## 2.2 Design Constraints (KTU High-Yield)

| Constraint | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|
| **Energy** | Primary bottleneck; drives sleep/wake scheduling. |
| **Size** | Limits battery capacity and antenna dimensions. |
| **Cost** | Must be cheap for dense deployment. |
| **Hardware** | Limited CPU, RAM, Flash. |
| **Software** | Must use lightweight RTOS/event kernels. |
| **Environment** | Must withstand heat, humidity, vibration. |
| **Communication** | Bandwidth-limited, error-prone wireless link. |
| **Deployment** | Often unattended for years. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Total node energy** | $E_{total} = \int_{0}^{T} P(t)\,dt$ | T = node lifetime target |
| **Average power** | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} P_i \cdot t_i}{T_{cycle}}$ | $t_i$ = duration in state $i$ |
| **Duty cycle** | $DC = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100\%$ | Usually $\leq 5\%$ for WSN |
| **Battery lifetime** | $L = \dfrac{C_{bat}\,[mAh]}{I_{avg}\,[mA]}\, [hours]$ | $C_{bat}$ = battery capacity |
| **Energy per bit (TX)** | $E_{bit} = V_{cc} \cdot I_{TX} / R_b$ | $R_b$ = bit rate |
| **Free-space path loss** | $PL_{FS} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ (dB) | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| **Radio on-time** | $T_{on} = \dfrac{N_{bytes} \cdot 8}{R_b}$ | $N_{bytes}$ = packet payload |
| **Sampling frequency** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | $f_{max}$ = max signal freq. |
| **SNR** | $SNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | dB scale |
| **Power conversion** | $P = V \cdot I$ | Watts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In markdown tables, absolute value uses $\vert x \vert$ — **never** write `|x|` inside a table row to avoid breaking KTU markdown rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sensor node design principles directly power applications in **precision agriculture** (soil moisture telemetry), **structural health monitoring** (bridge vibration), **battlefield surveillance**, **habitat monitoring** (Great Duck Island), **smart grids**, and **patient healthcare wearables**. The trade-offs established here cascade into MAC protocols, routing algorithms, and energy-harvesting research.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy-Optimized Duty Cycle

**Problem Statement:** A sensor node spends $T_a$ seconds in active mode drawing $I_a$ mA, and $T_s$ seconds in sleep drawing $I_s$ mA. The supply voltage is $V$. Derive the average power consumption and the optimal duty cycle for a target lifetime $L$.

**Step 1 — Define one operational cycle.**

$$T_{cycle} = T_a + T_s$$

*Conversion logic:* A mote's life is a repetition of active/sleep windows; the cycle is the smallest repeating interval.

**Step 2 — Total charge consumed per cycle.**

$$Q_{cycle} = I_a T_a + I_s T_s$$

*Conversion logic:* Charge (mA·s) is current integrated over time, summed across states.

**Step 3 — Average current drawn.**

$$I_{avg} = \dfrac{Q_{cycle}}{T_{cycle}} = \dfrac{I_a T_a + I_s T_s}{T_a + T_s}$$

*Conversion logic:* Average current is total charge divided by total cycle time.

**Step 4 — Express in terms of duty cycle.**

Define the duty cycle $D$ as the fraction of time spent active.

$$D = \dfrac{T_a}{T_{cycle}}$$

Substitute $T_s = T_{cycle} - T_a$ and $T_a = D \cdot T_{cycle}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} &= \dfrac{I_a (D T_{cycle}) + I_s (T_{cycle} - D T_{cycle})}{T_{cycle}} \\
        &= I_a D + I_s (1 - D) \\
        &= I_s + D (I_a - I_s)
\end{aligned}$$

*Conversion logic:* Algebraic substitution of the duty cycle definition into the average current equation.

**Step 5 — Average power.**

$$P_{avg} = V \cdot I_{avg} = V\bigl[I_s + D (I_a - I_s)\bigr]$$

*Conversion logic:* Power is voltage times current.

**Step 6 — Lifetime equation.**

If battery capacity is $C_{bat}$ (mAh):

$$L_{hours} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_s + D(I_a - I_s)}$$

*Conversion logic:* Lifetime is capacity (mAh) divided by average current drain (mA), yielding hours.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Canonical WSN Motes

| Parameter | MicaZ | TelosB | IRIS | BTnode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| MCU | ATmega128L (8 MHz) | MSP430F1611 (8 MHz) | ATmega1281 | ATmega128L |
| Program Flash | 128 KB | 48 KB | 128 KB | 64 KB |
| RAM | 4 KB | 10 KB | 8 KB | 64 KB ext. |
| Radio | CC2420 (2.4 GHz) | CC2420 (2.4 GHz) | AT86RF230 (2.4 GHz) | CC1000 / BT |
| Data Rate | 250 kbps | 250 kbps | 250 kbps | 38.4 kbps |
| TX Current | 17.4 mA | 17.4 mA | 16 mA | 27 mA |
| RX Current | 19.7 mA | 18.8 mA | 16 mA | 10 mA |
| Sleep Current | < 15 µA | < 6 µA | < 8 µA | < 50 µA |
| Battery | 2× AA | 2× AA | 2× AA | External |
| OS Support | TinyOS | TinyOS / Contiki | TinyOS / Contiki | TinyOS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Lower sleep current (TelosB: 6 µA) and modern MSP430 architecture make TelosB the most energy-efficient, which is why KTU questions frequently use it for numerical examples.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A MicaZ mote uses $I_a = 20\,mA$ in active mode and $I_s = 0.015\,mA$ in sleep. Battery capacity is $C_{bat} = 2000\,mAh$. If target lifetime is 1 year, calculate the maximum allowable duty cycle.

**Solution Steps:**

Step A — Convert lifetime to hours.

$$L = 1 \text{ year} = 365 \times 24 = 8760 \text{ hours}$$

Step B — Maximum average current.

$$I_{avg}^{max} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{L} = \dfrac{2000}{8760} = 0.2283\,mA$$

Step C — Solve the duty-cycle equation for D.

$$I_{avg} = I_s + D (I_a - I_s)$$

$$D = \dfrac{I_{avg} - I_s}{I_a - I_s} = \dfrac{0.2283 - 0.015}{20 - 0.015}$$

$$D = \dfrac{0.2133}{19.985} = 0.01067$$

Step D — Final answer.

$$\boxed{D \approx 1.067\%}$$

*Interpretation:* The mote must sleep ~99% of the time to last one year. This drives the design of **low-duty-cycle MAC protocols** like B-MAC and S-MAC.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hardware Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[Power Subsystem - Battery and DC DC Regulator]
    SEN[Sensor Array - Temperature Light Humidity]
    ADC[ADC - Analog to Digital Converter]
    MCU[Microcontroller - CPU Flash RAM]
    RAD[Radio Transceiver - CC2420 2.4 GHz]
    ANT[Antenna - 2.4 GHz]
    PMU[Power Management Unit - Duty Cycle Controller]

    PWR --> MCU
    PWR --> SEN
    PWR --> ADC
    PWR --> RAD
    PWR --> PMU

    SEN -->|analog signal| ADC
    ADC -->|digital samples| MCU
    MCU -->|control commands| RAD
    RAD -->|RF modulated| ANT
    ANT -->|wireless| EXT[Neighbouring Mote or Base Station]
    PMU -->|gate signals| MCU
    PMU -->|gate signals| RAD
```

**Explanation:** The Power Subsystem feeds every block, while the PMU gates power to MCU and Radio — this is the **key energy-saving mechanism**.

## 4.2 Software / OS Architecture (Layered Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP[Application Layer - User Sensing Program]
    MID[Middleware - Routing Aggregation Time Sync]
    OS[OS Kernel - TinyOS or Contiki Scheduler]
    HAL[Hardware Abstraction Layer - Drivers ADC Radio GPIO]
    HW[Physical Hardware - MCU Sensors Radio]

    APP --> MID
    MID --> OS
    OS --> HAL
    HAL --> HW
```

**Explanation:** TinyOS uses an **event-driven, component-based** model (nesC language), while Contiki uses **protothreads** for lightweight concurrency. The HAL isolates hardware specifics from upper layers.

## 4.3 Sensor Node Operational State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Sleep
    Sleep --> Wakeup: Timer or Interrupt
    Wakeup --> Sense: Active mode
    Sense --> Process: ADC read complete
    Process --> Transmit: Data ready
    Transmit --> Receive: Wait for ACK
    Receive --> Sleep: Acknowledgement or timeout
    Process --> Sleep: No interesting event
    Transmit --> Sleep: Channel busy retry later
```

**Explanation:** This state diagram captures the **duty-cycled behaviour** of a typical mote — the Sleep state dominates the timeline, justifying the low duty cycle derived in Section 3.3.

## 4.4 Energy Consumption Distribution (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Energy Budget 100 percent] --> B[Radio Communication 70 to 80 percent]
    A --> C[Sensing 5 to 10 percent]
    A --> D[CPU Processing 5 to 10 percent]
    A --> E[Idle Leakage 5 to 15 percent]
```

**Explanation:** This is the **canonical WSN energy pie-chart** referenced in every textbook. Communication dominates — hence *cross-layer protocol design* and *in-network processing* are the principal research levers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**List the four main subsystems of a sensor node and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory four-subsection answer expected by KTU valuators.**

1. **Sensing Subsystem** – Consists of physical sensors and ADC; converts environmental phenomena (temperature, light, pressure) into digital data.
2. **Processing Subsystem** – Contains microcontroller, RAM, and flash; executes sensing schedules, data aggregation, and communication protocols.
3. **Communication Subsystem** – Radio transceiver + antenna; transmits/receives packets over the wireless channel.
4. **Power Subsystem** – Battery and DC-DC regulator; supplies energy and performs duty cycling for energy efficiency.

*(3 marks – 1 for listing, 2 for the function descriptions.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Why is the communication subsystem considered the dominant energy consumer in a WSN node?**

**Model Answer:**

Radio transmission requires substantial current (~17–20 mA) and reception is comparable. Each transmitted/received byte keeps the radio in TX/RX state far longer than the CPU takes to process the same byte. Since wireless transmission must overcome path loss (which scales with distance squared in free space), power grows rapidly with link range. Additionally, idle listening and retransmissions due to collisions further inflate radio energy — making it 70–80% of total node energy.

*(3 marks – 1 for identifying the radio, 2 for the comparative reasoning.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **hardware architecture of a sensor node** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of each block.

**(b) [7 marks]** A WSN mote operates in two states: active (current 25 mA, voltage 3.3 V) and sleep (current 0.02 mA). The battery capacity is 2500 mAh. Compute the maximum duty cycle for a 2-year lifetime and comment on the result.

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Block Diagram (describe the four subsystems):**

1. **Sensing Unit:** Transducers + ADC; converts physical to digital.
2. **Processing Unit:** MCU with on-chip flash (program) and RAM (data); runs OS, MAC, and application logic.
3. **Transceiver Unit:** Radio (e.g., CC2420) + antenna; modulates/demodulates and transmits/receives.
4. **Power Unit:** Battery + voltage regulator + power gating; PMU controls duty cycling.

**Supporting Components:**
- **External memory** (EEPROM/flash) for bulk storage.
- **Sensor interface bus** (I²C/SPI) for plug-and-play sensors.
- **Debug/Programming port** (JTAG/UART).

**Design Trade-offs:** Energy vs. accuracy, range vs. battery, sampling rate vs. duty cycle.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1:** Lifetime in hours.

$$L = 2 \times 365 \times 24 = 17520 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2:** Maximum average current.

$$I_{avg}^{max} = \dfrac{2500}{17520} = 0.1427 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3:** Duty cycle formula.

$$D = \dfrac{I_{avg} - I_s}{I_a - I_s} = \dfrac{0.1427 - 0.02}{25 - 0.02}$$

$$D = \dfrac{0.1227}{24.98} = 0.00491$$

**Step 4:** Convert to percentage.

$$\boxed{D \approx 0.491\%}$$

**Comment:** The mote must sleep **99.5% of the time**, demonstrating the severe energy constraint. This mandates energy-aware MAC protocols (e.g., S-MAC, B-MAC), data aggregation at intermediate nodes, and possible energy harvesting for sustainability.

*(14 marks: 7 for block diagram + description, 7 for numerical with comment.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **hardware specifications of MicaZ, TelosB, and IRIS** sensor motes. Highlight which is most energy-efficient and why.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **software architecture of a WSN node**. Discuss TinyOS and Contiki in terms of architecture, scheduling, and memory footprint.

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

| Feature | MicaZ | TelosB | IRIS |
|---|---|---|---|
| MCU | ATmega128L (8-bit) | MSP430F1611 (16-bit) | ATmega1281 (8-bit) |
| RAM | 4 KB | 10 KB | 8 KB |
| Flash | 128 KB | 48 KB | 128 KB |
| Radio | CC2420 | CC2420 | AT86RF230 |
| Sleep current | < 15 µA | < 6 µA | < 8 µA |

**Conclusion:** **TelosB is most energy-efficient** because:
- MSP430's ultra-low-power design and 6 µA sleep current.
- Integrated onboard sensors (humidity, temperature, light) reduce external leakage.
- 16-bit RISC architecture executes instructions faster → returns to sleep quickly.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Software Stack Layers:**
1. **Application layer** – Sensing, actuation logic.
2. **Middleware** – Routing (e.g., CTP), aggregation, time sync (FTSP).
3. **OS kernel** – Scheduler, drivers.
4. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** – GPIO, ADC, radio primitives.
5. **Physical hardware**.

**TinyOS:**
- Written in **nesC** (C dialect for event-driven systems).
- **Component-based** model: *configurations* and *modules* wired together.
- **Event-driven scheduler** with two-level concurrency: tasks (deferred) and hardware event handlers.
- Footprint: ~400 bytes RAM, ~16 KB flash.

**Contiki:**
- Written in standard C with **protothreads** for stackless threads.
- **Multi-threading** support; optional *uIP* TCP/IP stack.
- Provides **COOJA** simulator.
- Footprint: ~2 KB RAM, ~30 KB flash.

**Comparison:** TinyOS = simpler, smaller, more popular in research. Contiki = more flexible, supports IP (6LoWPAN), better for IoT convergence.

*(14 marks: 7 for comparative table + conclusion, 7 for OS architecture + comparison.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not write `|x|`** for absolute value in tables. Use $\vert x \vert$ — valuators dock 0.5 marks for markdown breakage.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers (mA, mAh, hours) is the #1 mark-loss reason in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Naming only "sensors and radio"** without specifying ADC, MCU, and power management loses 2 of 7 marks on architecture questions.
> 4. **Confusing program flash and RAM** in comparisons is a frequent error — flash holds *code*, RAM holds *runtime data*.
> 5. **Skipping the comment/discussion** at the end of numerical problems loses the last 1–2 marks; KTU awards marks for engineering insight, not just arithmetic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Memorize Before Exam)**

- ☐ A sensor node has **4 subsystems**: Sensing, Processing, Communication, Power.
- ☐ Communication (radio) consumes **70–80%** of total node energy.
- ☐ Typical mote size is **matchbox-sized** with a **3.3 V** supply.
- ☐ MicaZ: ATmega128L + CC2420; TelosB: MSP430 + CC2420 (lowest sleep current).
- ☐ **Duty cycle** $D = T_{active} / T_{cycle}$ is the master energy knob.
- ☐ Lifetime equation: $L = C_{bat} / I_{avg}$ where $I_{avg} = I_s + D(I_a - I_s)$.
- ☐ TinyOS uses **nesC + event-driven** scheduler; Contiki uses **C + protothreads**.
- ☐ **Free-space path loss** scales as $20\log(d) + 20\log(f) + 32.45$ dB.
- ☐ Sampling must obey **Nyquist**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- ☐ Power: $P = V \cdot I$ (instantaneous); $P_{avg}$ is integral over a cycle.
- ☐ Design drivers: **energy, size, cost, environment, deployment lifetime**.
- ☐ Always convert **years → hours** before plugging into lifetime formula.
- ☐ Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ (LaTeX) — never raw `|x|` — in markdown tables.
- ☐ Three radio states: **TX, RX, Sleep**; sleep current is in **µA**, not mA.
- ☐ **In-network processing** and **data aggregation** are key to extending WSN lifetime.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Protocols in WSNs: MAC, Routing, and Transport Layer Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT863 - ADHOC AND WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Communication Protocols in WSNs: MAC, Routing, and Transport Layer Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Introduction to Communication Protocols in WSNs

**Communication Protocols** in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) constitute the structured set of rules and conventions that govern how sensor nodes exchange, route, and reliably transport data across a resource-constrained, energy-limited, and often unreliable wireless medium. Unlike traditional wired or ad-hoc networks, WSN protocols must be designed to optimize three critical, often conflicting objectives: **energy efficiency**, **scalability**, and **reliability** under strict hardware limitations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Communication protocols in WSNs operate across all three layers of the protocol stack—MAC (Medium Access Control), Network/Routing, and Transport—each tailored to address the unique constraints of sensor nodes: limited battery, low bandwidth, small memory, and lossy wireless channels.*

### 1.2 The Three-Layer Communication Stack — A Bird's-Eye View

| Layer | Primary Concern | Why It Matters in WSNs |
|:------|:----------------|:-----------------------|
| **MAC Layer** | Channel access, collision avoidance, duty cycling | Idle listening wastes up to 50–70% of node energy |
| **Routing Layer** | Path selection from source to sink | Sensor data must traverse multi-hop paths efficiently |
| **Transport Layer** | End-to-end reliability, congestion control | Lossy wireless + multi-hop = high packet loss; reliability is critical |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Emergency Room" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine a **large hospital emergency ward** with hundreds of patients (sensor nodes), a few doctors (base station/sink), and strict rules to manage communication:
>
> 1. **MAC Layer = Ward Silence Protocol** — Doctors rotate listening hours; nurses (nodes) only speak when it's their scheduled slot to avoid overlapping chatter (collisions) and wasted hearing (idle listening).
> 2. **Routing Layer = Patient Referral System** — Critical cases are referred to specialists (cluster heads) who then forward summarized reports to senior doctors (sink), avoiding every nurse running to the chief directly.
> 3. **Transport Layer = Medical Report Acknowledgment** — When a nurse hands over a report, they wait for a "Received" slip; if the slip doesn't arrive, they resend. This ensures no critical patient data is lost.

This analogy captures the **energy awareness**, **hierarchical data aggregation**, and **retransmission logic** that defines WSN communication protocols.

---

## 1.4 Design Challenges in WSN Communication Protocols

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Challenges to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Energy Constraints:** Sensor nodes are battery-powered; replacing batteries is infeasible in remote deployments.
> - **Limited Bandwidth:** Wireless channel capacity is shared and limited.
> - **Unreliable Links:** Wireless channels are prone to interference, fading, and high Bit Error Rate (BER), often **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁵**.
> - **Scalability:** Networks may grow to thousands of nodes.
> - **Data Redundancy:** Neighboring nodes often sense correlated data, requiring in-network processing.
> - **Duty Cycling:** Nodes alternate between sleep and active states to conserve energy.

---

## 1.5 MAC Layer Protocols — Formal Definition

The **Medium Access Control (MAC) protocol** in WSNs manages when and how sensor nodes access the shared wireless channel, with the principal objective of **minimizing energy consumption** while ensuring fair and collision-free communication. Standard MAC protocols designed for Wi-Fi (e.g., IEEE 802.11) are unsuitable because they assume continuous power supply and emphasize throughput and latency over energy savings.

**Key MAC Design Goals:**
- **Energy Efficiency** (primary)
- **Scalability** to dynamic topology
- **Collision Avoidance**
- **Overhead Reduction** (control packets)
- **Self-Organization**

---

## 1.6 Routing Layer Protocols — Formal Definition

**Routing protocols** in WSNs define the path that data packets traverse from source sensor nodes to the sink (base station). Unlike traditional IP-based routing, WSN routing is **data-centric**, **energy-aware**, and often **application-specific**, leveraging techniques like data aggregation, clustering, and geographic addressing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** WSN routing is classified based on network structure:
> - **Flat routing** (e.g., SPIN, Directed Diffusion)
> - **Hierarchical/Cluster-based** (e.g., LEACH, PEGASIS)
> - **Location-based** (e.g., GAF, GEAR)
> - **QoS-aware** (e.g., SPEED, SAR)

---

## 1.7 Transport Layer Protocols — Formal Definition

**Transport layer protocols** in WSNs provide **end-to-end reliability**, **congestion control**, and **data integrity** between source nodes and the sink. They address two primary issues: **packet loss recovery** and **congestion mitigation**, both of which are exacerbated by the multi-hop, wireless nature of WSNs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard TCP is NOT suitable for WSNs** because:
> - TCP's retransmission mechanism is energy-expensive.
> - TCP assumes end-to-end acknowledgments, which drain battery in multi-hop paths.
> - WSNs often tolerate some packet loss (lossy-by-design), but TCP treats all loss as congestion.
> - WSN data is often correlated, so individual packet loss is less critical.

**Custom WSN Transport Protocols** include **RMST (Reliable Multi-Segment Transport)**, **PSFQ (Pump Slowly, Fetch Quickly)**, and **CODA (Congestion Detection and Avoidance)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 MAC Layer Protocols — Detailed Taxonomy

MAC protocols for WSNs are broadly classified based on how nodes access the channel:

### 2.1.1 Contention-Based MAC Protocols

Nodes compete for channel access; collisions are resolved via backoff mechanisms.

#### **A. S-MAC (Sensor-MAC)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **S-MAC** is a **synchronous, duty-cycled** MAC protocol that reduces idle listening by putting nodes into periodic **sleep-wake cycles**.

**Operational Principles:**

1. **Periodic Listen and Sleep:**
   - Each node cycles through **Listen** (active) and **Sleep** (low-power) periods.
   - Duty cycle = $\frac{T_{listen}}{T_{listen} + T_{sleep}}$
   - Typical values: 10–50% duty cycle saves **up to 50% energy**.

2. **Synchronization:**
   - Nodes exchange **SYNC packets** to align their sleep schedules with neighbors.
   - Virtual clusters of synchronized nodes are formed.

3. **RTS/CTS Mechanism:**
   - Uses **Request-To-Send** and **Clear-To-Send** handshake to avoid hidden terminal problem.
   - Non-persistent CSMA used for SYNC packets.

4. **Overhearing Avoidance:**
   - When a node hears an RTS/CTS not destined to it, it goes to **sleep** until the transmission ends.

5. **Message Passing:**
   - Long messages are fragmented into bursts; all bursts transmitted in one listen period to amortize control overhead.

**Drawbacks of S-MAC:**
- Fixed duty cycle is not adaptive to traffic load.
- Synchronization overhead is significant in highly mobile networks.
- Sleep latency increases end-to-end delay.

#### **B. B-MAC (Berkeley-MAC)**

B-MAC uses **Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)** and **Low Power Listening (LPL)**:
- Each transmitted packet is preceded by a **long preamble** longer than the receiver's sleep period.
- Receivers wake periodically to sample the channel; if preamble is detected, they stay awake to receive.
- **Advantage:** No synchronization required; highly adaptive.
- **Disadvantage:** Long preambles waste energy at the transmitter.

#### **C. IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee MAC**

- Standard for **Low-Rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPANs)**.
- Uses **slotted CSMA/CA** in the **beacon-enabled mode** and **unslotted CSMA/CA** in non-beacon mode.
- Operates in the **2.4 GHz** industrial, scientific, and medical (ISM) band, with **16 channels**, each providing **250 kbps** data rate.
- **Superframe structure:** bounded by **beacons** from the PAN coordinator; contains **Contention Access Period (CAP)** and optional **Contention-Free Period (CFP)** with **Guaranteed Time Slots (GTSs)**.

**Superframe Duration Formula:**

$$SD = \text{BeaconOrder} \times 15.36 \text{ ms (for 2.4 GHz)}$$

where Beacon Order (BO) ranges from 0 to 14.

---

### 2.1.2 Schedule-Based (TDMA-based) MAC Protocols

Nodes are assigned dedicated time slots; no contention.

#### **A. LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy) — MAC Perspective**

While primarily a routing protocol, LEACH uses **TDMA schedules** within each cluster:
- The cluster head assigns each member a unique time slot.
- Members transmit only in their slot, then sleep → energy savings.
- Slots are reused across non-adjacent clusters to improve spatial efficiency.

#### **B. SMACS (Self-Organizing Medium Access Control for Sensor Networks)**

- Establishes a **collision-free, time-synchronized frame structure** without a global clock.
- Neighbor discovery occurs by random wake-up; pairs of nodes negotiate a frequency and time slot pair.

**Key Formulas in MAC Protocol Analysis:**

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|:----------|:--------|:------------|
| **Duty Cycle (DC)** | $DC = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{cycle}}$ | Fraction of time node is awake |
| **Energy per Bit** | $E_b = \frac{P_{tx} \cdot T_{tx}}{L}$ | Energy consumed to transmit $L$ bits |
| **Idle Listening Cost** | $E_{idle} = P_{idle} \cdot T_{listen}$ | Wasted energy when no traffic |
| **Throughput** | $\eta = \frac{L_{data}}{T_{total}} \times N_{slots}$ | Effective data rate per slot |
| **Channel Utilization** | $U = \frac{T_{useful}}{T_{frame}}$ | Fraction of time used for data |
| **Collision Probability (CSMA)** | $P_c = 1 - (1 - \tau)^{n-1}$ | With $n$ nodes, transmission prob. $\tau$ |
| **Frame Time (802.15.4)** | $T_{frame} = \text{SO} \times 15.36 \text{ ms}$ | Superframe duration with SO=SuperframeOrder |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a real deployment of 1000 nodes with 1% duty cycle and 250 kbps channel, total network throughput is roughly $1000 \times 0.01 \times 250 \text{ kbps} = 2.5 \text{ Mbps}$ aggregated. This is why **in-network aggregation** is vital.

---

## 2.2 Routing Layer Protocols — Detailed Taxonomy

### 2.2.1 Data-Centric Routing

#### **A. SPIN (Sensor Protocols for Information via Negotiation)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SPIN family** uses **meta-data negotiation** to eliminate redundant data transmission — a hallmark of WSNs.

**Three-Stage Handshake:**
1. **ADV** — Node advertises new data using meta-data descriptor.
2. **REQ** — Interested neighbor sends request.
3. **DATA** — Source sends the actual data.

**Variants:** SPIN-PP (point-to-point), SPIN-EC (energy-conscious), SPIN-BC (broadcast), SPIN-RL (reliable).

**Advantage:** Energy-efficient by avoiding implosion (duplicate messages to same node) and overlap (multiple nodes sensing same event).

**Disadvantage:** Cannot guarantee data delivery if the sink is far from the source and intermediate nodes don't advertise.

#### **B. Directed Diffusion (DD)**

- **Sink-centric, attribute-based** routing.
- Sink **floods "interests"** (named data queries) through the network.
- Sources **reinforce** one path via **positive/negative reinforcements**.
- Intermediate nodes perform **in-network aggregation** to consolidate data.

**Key Phases:**
1. **Interest Propagation** — Sink broadcasts interest gradients.
2. **Data Propagation** — Sources send data along gradients.
3. **Reinforcement** — Sink chooses one path (e.g., lowest latency) and reinforces it.

**Gradient Field:** Each node maintains a gradient toward the sink with attributes like **direction, duration, and data rate**.

---

### 2.2.2 Hierarchical (Cluster-Based) Routing

#### **A. LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LEACH is the foundational cluster-based protocol** — it appeared in nearly every KTU past paper.

**Two-Phase Operation:**

1. **Set-Up Phase:**
   - Each node picks a random number $r \in [0,1]$.
   - If $r < T(n)$, node becomes a **Cluster Head (CH)** for the current round.

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \frac{p}{1 - p \cdot \left(r \mod \frac{1}{p}\right)} & \text{if } n \in G \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where:
- $p$ = desired percentage of cluster heads (typically 0.05)
- $r$ = current round number
- $G$ = set of nodes that have not been CHs in the last $1/p$ rounds

2. **Steady-State Phase:**
   - CHs create a **TDMA schedule** and broadcast it to cluster members.
   - Members send data only in their assigned slot using **DS-SS (Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum)**.
   - CHs **aggregate** data and send to sink (often single-hop).

**Energy Model Used in LEACH:**

$$E_{tx}(k, d) = \begin{cases} k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2 & d < d_0 \\ k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4 & d \geq d_0 \end{cases}$$

$$E_{rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

**Threshold Distance:**

$$d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}$$

**Typical Parameter Values:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|:----------|:-------|:------|
| Transmitter/Receiver Electronics | $E_{elec}$ | 50 nJ/bit |
| Free Space Amplifier | $\epsilon_{fs}$ | 10 pJ/bit/m² |
| Multipath Amplifier | $\epsilon_{mp}$ | 0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴ |
| Data Aggregation | $E_{DA}$ | 5 nJ/bit/signal |
| Threshold Distance | $d_0$ | ~87 m |

#### **B. PEGASIS (Power-Efficient Gathering in Sensor Information Systems)**

- Improvement over LEACH; forms a **chain** of nodes instead of clusters.
- Each node transmits only to a **close neighbor**, aggregates, and forwards.
- Only **one node** transmits to the sink per round.
- **Energy savings over LEACH:** approximately 100–300% in many simulations.

**Chain Construction:** Greedy algorithm starting from the farthest node from sink.

**Token Passing:** A control token circulates; the node holding it transmits per round.

#### **C. TEEN (Threshold-sensitive Energy-Efficient sensor Network)**

- Reactive, event-driven hierarchical protocol.
- Two thresholds:
  - **Hard Threshold (HT):** Absolute value to trigger transmission.
  - **Soft Threshold (ST):** Small change in sensed value to trigger re-transmission.
- **Suitable for time-critical applications** (e.g., fire detection).
- **Drawback:** If thresholds not reached, no data is sent — not suitable for periodic reporting.

#### **D. APTEEN (Adaptive Periodic Threshold-sensitive Energy-Efficient sensor Network)**

- Hybrid of LEACH and TEEN.
- Captures both **periodic data** and **event-driven** responses.
- Uses **count time** parameter to limit transmissions.

---

### 2.2.3 Location-Based Routing

#### **A. GEAR (Geographic and Energy-Aware Routing)**

- Uses **location information** (via GPS) to route queries to target regions.
- Combines geographic forwarding with **energy-aware neighbor selection**.
- **Two phases:** (1) Forwarding to target region, (2) Dissemination within region.

**Cost Function:**

$$\text{Cost}(N_i) = \alpha \cdot d(N_i, N_{\text{closest to target}}) + (1-\alpha) \cdot E_{\text{consumed}}(N_i)$$

where $\alpha$ balances distance vs. energy.

#### **B. GAF (Geographic Adaptive Fidelity)**

- Divides area into **fixed grids**; equivalent nodes (same grid) can be put to sleep.
- **Energy savings** at the cost of routing precision.

---

### 2.2.4 QoS-Based Routing

#### **A. SPEED**

- Provides **end-to-end soft real-time** guarantees.
- Uses **Stateless Nondeterministic Geo-Forwarding (SNGF)**.
- Maintains a desired **delivery speed** across the network.

#### **B. SAR (Sequential Assignment Routing)**

- Creates **multiple trees** rooted at the sink, each with a different QoS metric and energy priority.
- Selects path based on weighted QoS metric and residual energy.

---

## 2.3 Transport Layer Protocols — Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 RMST (Reliable Multi-Segment Transport)

- **Designed for directed diffusion** environments.
- Operates at the **sink and source**; provides **hop-by-hop** and **end-to-end** reliability.
- Uses **caching** at intermediate nodes to enable **local recovery** of lost fragments.
- **Three modes:** (1) Caching, (2) Non-caching, (3) Hop-by-hop recovery.

### 2.3.2 PSFQ (Pump Slowly, Fetch Quickly)

- Designed for **downstream data dissemination** (sink to sensors).
- **Pump Slowly:** Inject packets into the network at a slow, controlled rate to avoid congestion.
- **Fetch Quickly:** If a node detects a gap in sequence numbers, it aggressively fetches missing packets from neighbors via **NACK-based recovery**.

**Operation Flow:**
1. Sink "pumps" data at low rate.
2. Intermediate nodes cache and forward.
3. On loss detection, NACK sent upstream to quickly recover.
4. Trade-off: balances reliability with energy efficiency.

### 2.3.3 CODA (Congestion Detection and Avoidance)

- Addresses **upstream congestion** in WSNs.
- Three mechanisms:
  1. **Receiver-based congestion detection** — uses **queue length** and **channel sampling**.
  2. **Open-loop hop-by-hop backpressure** — sends backpressure signals to throttle senders.
  3. **Closed-loop multi-source regulation** — uses **ACK** to regulate source rate.

### 2.3.4 ESRT (Event-to-Sink Reliable Transport)

- **Sink-centric reliability**; sink controls the reporting frequency of all sources.
- Achieves a target reliability $\eta$ (e.g., 99% packet delivery) by adjusting sensor reporting rate $f$.
- Five operating regions: **(NCR, OR, CR, (CR,OR), SOR** — non-congestion, congestion, etc.

**Reliability Metric:**

$$\eta = \frac{e}{r}$$

where $e$ = number of received packets, $r$ = total number of packets sent.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for last-minute revision.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $DC = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{cycle}}$ | Duty cycle of a node | dimensionless |
| 2 | $E_{tx}(k,d) = kE_{elec} + k\epsilon d^n$ | Transmission energy | Joules |
| 3 | $E_{rx}(k) = kE_{elec}$ | Reception energy | Joules |
| 4 | $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}}$ | Crossover distance | meters |
| 5 | $T(n) = \frac{p}{1-p(r \bmod 1/p)}$ | LEACH CH threshold | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_c = 1-(1-\tau)^{n-1}$ | CSMA collision prob. | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta = e/r$ | ESRT reliability | dimensionless |
| 8 | $E_{total} = E_{tx} + E_{rx} + E_{sleep} + E_{idle}$ | Total energy per round | Joules |
| 9 | $L_{chain} = \sum d_i$ | PEGASIS chain length | meters |
| 10 | $SD = BO \times 15.36$ ms | 802.15.4 superframe | ms |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These protocols power:
> - **Precision Agriculture** (LEACH, ZigBee) — soil moisture, temperature monitoring.
> - **Structural Health Monitoring** (Directed Diffusion, RMST) — bridge vibration sensing.
> - **Smart Grids** (TEEN, IEEE 802.15.4) — outage detection with hard thresholds.
> - **Military Surveillance** (SPIN, PEGASIS) — energy-efficient reconnaissance.
> - **Healthcare Body Area Networks** (B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4) — wearable sensors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Consumption per Round in LEACH

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic 14-mark derivation** frequently asked in KTU exams. Master it.

**Given:**
- $N$ = total number of sensor nodes
- $k$ = number of cluster heads per round (typically $k = Np$)
- $N/k$ = nodes per cluster on average
- $E_{DA}$ = data aggregation energy per bit
- Each CH compresses $N/k$ packets into 1 fixed-length packet of $k$ bits

---

### Step 1: Energy Spent by a Non-CH Node

A non-CH node transmits $k$ bits to its CH at distance $d_{toCH}$.

$$E_{nonCH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^2$$

We assume intra-cluster distance follows uniform distribution; mean squared distance to CH is approximated as $\frac{M^2}{2\pi k}$ in an $M \times M$ field, so:

$$E[d_{toCH}^2] = \frac{M^2}{2\pi k}$$

> **Logic:** The expected squared Euclidean distance from a uniform random point to a fixed point in a square of area $M^2$ is $M^2/6$, but for cluster-based analysis, integrating over Voronoi cells gives the $1/(2\pi k)$ factor.

---

### Step 2: Total Energy of All Non-CH Nodes in One Round

$$E_{nonCH,total} = (N - k) \cdot \left[k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^2}{2\pi k}\right]$$

$$E_{nonCH,total} = (N - k) \cdot k \cdot \left[E_{elec} + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^2}{2\pi k}\right]$$

---

### Step 3: Energy Spent by a Cluster Head

A CH receives $N/k - 1$ packets, aggregates them, and sends to sink at distance $d_{toBS}$.

$$E_{CH} = \left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{N}{k} \cdot k \cdot E_{DA} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^4$$

> **Logic:** Reception cost = $E_{elec}$ per bit; aggregation cost = $E_{DA}$ per signal (one per source); transmission uses multipath model since $d_{toBS}$ is typically large.

---

### Step 4: Total Energy of All Cluster Heads in One Round

$$E_{CH,total} = k \cdot E_{CH}$$

---

### Step 5: Total Energy per Round

$$E_{round} = E_{CH,total} + E_{nonCH,total}$$

$$E_{round} = k \cdot \left[\left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) k E_{elec} + N E_{DA} + k E_{elec} + k \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^4\right] + (N - k) \cdot k \left[E_{elec} + \epsilon_{fs} \frac{M^2}{2\pi k}\right]$$

$$E_{round} = N k E_{elec} - k^2 E_{elec} + N k E_{DA} + k^2 E_{elec} + k^2 \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^4 + (N - k) k E_{elec} + (N - k) \frac{M^2 \epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi}$$

Simplifying (the $-k^2 E_{elec}$ and $+k^2 E_{elec}$ cancel, and the $(N-k)kE_{elec} + NkE_{elec} = 2NkE_{elec} - k^2 E_{elec}$):

$$E_{round} = 2 N k E_{elec} - k^2 E_{elec} + N k E_{DA} + k^2 \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^4 + (N - k) \frac{M^2 \epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi}$$

> **Final Form (commonly quoted):**
> $$E_{round} \approx 2 N k E_{elec} + N k E_{DA} + k \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^4 + N \frac{M^2 \epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi}$$
> (Dropping lower-order $k^2$ terms when $k \ll N$.)

---

### Step 6: Optimal Number of Cluster Heads

To minimize $E_{round}$, take the derivative with respect to $k$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dE_{round}}{dk} = 2NE_{elec} + NE_{DA} + \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^4 - \frac{N M^2 \epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi k^2} = 0$$

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N M^2 \epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi (2 E_{elec} + E_{DA} + \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^4 / N)}}$$

For large $N$ and typical values, $k_{opt} \approx \sqrt{N} \cdot C$ for some constant $C$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Throughput of S-MAC with Periodic Sleep

**Given:**
- Listen interval: $T_L$
- Sleep interval: $T_S$
- Total cycle: $T_{cyc} = T_L + T_S$
- Synchronization overhead: $t_{sync}$

**Effective channel access time per cycle:**

$$T_{eff} = T_L - t_{sync} - t_{RTS/CTS}$$

**Duty Cycle:**

$$DC = \frac{T_L}{T_{cyc}} = \frac{T_L}{T_L + T_S}$$

**Energy Saved Compared to Always-On MAC:**

$$\text{Savings} = 1 - DC = \frac{T_S}{T_L + T_S}$$

**Example:** For $T_L = 100$ ms and $T_S = 900$ ms, $DC = 10\%$, savings = 90%.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: PEGASIS Energy Savings vs. LEACH

**Setup:** $N$ nodes in a $M \times M$ field; sink at corner; data packet of $k$ bits.

**LEACH Energy per Round (approximate):**

$$E_{LEACH} \approx 2 N k E_{elec} + N k E_{DA} + k \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^4 + N \frac{M^2 \epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi}$$

**PEGASIS Energy per Round:**

Each node (except leader) transmits to one close neighbor at distance $d \approx \frac{M}{\sqrt{N}}$ (chain spacing).

$$E_{PEGASIS} = (N-1) \cdot \left[k E_{elec} + k \epsilon_{fs} d^2\right] + k E_{elec} + k \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^4$$

$$E_{PEGASIS} \approx (N-1) k E_{elec} + (N-1) k \epsilon_{fs} \frac{M^2}{N} + k E_{elec} + k \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^4$$

**Ratio (simplified for large N, ignoring aggregation/amplification in long-distance CH-to-BS):**

$$\frac{E_{PEGASIS}}{E_{LEACH}} \approx \frac{1}{2} \text{ to } \frac{1}{3}$$

> **Conclusion:** PEGASIS reduces energy consumption by approximately 50–67% compared to LEACH, at the cost of higher per-node delay and increased message size.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the importance of energy efficiency in MAC protocol design for WSNs. List any two MAC protocols designed for WSNs.**

**Model Answer:**

Energy efficiency is the **primary design goal** of WSN MAC protocols because sensor nodes are **battery-powered** and deployed in **inaccessible or hostile environments** where battery replacement is infeasible. The major sources of energy waste in MAC protocols are:
1. **Idle listening** (50–70% of total energy) — nodes listening to an idle channel.
2. **Collisions** — corrupted packets require retransmission.
3. **Overhearing** — receiving packets destined for other nodes.
4. **Control packet overhead** — RTS/CTS/ACK consume bandwidth and energy.

Two MAC protocols for WSNs:
- **S-MAC**: Uses periodic sleep-wake cycles and SYNC/RTS/CTS handshake to reduce idle listening.
- **B-MAC**: Uses Low Power Listening (LPL) with extended preambles; no synchronization needed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse B-MAC with S-MAC and write "B-MAC uses sleep-wake cycles." The correct distinguishing feature is **LPL with long preambles**, not synchronized sleep.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between data-centric and address-centric routing. Give one example protocol for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Address-Centric Routing | Data-Centric Routing |
|:-------|:------------------------|:---------------------|
| **Addressing** | Uses unique node IDs (IP-like) | Uses attribute-value pairs (e.g., "temperature > 30°C") |
| **Query** | Node-to-node communication | Sink queries the network for data, not a node |
| **Aggregation** | Limited, packet-level | Strong, in-network aggregation |
| **Redundancy Handling** | Each packet routed individually | Filters redundant data via negotiation |
| **Example** | AODV, DSR (MANET protocols) | SPIN, Directed Diffusion (WSN) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Don't write "data-centric is faster." WSN routing prioritizes **energy efficiency**, not speed. A correct comparison must mention **attribute-based naming** and **in-network aggregation**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working of the **LEACH protocol** with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **energy consumption equation for one round of LEACH** and show how the optimal number of cluster heads is determined. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)** is a hierarchical, cluster-based routing protocol for WSNs that uses **probabilistic cluster head selection** and **data aggregation** to extend network lifetime.

**Working of LEACH:**

LEACH operates in **two phases**, repeated in **rounds**:

**Phase 1: Set-Up Phase**
- Each sensor node chooses a random number $r \in [0,1]$.
- The node becomes a **Cluster Head (CH)** for the current round if $r < T(n)$, where:

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \frac{p}{1 - p \cdot (r \bmod 1/p)} & n \in G \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

- CHs broadcast an **ADV (advertisement)** message using CSMA MAC.
- Non-CH nodes choose their CH based on **received signal strength** and send a **JOIN-REQ** message.
- The CH creates a **TDMA schedule** and assigns each member a unique time slot.

**Phase 2: Steady-State Phase**
- Each member transmits its data to the CH in its assigned slot using **DS-SS** (Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum) to reduce inter-cluster interference.
- The CH receives all data, **aggregates** it (e.g., averages, removes redundancy), and transmits the compressed data to the **Base Station (BS)**.
- After a fixed duration, the network returns to the set-up phase for the next round.

**Diagram (already shown in Section 4.3):** Refer to the LEACH Operation Flowchart.

**Advantages of LEACH:**
1. **Energy efficient** — CHs aggregate data, reducing transmissions.
2. **Self-organizing** — no global coordination needed.
3. **Load balancing** — CH role rotates each round.
4. **Scalable** — works for large networks.

**Limitations of LEACH:**
1. **Single-hop CH-to-BS** — energy expensive for distant CHs.
2. **Uneven cluster distribution** — probabilistic selection may create hot spots.
3. **Not suitable for mobile nodes** — assumes static deployment.
4. **Hot-spot problem** — CHs near sink die faster due to relaying.
5. **Cluster formation overhead** — repeated in every round.

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

The derivation is fully worked out in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Recap of the key result:

**Energy per round:**

$$E_{round} \approx 2 N k E_{elec} + N k E_{DA} + k \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^4 + N \frac{M^2 \epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi}$$

**Optimization:** To find the optimal number of cluster heads $k_{opt}$ that minimizes $E_{round}$, we differentiate with respect to $k$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dE_{round}}{dk} = 2NE_{elec} + NE_{DA} + \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^4 - \frac{N M^2 \epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi k^2} = 0$$

Solving:

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N M^2 \epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi (2 E_{elec} + E_{DA} + \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^4 / N)}}$$

**For typical values:** $N = 100$, $M = 100$ m, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, $d_{toBS} = 100$ m, $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/signal:

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{100 \cdot 100^2 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-12}}{2\pi (2 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} + 5 \times 10^{-9} + 0.0013 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 100^4 / 100)}}$$

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{10^{-4}}{2\pi \cdot (1.05 \times 10^{-7} + 1.3 \times 10^{-10})}}$$

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{10^{-4}}{6.6 \times 10^{-7}}} \approx \sqrt{151.5} \approx 12.3$$

So approximately **5 cluster heads per 100 nodes** ($p \approx 5\%$) is optimal, matching the standard LEACH design choice.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Lose 2 Marks Here:**
> - Forgetting to specify that **$E_{DA}$ is per signal, not per bit** in the aggregation term. Some texts use 5 nJ/bit; the original Heinzelman paper uses 5 nJ/bit/signal.
> - Not justifying why $E[d_{toCH}^2] = M^2/(2\pi k)$. **Always mention the assumption** that nodes are uniformly distributed in the $M \times M$ field.
> - Skipping the differentiation step. Examiners want to see the calculus, not just the final formula.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare **S-MAC, B-MAC, and IEEE 802.15.4 MAC protocols** for WSNs in terms of synchronization, energy efficiency, and suitability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Directed Diffusion** and **SPIN** protocols. Discuss how Directed Diffusion handles data aggregation and reinforcement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | S-MAC | B-MAC | IEEE 802.15.4 |
|:--------|:------|:------|:--------------|
| **Synchronization** | Required (SYNC packets) | Not required (asynchronous) | Beacon-based (optional) |
| **Duty Cycling** | Periodic listen-sleep | Low Power Listening (LPL) | Beacon-enabled superframe |
| **Collision Avoidance** | RTS/CTS handshake | CCA + backoff | Slotted/Unslotted CSMA-CA |
| **Energy Efficiency** | High (fixed DC) | Adaptive (preamble-based) | High (GTS support) |
| **Latency** | Higher (sleep latency) | Lower for low traffic | Low (GTS guarantees) |
| **Complexity** | Medium | Low | High (full standard) |
| **Best For** | Periodic monitoring | Variable-traffic apps | Industrial/ZigBee, IoT |
| **Drawback** | Sync overhead | Long preamble waste | Beacon management overhead |

**Suitability Discussion:**

- **S-MAC** is best for **homogeneous, periodic sensing** applications (e.g., environmental monitoring) where traffic patterns are predictable and energy is paramount.
- **B-MAC** is suitable for **event-driven, low-duty-cycle** applications where traffic is bursty and synchronization overhead would dominate.
- **IEEE 802.15.4** is the **industry standard** for IoT/WPAN; suitable when **interoperability** with commercial ZigBee products is needed and real-time guarantees (GTS) are required.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often claim "S-MAC is better than B-MAC" — both have merit. Examiners want a **nuanced comparison** with application context.

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**SPIN (Sensor Protocols for Information via Negotiation):**

SPIN is a **flat, data-centric** protocol family that uses **meta-data negotiation** to eliminate redundant data transmissions. It addresses two key problems of traditional flooding:
- **Implosion:** Same data sent to same node multiple times.
- **Overlap:** Multiple nodes send overlapping data about the same event.

**Three-Stage Handshake (per transmission):**
1. **ADV:** Source broadcasts an advertisement containing **meta-data** describing the data (e.g., "temperature readings from Sector 4").
2. **REQ:** Neighbors interested in this data reply with a request.
3. **DATA:** Source transmits the actual data only to those who requested it.

**SPIN Variants:**
- **SPIN-PP:** Point-to-point communication.
- **SPIN-EC:** Energy-conscious (decides whether to participate based on residual energy).
- **SPIN-BC:** Broadcast channel.
- **SPIN-RL:** Reliable (loss detection via ACK).

---

**Directed Diffusion (DD):**

Directed Diffusion is a **sink-centric, attribute-based** routing protocol designed for WSNs. It is fundamentally different from SPIN in that **queries (interests) flow from sink to sources**, and **data flows back along established gradients**.

**Three Phases:**

**Phase 1: Interest Propagation**
- The sink **floods** "interests" — queries naming types of data (e.g., "report temperature every 10 seconds in region X").
- Each interest contains: **attribute-value pairs, duration, gradient direction**.
- A **gradient** is a reply direction established at each node toward the sink.

**Phase 2: Initial Data Propagation**
- Source nodes that match the interest begin sending **exploratory data** at a low rate along all gradients.
- Intermediate nodes cache and may **aggregate** data.
- The sink begins receiving data.

**Phase 3: Reinforcement**
- The sink identifies the **best path** (e.g., lowest latency, highest quality) and sends a **positive reinforcement** message.
- The chosen neighbor reinforces its upstream neighbor, and so on — establishing a single high-quality path.
- **Negative reinforcements** can be sent to suppress suboptimal paths.

**Data Aggregation in Directed Diffusion:**
- Intermediate nodes **cache and merge** data from multiple sources.
- Aggregation is performed at every hop along the reinforced path.
- This significantly reduces the number of packets reaching the sink and conserves energy.

**Reinforcement Mechanism (in detail):**
- The sink tracks latency of received exploratory data.
- The first neighbor that delivers low-latency data is reinforced.
- Reinforcement increases the data rate on that gradient (e.g., from 1 event/sec to 10 events/sec).
- The reinforced path is used for subsequent data delivery.

**Comparison (SPIN vs. DD):**

| Feature | SPIN | Directed Diffusion |
|:--------|:-----|:-------------------|
| **Data Flow** | Source-initiated | Sink-initiated (query-driven) |
| **Naming** | Meta-data | Attribute-value pairs |
| **Path Setup** | Hop-by-hop negotiation | Gradient establishment |
| **Aggregation** | No | Yes (in-network) |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Medium | High |
| **Suitability** | Event detection | Continuous query-based monitoring |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Lose 3 Marks Here:**
> - Confusing SPIN's "ADV-REQ-DATA" with Directed Diffusion's "interest-gradient-data." They are **fundamentally different** paradigms.
> - Forgetting to mention **gradient** — the central data structure in DD.
> - Claiming DD uses "proactive" routing — it is **reactive** (interests flooded only when needed).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist:**

### **MAC Layer — Key Points**
- ✅ S-MAC: synchronous, duty-cycled, RTS/CTS, virtual clusters, SYNC packets.
- ✅ B-MAC: asynchronous, Low Power Listening (LPL), long preambles, no sync.
- ✅ IEEE 802.15.4: 2.4 GHz, 250 kbps, slotted CSMA/CA, GTS, superframe structure.
- ✅ SMACS: TDMA-based, no global clock, frequency-slot pairs.
- ✅ Major energy wastes: idle listening, collisions, overhearing, control overhead.

### **Routing Layer — Key Points**
- ✅ **SPIN:** meta-data negotiation; ADV-REQ-DATA handshake; eliminates implosion/overlap.
- ✅ **Directed Diffusion:** sink-centric; interest propagation; gradients; reinforcement; in-network aggregation.
- ✅ **LEACH:** probabilistic CH selection using $T(n) = \frac{p}{1-p(r \bmod 1/p)}$; TDMA inside clusters; rotation of CH role.
- ✅ **PEGASIS:** chain-based; greedy chain construction; token passing; one node transmits to sink per round.
- ✅ **TEEN:** reactive; hard and soft thresholds; event-driven; not for periodic reports.
- ✅ **APTEEN:** hybrid of LEACH + TEEN; count-time parameter.
- ✅ **GEAR:** location-based; combines geo-forwarding with energy awareness.
- ✅ **SPEED:** soft real-time; stateless non-deterministic geo-forwarding.
- ✅ **SAR:** multi-path QoS; energy-aware tree construction.

### **Transport Layer — Key Points**
- ✅ **RMST:** upstream, hop-by-hop caching, end-to-end reliability.
- ✅ **PSFQ:** downstream, pump slowly + fetch quickly, NACK-based recovery.
- ✅ **CODA:** upstream, three mechanisms — receiver detection, open-loop backpressure, closed-loop ACK.
- ✅ **ESRT:** sink-centric, target reliability $\eta$, five operating regions.
- ✅ **GARUDA:** downstream, NACK + pulse-based recovery.
- ✅ **Why TCP is unsuitable:** end-to-end ACK energy cost; assumes congestion; treats all loss as congestion; no WSN awareness.

### **Key Energy Model Equations to Memorize**
- ✅ $E_{tx}(k,d) = kE_{elec} + k\epsilon_{fs}d^2$ (short range, free space)
- ✅ $E_{tx}(k,d) = kE_{elec} + k\epsilon_{mp}d^4$ (long range, multipath)
- ✅ $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}}$ (threshold distance)
- ✅ $E_{round} = 2NkE_{elec} + NkE_{DA} + k\epsilon_{mp}d_{toBS}^4 + \frac{NM^2\epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi}$

### **Numerical Values to Recall**
- ✅ $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit
- ✅ $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m²
- ✅ $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴
- ✅ $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/signal
- ✅ Optimal cluster ratio $p \approx 5\%$
- ✅ 802.15.4: 250 kbps, 2.4 GHz, 16 channels

### **Mnemonic to Remember Protocol Families**
- **"C-D-H-L"** for Routing: **C**luster (LEACH, PEGASIS, TEEN) → **D**ata-centric (SPIN, DD) → **H**ierarchical (LEACH) → **L**ocation-based (GEAR, GAF).
- **"C-S-T"** for MAC: **C**ontention (S-MAC, B-MAC) → **S**cheduled (SMACS, LEACH-TDMA) → **T**DMA-hybrid (802.15.4).
- **"R-P-C-E-G"** for Transport: **R**MST, **P**SFQ, **C**ODA, **E**SRT, **G**ARUDA.

> **Best of luck for your KTU exams — remember to draw neat diagrams in every long-answer question!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Management in WSNs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT863 - ADHOC AND WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy Management in WSNs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Management in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)** refers to the systematic set of hardware, protocol, and algorithmic strategies employed to monitor, control, and optimize the consumption of electrical energy across sensing, computation, communication, and storage subsystems of resource-constrained sensor nodes, with the primary objective of **maximizing the network's operational lifetime** while maintaining acceptable Quality of Service (QoS) parameters such as throughput, latency, and packet delivery ratio.

In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme, energy management is treated as a **cross-layer design problem** spanning the **physical, MAC, network, and application layers** of the OSI/ISO stack adapted for WSNs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Village Water-Carrier":** Imagine a village where one person (the sensor node) must walk long distances carrying water (data) to a central tank (the sink/base station). The water-carrier has only one small bottle of water for the entire trip. To survive, he must: (1) carry only the **most important** water samples (data aggregation), (2) **rest** at shaded spots instead of walking pointlessly (sleep/idle modes), (3) take **shorter, smarter paths** (energy-aware routing), and (4) refill the bottle from rain when possible (energy harvesting). Energy Management in WSNs is exactly this — getting the maximum useful work from a strictly limited "energy budget."

## 1.3 Why Energy Management is the Central Problem in WSNs

Wireless sensor nodes are typically **battery-powered** and deployed in hostile, inaccessible, or remote environments (forests, battlefields, ocean floors, volcanoes, structural interiors of bridges). **Replacing or recharging batteries is infeasible**, making energy the single most constrained resource.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Network Lifetime ($T_L$):** Time until the first node (or a fraction $\alpha$ of nodes) dies, measured in seconds, hours, or rounds.
> - **Energy per Bit ($E_b$):** Joules required to successfully deliver **one bit** of useful information to the sink.
> - **Energy per Useful Packet ($E_p$):** Joules consumed per correctly delivered application-layer packet.
> - **Duty Cycle ($\delta$):** Fraction of time a node is **active** vs. in sleep mode. $\delta = T_{active}/(T_{active}+T_{sleep})$.
> - **Residual Energy ($E_{res}$):** Energy remaining in a node at time $t$.
> - **Energy Efficiency ($\eta$):** Useful information delivered per Joule of energy consumed.

## 1.4 Sources of Energy Consumption in a Sensor Node

A typical sensor node (e.g., MicaZ, TelosB, IRIS) consumes energy in four primary subsystems:

| Subsystem | Typical Share of Energy | KTU-Relevant Operations |
|---|---|---|
| **Communication (Radio)** | **~70%–80%** | Transmit ($E_{Tx}$), Receive ($E_{Rx}$), Listen (idle), Sleep |
| **Sensing Subsystem** | ~5%–10% | ADC conversion, sensor sampling |
| **Computation (CPU/MCU)** | ~10%–15% | Processing, encryption, aggregation |
| **Storage & Others** | ~1%–5% | Flash read/write, LEDs |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often claim "the sensor (sensing subsystem) is the biggest energy consumer." This is **incorrect** for almost all practical WSN deployments — the **radio** dominates energy use. Always state the radio as the primary energy sink in exam answers.

## 1.5 Power States of a Radio

A sensor radio (e.g., CC2420) typically supports four power states, each with very different current draw:

1. **Transmit ($P_{Tx}$):** Highest power (e.g., 17.4 mA @ 0 dBm).
2. **Receive ($P_{Rx}$):** Slightly less than Transmit (e.g., 19.7 mA for CC2420, varies by chip).
3. **Idle / Listen:** Almost as costly as Receive because the radio circuits are fully ON, only the PA is off.
4. **Sleep:** Radio powered down — current drops to **microamps (µA)** (e.g., 1 µA for CC2420).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The First-Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman Model)

The most widely accepted analytical model for WSN energy analysis is the **First-Order Radio Model** proposed by Heinzelman, Chandrakasan, and Balakrishnan (2000) for the LEACH protocol. It is the **default model expected in KTU derivations**.

### 2.1.1 Assumptions
- Radio dissipates $E_{elec}$ (J/bit) to run the transmitter/receiver electronics.
- Transmit amplifier dissipates $\varepsilon_{amp}$ (J/bit/m²) for free-space ($d^2$ loss) or $\varepsilon_{amp,mp}$ (J/bit/m⁴) for multipath ($d^4$ loss).
- A threshold distance $d_0$ decides the channel model:

$$d_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}$$

### 2.1.2 Energy to Transmit an $k$-bit packet over distance $d$

$$\boxed{E_{Tx}(k,d) = E_{Tx\text{-}elec}(k) + E_{Tx\text{-}amp}(k,d)}$$

**For free-space model** ($d < d_0$):

$$E_{Tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$$

**For multipath model** ($d \geq d_0$):

$$E_{Tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$$

### 2.1.3 Energy to Receive an $k$-bit packet

$$E_{Rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

> [!NOTE]
