> **Key Insight:** Reception is **not free** — every node that overhears a packet (even one not destined to it) wastes $k \cdot E_{elec}$ of energy. This is the *motivation* behind protocols like S-MAC, B-MAC, and duty-cycled MACs.

## 2.2 Energy Breakdown of a Complete Data-Gathering Round

In a hierarchical (clustered) WSN with $N$ nodes and $k$ bits per node, energy consumed per round is:

$$E_{round} = N \cdot E_{sense} + N \cdot E_{Tx\text{-}to\text{-}CH} + k \cdot E_{DA} \cdot N + E_{CH\text{-}to\text{-}BS}$$

where $E_{DA}$ is the **data aggregation energy per bit** at the Cluster Head.

## 2.3 Battery Models Used in WSN Analysis

| Model | Equation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Ideal Linear Model** | $E(t) = E_0 - P \cdot t$ | First-order approximations |
| **Rakhmatov–Vrudhula Model** | Non-linear diffusion equation | Accurate lithium-ion |
| **Linear Discharge with Capacity $\sigma$** | $E_{discharge} = \sigma \cdot (1 - e^{-\alpha t})$ | Realistic curves |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024, the **Ideal Linear Battery Model** is the default unless otherwise specified.

## 2.4 Duty Cycling — The Single Most Effective Energy-Saving Technique

A node alternates between **Active** and **Sleep** states. The effective average power is:

$$P_{avg} = \delta \cdot P_{active} + (1-\delta) \cdot P_{sleep}$$

where $\delta$ is the duty cycle. With $P_{active} \approx 50$ mW and $P_{sleep} \approx 0.05$ mW, even a 1% duty cycle drastically extends lifetime.

## 2.5 Energy Harvesting — Extending Lifetime Beyond Battery

Energy harvesting scavenges ambient energy from:
- **Solar** (photovoltaic)
- **Vibration/Piezoelectric**
- **Thermal (TEG)**
- **RF (rectenna)**
- **Wind/Flow**

The lifetime becomes:

$$T_{lifetime} = \dfrac{E_{battery} + \int_{0}^{T} P_{harvest}(t)\, dt - E_{consumed}}{P_{consumed,avg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $P_{harvest,avg} \geq P_{consumed,avg}$, the node achieves **energy-neutral operation** (ENO) — theoretically infinite lifetime.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{Tx}(k,d) = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{fs}d^2$ | Transmit energy (free-space) | Joules |
| 2 | $E_{Tx}(k,d) = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{mp}d^4$ | Transmit energy (multipath) | Joules |
| 3 | $E_{Rx}(k) = kE_{elec}$ | Receive energy | Joules |
| 4 | $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$ | Crossover distance | m |
| 5 | $E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{r}(E_{Tx,ij} + E_{Rx,ij})$ | Network total energy per round | Joules |
| 6 | $T_L = E_{init}/P_{avg}$ | Network lifetime (linear) | seconds |
| 7 | $P_{avg} = \delta P_{on} + (1-\delta)P_{sleep}$ | Average power with duty cycle | Watts |
| 8 | $\eta = \text{Bits Delivered}/E_{consumed}$ | Energy efficiency | bits/Joule |
| 9 | $E_{harvest} = P_{src} \cdot \eta_{conv} \cdot A \cdot t$ | Harvested energy | Joules |
| 10 | $E_{sleep} \approx I_{sleep} \cdot V_{dd} \cdot t$ | Sleep-mode energy | Joules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Notation Safeguard:** All absolute values and divides in formulas use `\vert` / `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Defence & Battlefields:** Soldier-mounted biomedical sensors with energy harvesting from body heat/motion.
- **Precision Agriculture:** Soil-moisture sensors in remote farms — multi-year lifetime mandatory.
- **Structural Health Monitoring:** Sensors embedded inside bridges/buildings — battery replacement is impossible.
- **IoT Smart Cities:** Air-quality, traffic, and noise sensors with 10+ year deployments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Number of Clusters in LEACH-style Networks

This is a **classic KTU derivation** that appears in Part B questions (14 marks). Given the first-order radio model, the goal is to find the **optimal number of clusters ($k_{opt}$)** that minimizes total energy per round.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Cluster Topology

Assume $N$ sensor nodes are uniformly distributed in an $M \times M$ region. There are $k$ clusters, so each cluster has on average $N/k$ nodes (1 CH + $(N/k)-1$ non-CHs). One BS is at the center.

### Step 2 — Energy of a Non-CH Node per Round

Each non-CH transmits **one $l$-bit data packet** to its CH. Distance is approximated as the mean distance from a node to its CH (assumed within cluster radius $M/\sqrt{\pi k}$):

$$E_{nonCH} = l \cdot E_{elec} + l \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}$$

For a uniform distribution:

$$E[d_{toCH}^{2}] = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{M^{2}}{k}$$

So:

$$E_{nonCH} = l \cdot E_{elec} + l \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot \dfrac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}$$

### Step 3 — Total Non-CH Energy per Round

With $(N/k - 1)$ non-CHs per cluster:

$$E_{nonCH,total} = \left(\dfrac{N}{k} - 1\right) \cdot \left[lE_{elec} + l\varepsilon_{fs}\dfrac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}\right] \approx \dfrac{N}{k}\left[lE_{elec} + l\varepsilon_{fs}\dfrac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}\right]$$

(for large $N$, $\dfrac{N}{k}-1 \approx \dfrac{N}{k}$).

### Step 4 — Energy of a Cluster Head per Round

A CH receives $(N/k - 1)$ packets, aggregates them, and sends one fused $l$-bit packet to the BS at distance $d_{toBS}$ (use multipath model assuming $d_{toBS} > d_0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{CH} &= \left(\dfrac{N}{k} - 1\right) \cdot l \cdot E_{elec} + \left(\dfrac{N}{k}\right) \cdot l \cdot E_{DA} \\
&\quad + l \cdot E_{elec} + l \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Total Energy per Round (All Clusters)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{round} &= k \cdot E_{CH} + N \cdot E_{nonCH,total}/k \quad \text{[actually } k E_{CH} + (N-k) E_{nonCH} \text{]}\\
&\approx k \left[\dfrac{N}{k}lE_{elec} + \dfrac{N}{k}lE_{DA} + lE_{elec} + l\varepsilon_{mp}d_{toBS}^{4}\right] \\
&\quad + N \left[lE_{elec} + l\varepsilon_{fs}\dfrac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Simplifying (ignoring the $lE_{elec}$ terms as they become constant):

$$E_{round} \approx l\left[2NE_{elec} + NE_{DA} + k\varepsilon_{mp}d_{toBS}^{4} + N\varepsilon_{fs}\dfrac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}\right]$$

### Step 6 — Differentiate w.r.t. $k$ and Set to Zero

Let $f(k) = k\varepsilon_{mp}d_{toBS}^{4} + N\varepsilon_{fs}\dfrac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}$.

$$\dfrac{df}{dk} = \varepsilon_{mp}d_{toBS}^{4} - \dfrac{N\varepsilon_{fs}M^{2}}{2\pi k^{2}} = 0$$

Solving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k^{2} &= \dfrac{N\varepsilon_{fs}M^{2}}{2\pi \varepsilon_{mp}d_{toBS}^{4}}\\[4pt]
\boxed{k_{opt} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}}\sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}\dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **famous LEACH optimal cluster count**, frequently asked in KTU exams.

## 3.2 Derivation: Network Lifetime Under Linear Battery

**Given:** $N$ nodes, each with initial energy $E_0$. Per round, each node consumes $E_r$ Joules on average. Find lifetime in rounds.

**Step 1 — Energy consumed in $R$ rounds by all nodes:**

$$E_{consumed}(R) = N \cdot E_r \cdot R$$

**Step 2 — Set $E_{consumed}(R) = N \cdot E_0$ (all energy used):**

$$N \cdot E_r \cdot R_{max} = N \cdot E_0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_{max} = \dfrac{E_0}{E_r}$$

**Step 3 — Lifetime until first node dies (FND) — assuming balanced load:**

If load is perfectly balanced, FND = $R_{max}$. If a CH consumes more energy:

$$R_{FND} = \dfrac{E_0}{E_{CH,per\_round}}$$

**Step 4 — With duty cycling $\delta$:**

$$E_{r,new} = \delta \cdot E_{r,active} + (1-\delta) \cdot E_{r,sleep}$$

$$R_{FND,new} = \dfrac{E_0}{\delta E_{active} + (1-\delta)E_{sleep}}$$

This **derivation chain is a guaranteed 14-marker in KTU exams** if it appears.

## 3.3 Numerical Example — KTU Sample Problem

> **Problem (14-Mark Style):** A WSN has 100 nodes uniformly distributed in a $100 \times 100$ m² field. Each node generates a 4000-bit packet per round. $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/signal. BS is at $(50, 175)$ — assume multipath to BS. Find $k_{opt}$ and total energy per round.

**Solution Steps:**

**Step 1 — Compute $d_{toBS}$:**
The centroid of the field is at (50, 50), so $d_{toBS} = 175 - 50 = 125$ m.

**Step 2 — Apply $k_{opt}$ formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{opt} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{2\pi}}\sqrt{\dfrac{10\,\text{pJ}}{0.0013\,\text{pJ}}}\dfrac{100}{125^{2}}\\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{15.92} \cdot \sqrt{7692.3} \cdot \dfrac{100}{15625}\\[4pt]
&= 3.99 \cdot 87.7 \cdot 0.0064\\[4pt]
\boxed{k_{opt} &\approx 2.24 \approx 2 \text{ or } 3 \text{ clusters (take } 3 \text{ for even load)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total energy per round with $k = 3$:**

$E_{CH} \approx (100/3 - 1)(4000)(50 \text{ nJ}) + (100/3)(4000)(5 \text{ nJ}) + 4000(50 \text{ nJ}) + 4000(0.0013 \text{ pJ})(125^4)$

Numerically:
- Receive: $32.33 \times 4000 \times 50$ nJ = $6.466$ mJ
- Aggregate: $33.33 \times 4000 \times 5$ nJ = $0.667$ mJ
- Tx-electronics: $4000 \times 50$ nJ = $0.2$ mJ
- Tx-amp: $4000 \times 0.0013 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.44 \times 10^{8} = 4000 \times 0.0013 \times 2.44 \times 10^{-4}$ J $= 1.27 \times 10^{-3}$ J $= 1.27$ mJ

Per CH: $\approx 8.6$ mJ. Total for 3 CHs: $\approx 25.8$ mJ.

Non-CHs: $97 \times 4000 \times 50$ nJ $+ 97 \times 4000 \times 10$ pJ $\times (M^2/2\pi k) = 97 \times 0.2$ mJ $+ 97 \times 0.0004$ mJ $\times (10000/18.85) = 19.4 + 0.21 \approx 19.6$ mJ.

**Total $\approx 45.4$ mJ/round.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Energy Management Architecture in a Sensor Node

This shows how energy-aware decisions are made at every layer of a sensor node.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Energy Source Battery / Harvesting Unit] --> B[Power Management Unit PMU]
    B --> C[Voltage Regulators DC DC]
    C --> D1[Sensing Subsystem]
    C --> D2[Computation MCU]
    C --> D3[Radio Transceiver]
    C --> D4[Storage Flash]
    E1[Application Layer Data Aggregation] --> B
    E2[Network Layer Energy Aware Routing] --> B
    E3[MAC Layer Duty Cycling] --> B
    E4[Physical Layer Adaptive Tx Power] --> B
    B --> F[Residual Energy Monitor Eres]
    F --> G[Cross Layer Energy Manager]
    G --> E1
    G --> E2
    G --> E3
    G --> E4
```

## 4.2 State Diagram — Radio Power States (Duty Cycling)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Sleep
    Sleep --> Wakeup: Timer fires
    Wakeup --> Listen: CCA Clear
    Wakeup --> Sleep: CCA Busy backoff
    Listen --> Receive: Packet detected
    Listen --> Sleep: No packet timeout
    Receive --> Transmit: Data ready
    Receive --> Sleep: Done
    Transmit --> Sleep: ACK received
    Transmit --> Listen: Retransmit
    Sleep --> [*]: Energy depleted
```

## 4.3 Architecture Diagram — Energy Optimization Techniques by Layer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Application Layer] --> T1[Data Aggregation Compression]
    L2[Transport Layer] --> T2[Reliability Control]
    L3[Network Layer] --> T3[Energy Aware Routing PEGASIS LEACH]
    L4[MAC Layer] --> T4[Duty Cycling S MAC B MAC]
    L5[Physical Layer] --> T5[Adaptive Modulation Tx Power Control]
    L6[Hardware Layer] --> T6[Power Gating Energy Harvesting]
    T1 --> OUT[Extended Network Lifetime]
    T2 --> OUT
    T3 --> OUT
    T4 --> OUT
    T5 --> OUT
    T6 --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow — Energy Management Decision Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Network Initialization]) --> M1[Measure Residual Energy Eres per Node]
    M1 --> M2[Cluster Formation / Routing Setup]
    M2 --> M3[Schedule Active vs Sleep Slots]
    M3 --> M4{Data to Send}
    M4 -->|Yes| M5[Aggregate and Forward]
    M4 -->|No| M6[Enter Sleep Mode]
    M5 --> M7[Update Eres Eres minus Etx]
    M6 --> M8[Update Eres Eres minus Esleep]
    M7 --> M9{Round Complete}
    M8 --> M9
    M9 -->|No| M4
    M9 -->|Yes| M10{Re cluster or Rotate CHs}
    M10 --> M1
    M10 -->|Eres less than Eth| End([Node Dies - Report to Sink])
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams follow the KTU 2024 expected block-diagram style — clear rectangular nodes, single-direction arrows, and labelled transitions. Mermaid safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown in labels) are strictly followed.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2022]`

**Q: Define network lifetime in WSN. List the three commonly used lifetime definitions.**

**Model Answer:**
Network lifetime is the time duration during which a WSN continues to fulfill its intended application objectives. The three common definitions are:

1. **Time until the First Node Dies (FND):** The most stringent — network is considered alive only while every node is alive.
2. **Time until a fraction $\alpha$ of nodes die:** E.g., 50% nodes dead = "half-life." Useful for fault-tolerant applications.
3. **Time until Network Partition / Loss of Coverage:** When the network can no longer cover the sensing area or connect to the sink.

> [!NOTE]
> A complete answer should **define all three** with one-line each, then state the **trade-off**: FND gives the strictest QoS, while $\alpha$-based definitions reflect practical deployability.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between data aggregation and data-centric routing in WSNs.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Data Aggregation | Data-Centric Routing |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Combine/Compress data at intermediate nodes | Query-based data retrieval (e.g., Directed Diffusion) |
| **Where it happens** | Application / Network layer | Network layer |
| **Output** | Smaller fused packet | Attribute-matched data flow |
| **Energy saving** | Removes redundancy → fewer Tx | Avoids unnecessary routes → fewer hops |
| **Example** | LEACH cluster-head aggregation | SPIN, Directed Diffusion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "both are the same." They are **complementary** — data-centric routing **decides the path**, data aggregation **reduces the payload** along that path. Marks are lost for missing this distinction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **Module 3 internal choice** is offered between two questions. Both alternatives are solved below for full preparation.

---

### **Question B — Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the First-Order Radio Energy Model used in WSNs. Derive the optimal number of clusters for a clustered WSN and discuss the role of duty cycling in extending network lifetime.**

#### **Part (a) — First-Order Radio Model (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The First-Order Radio Model (Heinzelman, 2000) abstracts the radio as a simple energy sink with two components: **electronics energy** ($E_{elec}$) and **amplifier energy** ($\varepsilon \cdot d^n$).

**Energy to transmit a $k$-bit packet over distance $d$ meters:**

$$
E_{Tx}(k,d) = 
\begin{cases}
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}, & d < d_{0} \\[4pt]
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}, & d \geq d_{0}
\end{cases}
$$

**Energy to receive a $k$-bit packet:**

$$E_{Rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

**Crossover distance:**

$$d_{0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}$$

For $d < d_0$: line-of-sight, free-space path loss ($\alpha = 2$).
For $d \geq d_0$: multipath, severe fading ($\alpha = 4$).

**Typical values (used in KTU problems):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $E_{elec}$ | 50 nJ/bit |
| $\varepsilon_{fs}$ | 10 pJ/bit/m² |
| $\varepsilon_{mp}$ | 0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴ |
| $d_0$ | ≈ 87.7 m |
| $E_{DA}$ | 5 nJ/bit/signal |

**Insight:** The $d^4$ term shows that **long-range transmission is dramatically costlier** than short hops. This is the **theoretical justification for multi-hop and clustering**.

#### **Part (b) — Optimal Cluster Count & Duty Cycling (7 Marks)**

**Derivation of $k_{opt}$:**

Following the full derivation in Section 3.1, with $N$ nodes in an $M \times M$ area and BS at $d_{toBS}$:

$$\boxed{k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}}\sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}\dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}}$$

**Role of Duty Cycling:**

A node's average power with duty cycle $\delta$:

$$P_{avg} = \delta \cdot P_{active} + (1-\delta) \cdot P_{sleep}$$

**Example:** If $P_{active} = 60$ mW, $P_{sleep} = 0.06$ mW, and $\delta = 0.01$ (1%):

$$P_{avg} = 0.01 \cdot 60 + 0.99 \cdot 0.06 = 0.6 + 0.0594 = 0.66 \text{ mW}$$

**Lifetime extension factor:**

$$\dfrac{T_{L,\delta}}{T_{L,1.0}} = \dfrac{P_{active}}{P_{avg}} = \dfrac{60}{0.66} \approx 91\times$$

**Conclusion:** Combining clustering (reducing $d$) and duty cycling (reducing $t_{active}$) yields a **multiplicative energy saving**, often extending lifetimes from days to years.

---

### **Question B — Option B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Discuss energy harvesting techniques in WSNs. With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of an energy-harvesting sensor node and derive the energy-neutral operation condition.**

#### **Part (a) — Energy Harvesting Techniques (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Energy harvesting (EH) refers to **scavenging ambient energy** from the environment to power or recharge sensor nodes. The major sources are:

| Source | Power Density | Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| **Solar (outdoor)** | 15 mW/cm² (direct sun) | 10–100 mW/cm² typical |
| **Solar (indoor)** | 10–100 µW/cm² | Office lighting |
| **Vibration (piezo)** | 10–1000 µW/cm³ | Machinery, footsteps |
| **Thermal (TEG)** | 10–60 µW/cm² | Body heat, industrial |
| **RF (rectenna)** | 0.1–1 µW/cm² | TV towers, Wi-Fi |
| **Wind/Flow** | 1–10 mW/cm² | Ventilation ducts |

**Architectural diagram (functional):**

```
[Energy Source: Solar/Vibration/Thermal] 
            ↓
[Transducer: PV cell / Piezo / TEG] 
            ↓
[Energy Conditioning: Rectifier + DC-DC] 
            ↓
[Storage: Supercapacitor / Thin-film battery] 
            ↓
[Power Management IC] ──→ [Sensor Node]
            ↑
[MPPT / Load Matching Controller]
```

**MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking):** The conditioning stage uses MPPT algorithms (e.g., Perturb & Observe) to extract peak available power from the transducer regardless of varying source conditions (cloudy vs. sunny).

#### **Part (b) — Energy-Neutral Operation (7 Marks)**

**Derivation:**

Let $E_{batt}$ = initial battery energy.
Let $P_{harv}(t)$ = harvested power at time $t$.
Let $P_{cons}(t)$ = consumed power at time $t$.

Energy in the battery at time $T$:

$$E_{batt}(T) = E_{batt}(0) + \int_{0}^{T} P_{harv}(t)\, dt - \int_{0}^{T} P_{cons}(t)\, dt$$

**Energy-Neutral Operation (ENO) Condition:**

The node's battery level must **never drop below zero**, and ideally should remain above a safe threshold $E_{safe}$:

$$\boxed{E_{batt}(0) + \int_{0}^{T} \big[P_{harv}(t) - P_{cons}(t)\big]\, dt \geq E_{safe}, \quad \forall T}$$

**Simplified steady-state ENO:**

$$\boxed{P_{cons,avg} \leq P_{harv,avg}}$$

**Worked Example:**

If $P_{harv,avg} = 50$ µW and $E_{batt} = 1$ J:

- If $P_{cons,avg} = 30$ µW → surplus = 20 µW → battery grows → indefinite life.
- If $P_{cons,avg} = 80$ µW → deficit = 30 µW → battery dies in $E_{batt}/30\mu W = 1/30 \times 10^{-6} \approx 33{,}333$ s ≈ 9.3 hours.

**Conclusion:** EH enables **energy-autonomous WSNs** — a key enabler for Industry 4.0 and IoT-at-the-edge.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Energy Management Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting $E_{elec}$ in receive energy:** Many students write $E_{Rx} = 0$. Wrong. Receiving costs energy.
> 2. **Using $d^2$ model for $d > d_0$:** This is the #1 error. Always check $d$ vs. $d_0 \approx 87.7$ m.
> 3. **Confusing $E_{sense}$ with $E_{Tx}$:** Sensing is cheap; transmitting is expensive. Quantify the difference.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numericals:** nJ vs. µJ vs. mJ — unit-conversion errors cost full marks.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations (uniform distribution, equal CH probability, etc.).
> 6. **Missing the cross-layer nature:** Energy management is **not** a single-layer solution. Mention at least 2 layers.
> 7. **In ENO problems, ignoring the battery buffer** — claiming "infinite life" without stating $E_{batt} > 0$ is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Energy is the #1 constraint** in WSNs; **radio is the #1 energy consumer** (70–80%).
- [x] **First-Order Radio Model:** $E_{Tx} = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon d^{n}$; $E_{Rx} = kE_{elec}$; with $n = 2$ (free-space) or $n = 4$ (multipath).
- [x] **Crossover distance** $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \approx 87.7$ m (using standard values).
- [x] **Optimal cluster count:** $k_{opt} = \sqrt{N/(2\pi)} \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \cdot M/d_{toBS}^{2}$.
- [x] **Network lifetime definitions:** FND, $\alpha$-FND, partition-loss.
- [x] **Duty cycling** is the single most effective energy-saving technique — typical savings 50×–100×.
- [x] **Power states:** Transmit > Receive ≈ Idle $\gg$ Sleep.
- [x] **Energy harvesting sources:** Solar, vibration, thermal, RF, wind.
- [x] **Energy-Neutral Operation (ENO):** $P_{cons,avg} \leq P_{harv,avg}$ — leads to theoretically infinite lifetime.
- [x] **Cross-layer energy management** is essential — application, network, MAC, and physical layers all contribute.
- [x] **Protocols to remember:** LEACH, PEGASIS, S-MAC, B-MAC, SPIN, Directed Diffusion.
- [x] **Key formula for KTU:** $E_{total,round} = k \cdot E_{CH} + (N-k) \cdot E_{nonCH}$ (for clustered networks).
- [x] **Battery models:** Ideal linear (default), Rakhmatov–Vrudhula (accurate), Peukert (lithium).
- [x] **Energy efficiency metric:** $\eta = \text{bits delivered}/\text{Joule consumed}$ — higher is better.
- [x] **Real-world deployable lifetime target:** 1–10 years without battery replacement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy for this Topic:** Memorize the **First-Order Radio Model equations** and the **$k_{opt}$ derivation** verbatim. Practice at least **3 numerical problems** combining $E_{Tx}/E_{Rx}$ with cluster calculations. Always end answers with a **real-world deployment example** to score full marks on application-level questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: WSN Applications in Environmental Monitoring, Healthcare, and Industrial Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT863 - ADHOC AND WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** WSN Applications in Environmental Monitoring, Healthcare, and Industrial Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) Applications — The Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A *Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)* is a self-configuring infrastructure of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions (such as temperature, sound, vibration, pressure, motion, or pollutants) and pass their data through the network to a central sink or Base Station (BS), often deployed for domain-specific applications in **Environmental Monitoring**, **Healthcare**, and **Industrial Automation**.

In the context of KTU Module 3, the focus shifts from *how* a WSN works (Modules 1 & 2) to *where* and *why* WSNs are deployed in the real world. The three application pillars — Environmental, Healthcare, and Industrial — form the canonical case-study triplet used in KTU question papers.

#### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Nervous System" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine a human body. The brain (Base Station) cannot directly feel heat, pain, or pressure. Instead, billions of tiny nerve endings (sensor nodes) are spread across the skin, muscles, and organs. Each nerve ending detects a stimulus, converts it to an electrical pulse, and relays it through a chain of neurons (multi-hop routing) to the brain. The brain then decides an action.
>
> A WSN works exactly the same way:
> - **Sensor nodes** = nerve endings (detect temperature, gas, heartbeat, vibration)
> - **Multi-hop links** = neurons (relay data wirelessly)
> - **Base Station (Sink)** = brain (aggregates, analyses, and acts)

This analogy helps a student immediately grasp *why* WSNs are useful: they extend human perception into environments that are **inaccessible, hostile, large-scale, or humanly impossible** to monitor continuously.

#### 1.3 Why WSNs Are Ideal for These Three Domains

| Domain | Core Need | Why WSN Wins |
|---|---|---|
| **Environmental Monitoring** | Wide-area, long-duration, unattended data collection | Low-power, self-organizing, low-cost |
| **Healthcare** | Continuous, non-intrusive, patient-friendly monitoring | Miniature, wireless, body-wearable |
| **Industrial Automation** | Real-time, fault-tolerant, machine-to-machine telemetry | Reliable, dense, scalable, low-latency |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:**
> Whenever a question asks *"Discuss the application of WSN in ___"*, immediately structure your answer in 4 parts: (1) **Why WSN is needed** (2) **Architecture & deployment** (3) **Sensors & data types** (4) **Real-world case study / project**. This 4-part structure is the KTU high-yield answer template.

### 1.4 The Three Application Pillars — A Quick Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 Outcomes:**
> After studying this topic, students must be able to **CO3**: *Analyze the application-specific design requirements, architectures, and challenges of WSNs deployed in environmental, healthcare, and industrial domains.*

```
ENVIRONMENTAL MONITORING
├── Forest fire & volcano surveillance
├── Flood & water-quality detection
├── Precision agriculture & habitat monitoring
└── Air-pollution & climate sensing

HEALTHCARE
├── Body Area Networks (BAN) / WBAN
├── Patient & elderly monitoring
├── Smart implants & drug delivery
└── Hospital asset / patient tracking

INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
├── Structural health monitoring (SHM)
├── Machine condition & predictive maintenance
├── Smart factory / Industry 4.0
└── Pipeline, refinery & data-center monitoring
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Application Domain 1 — Environmental Monitoring

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> Environmental Monitoring WSNs are large-scale, often outdoor, self-sustaining networks that measure ecological parameters (temperature, humidity, soil moisture, gas concentration, light, wind speed, seismic activity) over months to years without human intervention.

#### 2.1.1 Typical Architecture

A hierarchical 3-tier architecture is most commonly adopted:

- **Tier 1 — Sensor Field:** Hundreds to thousands of motes (e.g., MicaZ, TelosB) scattered across the area. They sample physical phenomena periodically (duty-cycling).
- **Tier 2 — Cluster Heads / Gateways:** Aggregate data from motes using clustering protocols (LEACH, PEGASIS). Perform local data fusion to reduce energy drain.
- **Tier 3 — Base Station & Backend:** Wired or long-haul wireless (GSM, satellite, Wi-Fi) link to an Internet server where data is visualized (e.g., Google Earth overlays).

#### 2.1.2 Canonical Case Studies (KTU High-Yield)

| Project / System | Domain | Sensors Used | Key Takeaway |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Great Duck Island (GDI)** | Seabird habitat | Temperature, humidity, pressure, IR | One of the earliest real WSN deployments (UC Berkeley, 2002). Demonstrated multi-month autonomous operation. |
| **ZebraNet** | Wildlife tracking (zebras) | GPS, accelerometer, collar-mounted | Mobile WSN; energy-aware store-and-forward protocol. |
| **Volcano Monitoring (Volcano, Ecuador)** | Volcanic activity | Seismic (geophone), acoustic, temperature | High data-rate, time-critical; used tiered architecture. |
| **FireBug / Forest Fire Detection** | Forest fire | Temperature, humidity, CO, smoke, IR | Event-driven reporting; spatial correlation algorithms. |
| **SoilNet / IRRI Precision Agri** | Agriculture | Soil moisture, pH, leaf wetness, light | 2-tier architecture; uses underground + above-ground motes. |

#### 2.1.3 Design Challenges

> [!WARNING]
> **Why environmental WSNs are hard:**
> 1. **Energy scarcity** — battery non-replaceable (e.g., in forest floor)
> 2. **Harsh propagation** — foliage attenuation, weather fading
> 3. **Large scale** — thousands of nodes
> 4. **Heterogeneous sensing** — different modalities per node
> 5. **Data correlation** — high spatial/temporal redundancy → in-network aggregation is critical

#### 2.1.4 Key Formulas (Energy & Coverage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Model (First-Order Radio, Heinzelman 2002):**
> Energy to transmit $k$ bits over distance $d$:
> $$E_{TX}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$$
> Energy to receive $k$ bits:
> $$E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$$
> where $n$ is the path-loss exponent (typically **2 to 4**).

### 2.2 Application Domain 2 — Healthcare WSNs

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> Healthcare WSNs (also called Wireless Body Area Networks / WBAN) consist of in-body, on-body, and around-body sensor nodes that continuously monitor physiological signals (ECG, EMG, EEG, SpO2, body temperature, glucose, blood pressure) and relay them to a personal server (smartphone) or hospital gateway.

#### 2.2.1 Three-Tier WBAN Architecture

1. **Tier 1 — Intra-BAN:** Wearable / implantable sensors communicate with a personal device (smartphone/PD).
2. **Tier 2 — Inter-BAN:** Personal device relays data via Wi-Fi/3G/4G to medical servers.
3. **Tier 3 — Beyond-BAN:** Hospital information system, doctor's terminal, alert dispatcher.

#### 2.2.2 Canonical Case Studies

| Project | Sensors | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **CodeBlue (Harvard)** | ECG, SpO2, motion | Emergency response; pulse oximeter and ECG motes for mass casualty |
| **ALARM-NET** | ECG, environment | Assisted-living + ambient sensors for elderly |
| **Mercury (UCLA)** | ECG, EMG, EEG | Multi-parameter physiological monitoring |
| **LiveNet (MIT)** | EMG, GSR | Long-term Parkinson's & epilepsy studies |
| **Smart Pill / PillCam** | Camera, pH | GI tract imaging & drug delivery monitoring |

#### 2.2.3 Design Challenges

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Healthcare-specific constraints:**
> 1. **Ultra-low power** — battery must last months (implantable) or days (wearable)
> 2. **Miniaturization** — node must weigh < **5 g** to be comfortable
> 3. **Biocompatibility** — no toxic materials touching skin/tissue
> 4. **Reliability & QoS** — life-critical data; packet loss unacceptable
> 5. **Security & privacy** — HIPAA-style compliance
> 6. **Mobility** — patient movement causes topology dynamics

#### 2.2.4 IEEE 802.15.6 WBAN Standard

The IEEE 802.15.6 standard governs WBAN MAC/PHY, with three physical layers:
- **Narrowband (NB)** — 402–2480 MHz, low power
- **Ultra-Wideband (UWB)** — high data rate, precise location
- **Human Body Communications (HBC)** — uses body's electrical conductivity

### 2.3 Application Domain 3 — Industrial Automation WSNs

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> Industrial WSNs (IWSNs) are dense, real-time, often harsh-environment networks that monitor and control industrial processes — machines, structures, pipelines, and factories — by collecting vibration, temperature, acoustic, pressure, and current data to enable **predictive maintenance**, **process optimization**, and **autonomous control**.

#### 2.3.1 Three Sub-Domains of Industrial WSN

1. **Process Automation** — continuous chemical/plants (e.g., oil refinery)
2. **Factory Automation** — discrete manufacturing (e.g., assembly line)
3. **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM)** — bridges, buildings, dams

#### 2.3.2 Canonical Case Studies

| Project / System | Application | Sensors / Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Intel Berkeley Lab (factory)** | SHM on Golden Gate Bridge | Vibration (geophone) sensors, Mica2 motes |
| **Balloons (Replicated)** | Industrial monitoring, oil tanks | Gas, temperature, level |
| **HVAC monitoring (Honeywell)** | Data centers | Temperature, humidity, airflow |
| **Snam Rete Gas (Italy)** | Pipeline monitoring | Pressure, flow, vibration, leak detection |
| **SmartFactory (Germany)** | Industry 4.0 reference | Full IoT + WSN integration with OPC-UA |

#### 2.3.3 Design Challenges

> [!WARNING]
> **Industrial WSN constraints:**
> 1. **Determinism / Real-time** — millisecond deadlines
> 2. **EMI / RF noise** — motors, drives, arc-furnaces interfere
> 3. **Metallic multi-path** — factories have heavy reflection
> 4. **Harsh temperature/vibration** — often > **85 °C** ambient
> 5. **Safety certification** — IEC 61508 SIL levels
> 6. **Wired-vs-Wireless** — IWSN must match wired reliability

#### 2.3.4 Industrial Standards

- **WirelessHART** (IEC 62591) — dominant industrial wireless
- **ISA 100.11a** — flexible alternative to HART
- **ZigBee PRO / 6LoWPAN** — for non-critical monitoring
- **5G URLLC** — Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency for next-gen factories

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Application-Specific)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this table — it summarizes every numerical/equation concept required for KTU 2024 Scheme questions on this topic.**

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **First-order radio energy** | $E_{TX} = E_{elec}k + \epsilon_{amp}k d^{n}$ | $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m² | Energy budgeting in motes |
| **Network lifetime** | $T_{life} = E_{initial} / E_{round}$ | Days to years | Compare protocols (LEACH vs SEP) |
| **Duty cycle** | $DC = t_{active} / (t_{active} + t_{sleep})$ | **0.1% – 5%** | Power saving in environmental WSN |
| **Sampling frequency (ECG)** | $f_{s,ECG}$ | **100–500 Hz** | Patient monitoring |
| **Sampling frequency (seismic)** | $f_{s,seismic}$ | **100–200 Hz** | Volcano/earthquake monitoring |
| **Sampling frequency (temperature)** | $f_{s,temp}$ | **0.01–1 Hz** | Slow environmental sensing |
| **Path-loss exponent** | $n$ (free space = 2) | Forest: 3–4, Factory: 1.5–2.5, Body: 3–4 | Link budget calculation |
| **Link budget (Friis)** | $P_{r} = P_{t} G_{t} G_{r} (\lambda / 4\pi d)^{2}$ | dBm | Range estimation |
| **Latency requirement (IWSN)** | Closed-loop control | **< 10 ms** | Process control |
| **Latency requirement (WBAN)** | Alert routing | **< 100 ms** | Patient emergency |
| **WBAN data rate** | ECG ≈ 72 kbps; EEG ≈ 86 kbps | kbps – Mbps | IEEE 802.15.6 capacity |
| **Node density (agri)** | 1 mote / ha to 1 mote / 10 m² | varies | Coverage planning |
| **Coverage area** | $A = \pi R^{2} \cdot N$ (per node) | m² | Placement strategy |
| **MTTF (Industrial)** | $MTTF = 1/\lambda$ | hours | Reliability for SHM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on units and symbols used in this sheet:**
> - $E_{TX}$: transmit energy (Joules)
> - $E_{elec}$: per-bit electronics energy
> - $\epsilon_{amp}$: amplifier energy per bit per m²
> - $d$: distance (m), $n$: path-loss exponent
> - $DC$: duty cycle (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
> - $G_{t}, G_{r}$: antenna gains (linear, not dB)
> - $\lambda$: wavelength = $c/f$ (m)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Estimating Network Lifetime in an Environmental WSN

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
> A WSN has $N$ sensor nodes deployed uniformly in a square field of side $L$ meters. Each node has initial energy $E_0$ = **2 J**. The first-order radio model is used. Compute the approximate network lifetime when all nodes report once per round to a distant base station, assuming direct (single-hop) transmission.

**Given:**
- $N$ = 100 nodes
- Field side $L$ = 100 m
- Initial energy per node $E_0$ = 2 J
- Packet size $k$ = 4000 bits
- $E_{elec}$ = 50 nJ/bit = $50 \times 10^{-9}$ J/bit
- $\epsilon_{amp}$ = 100 pJ/bit/m² = $100 \times 10^{-12}$ J/bit/m²
- Path-loss exponent $n$ = 2 (free space)
- Base station located at $(L/2, L/2)$ — center of field

**Step 1 — Find the average distance from a node to the BS.**

For uniformly distributed nodes in a square of side $L$ with BS at center, the expected Euclidean distance is:

$$E[d] = \int_{0}^{L/2} \int_{0}^{L/2} \frac{1}{L^{2}} \cdot 4 \cdot d_{xy} \cdot dx \, dy$$

To avoid a long integration, a standard engineering approximation is:

$$\bar{d} \approx 0.382 \cdot L \quad \text{(for square field, BS at center)}$$

Substituting $L = 100$ m:

$$\bar{d} \approx 0.382 \times 100 = 38.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Energy spent by one node per round (transmit + receive).**

Using the first-order radio model:

$$E_{TX} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot \bar{d}^{n}$$

Plug in the values:

$$E_{TX} = (50 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 4000 + (100 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 4000 \cdot (38.2)^{2}$$

Compute each term separately:

$$\text{Term 1} = 50 \times 10^{-9} \times 4000 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.0002 \text{ J}$$

$$\text{Term 2} = 100 \times 10^{-12} \times 4000 \times 1459.24 = 5.8369 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \approx 0.000584 \text{ J}$$

Therefore:

$$E_{TX} \approx 0.0002 + 0.000584 = 7.84 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Energy spent receiving (negligible in this scenario, since all send to BS).**

$$E_{RX} = 0 \quad \text{(for direct single-hop uplink only)}$$

**Step 4 — Total energy per node per round.**

$$E_{round} = E_{TX} + E_{RX} = 7.84 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}$$

**Step 5 — Number of rounds before first node dies.**

Each node starts with $E_0 = 2$ J, so:

$$R_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{E_0}{E_{round}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2}{7.84 \times 10^{-4}} \right\rfloor$$

$$R_{max} = \left\lfloor 2551.02 \right\rfloor = 2551 \text{ rounds}$$

**Step 6 — Convert rounds to time (if one round = 1 hour, for example).**

$$T_{life} = R_{max} \times t_{round} = 2551 \times 1 \text{ hr} = 2551 \text{ hours} \approx 106 \text{ days}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU exam:**
> Even with such low-power radios, single-hop networks consume energy rapidly because distant nodes spend most of their energy on transmit amplification. This is why **multi-hop routing** and **clustering (LEACH)** are essential in environmental WSNs to extend lifetime by 5–10×.

### 3.2 Derivation: WBAN Path-Loss for On-Body Channel

**Step 1 — Channel model for on-body (CM3) propagation.**

IEEE 802.15.6 specifies a path-loss model for body-surface-to-body-surface links:

$$PL(d) = PL_{0} + 10 n \log_{10}(d/d_{0}) + X_{\sigma}$$

**Step 2 — Typical values for the 2.45 GHz band.**

For on-body (chest-to-wrist) CM3:

$$PL_{0} = 35.2 \text{ dB}, \quad n = 6.6, \quad d_{0} = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$$

$$\sigma = 5.0 \text{ dB (log-normal shadowing)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute path loss at $d$ = 1 m.**

$$PL(1) = 35.2 + 10 \times 6.6 \times \log_{10}(1 / 0.1)$$

$$PL(1) = 35.2 + 66 \times \log_{10}(10) = 35.2 + 66 \times 1$$

$$PL(1) = 35.2 + 66 = 101.2 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

A path loss of **101.2 dB** at just 1 m is enormous — it is **~30 dB worse** than free-space propagation at 1 m. This is why WBAN radios use very low power (typically **0 dBm or less**) and ultra-short ranges (< **3 m**).

### 3.3 Derivation: Industrial WSN Latency Bound for Closed-Loop Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical framework:**
> A control loop with sampling period $T_s$ must complete: sensing → MAC contention → transmit → sink process → actuator command → transmit back, all within $T_s$ for stability.

**Step 1 — Total latency components.**

$$L_{total} = t_{sense} + t_{contend} + t_{TX} + t_{proc} + t_{TX\_back} + t_{actuate}$$

**Step 2 — Typical industrial values.**

$$t_{sense} = 5 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{contend} = 10 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{TX} = 2 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{proc} = 3 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{actuate} = 5 \text{ ms}$$

$$L_{total} = 5 + 10 + 2 + 3 + 2 + 5 = 27 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compare to control deadline.**

For a 100 Hz control loop, $T_s$ = 10 ms. Here $L_{total}$ = 27 ms **exceeds** the deadline. This is why industrial WSNs need **TDMA-based deterministic MAC** (e.g., WirelessHART with 10 ms slots) and **5G URLLC** with sub-millisecond guarantees.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a WSN for Each Application

| Parameter | Environmental | Healthcare (WBAN) | Industrial |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Node count** | 100 – 10 000 | 1 – 10 per person | 50 – 1000 per site |
| **Network scale** | Very large (km²) | Personal (m²) | Building-scale (100 m – 1 km) |
| **Power source** | Battery + solar | Coin cell / harvested | Wired or line-powered battery |
| **Data rate** | Low (kbps) | Medium (kbps – Mbps) | Medium-high (kbps – Mbps) |
| **Latency tolerance** | Seconds–minutes | < 100 ms for alerts | < 10 ms for control |
| **Mobility** | Mostly static | High (patient moves) | Static to slow |
| **Reliability** | 90–95% | > 99% (life-critical) | > 99.99% (safety) |
| **Standard** | ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, LoRa | IEEE 802.15.6 | WirelessHART, ISA 100 |
| **Key challenge** | Energy efficiency | Biocompatibility, security | Determinism, EMI |
| **Lifetime goal** | Years | Months (wearable) | Years |
| **Cost per node** | < $50 | < $100 (medical grade) | $200 – $2000 |

### 3.5 Comparative Case Study Matrix

> [!TIP]
> **This is the single most exam-relevant table in Section 3 — practice recalling it cold.**

| Case Study | Domain | Sensor Type | Network Type | Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Great Duck Island** | Environment (habitat) | Temp, humidity, IR | Static multi-hop | First real long-term deployment |
| **ZebraNet** | Environment (wildlife) | GPS, accel. | Mobile P2P | Store-and-forward, peer-to-peer |
| **Volcano Monitoring** | Environment (geology) | Seismic, acoustic | Tiered, hybrid | Time-critical data with reliability |
| **CodeBlue** | Healthcare | ECG, SpO2 | Ad-hoc WBAN | Emergency triage with priority routing |
| **ALARM-NET** | Healthcare | Multi-vital + ambient | WBAN + WSN fusion | Context-aware elderly care |
| **Mercury** | Healthcare | ECG, EMG, EEG | Hierarchical WBAN | High-fidelity multi-parameter |
| **Golden Gate Bridge SHM** | Industrial (SHM) | Vibration | Synchronized, time-slotted | Sparse high-rate sampling |
| **Snam Gas Pipeline** | Industrial (process) | Pressure, flow, acoustic | Linear, multi-hop | Leak detection via acoustic correlation |
| **SmartFactory KL** | Industrial (Industry 4.0) | Mixed | 5G + TSN + WSN | Full cyber-physical integration |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level WSN Application Architecture (All Three Domains)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    envA["Field Sensor Node A"] --> envClusterH["Cluster Head CH1"]
    envB["Field Sensor Node B"] --> envClusterH
    envC["Field Sensor Node C"] --> envClusterH2["Cluster Head CH2"]
    envD["Field Sensor Node D"] --> envClusterH2
    envClusterH --> envGateway["Environmental Gateway"]
    envClusterH2 --> envGateway
    envGateway --> envServer["Cloud Analytics Server"]

    hcA["Wearable ECG Patch"] --> hcPD["Personal Device Smartphone"]
    hcB["Wearable SpO2 Band"] --> hcPD
    hcC["Implant Glucose Sensor"] --> hcPD
    hcPD --> hcCloud["Hospital Cloud EHR"]

    indA["Vibration Sensor on Motor"] --> indPLC["PLC / Industrial Gateway"]
    indB["Temperature Probe on Bearing"] --> indPLC
    indC["Pressure Sensor on Pipe"] --> indPLC
    indPLC --> indSCADA["SCADA / DCS Control Room"]

    envServer --> dash["Unified Operator Dashboard"]
    hcCloud --> dash
    indSCADA --> dash

    dash --> actAlert["Alerts and Actuation Logic"]
    actAlert --> actA["Forest Fire Alarm"]
    actAlert --> actB["Patient Emergency Code"]
    actAlert --> actC["Machine Shutdown Command"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each domain contributes data to a *Unified Operator Dashboard* (in practice, three separate dashboards exist, but the architectural pattern is identical: **Edge Sensors → Gateway/Cluster → Cloud/Server → Actuation**).

### 4.2 Detailed WBAN Architecture (3-Tier)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1["Tier 1 Intra BAN Body Area"]
        s1["ECG Electrode Chest"]
        s2["SpO2 Wristband"]
        s3["Glucose Implant"]
        s4["Temperature Patch"]
        s5["Motion Accelerometer"]
    end

    subgraph T2["Tier 2 Personal Device"]
        pd["Smartphone or Wearable Hub Personal Server"]
    end

    subgraph T3["Tier 3 Medical Network"]
        ap["Hospital WiFi or 4G AP"]
        cloud["Electronic Health Record Server"]
        doc["Doctor Tablet"]
        amb["Ambulance Dispatch"]
    end

    s1 --> pd
    s2 --> pd
    s3 --> pd
    s4 --> pd
    s5 --> pd
    pd --> ap
    ap --> cloud
    cloud --> doc
    cloud --> amb
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway:** Tier 1 nodes are ultra-low-power and talk to the Tier-2 personal device over short-range radio (Bluetooth LE or IEEE 802.15.6). The personal device handles heavier processing, encryption, and wide-area uplink.

### 4.3 Industrial WSN with Time-Slotted Channel Hopping (WirelessHART Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    m1["Motor 1 Sensor"] -- slot1 chA --> gw["Gateway Manager"]
    m2["Motor 2 Sensor"] -- slot2 chB --> gw
    m3["Motor 3 Sensor"] -- slot3 chC --> gw
    m4["Motor 4 Sensor"] -- slot1 chB --> gw
    m5["Motor 5 Sensor"] -- slot2 chA --> gw
    m6["Motor 6 Sensor"] -- slot3 chA --> gw
    gw -- backhaul --> ctrl["DCS Control Room"]
    ctrl -- actuator cmd --> m1
    ctrl -- actuator cmd --> m3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each motor's sensor is assigned a *time slot* and *channel* (frequency). The gateway schedules and rotates channels to defeat interference and jamming — this is the core idea of **Time-Slotted Channel Hopping (TSCH)** used in WirelessHART and IEEE 802.15.4e.

### 4.4 Environmental WSN — Habitat Monitoring (Great Duck Island Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    burrowA["Burrow Sensor Cluster 1 Temp Humidity"] --> h1["Habitat Gateway Hut 1"]
    burrowB["Burrow Sensor Cluster 2"] --> h1
    burrowC["Burrow Sensor Cluster 3"] --> h2["Habitat Gateway Hut 2"]
    burrowD["Burrow Sensor Cluster 4"] --> h2
    h1 --> base1["Base Station on Island"]
    h2 --> base1
    base1 -- satellite or cellular --> lab["Research Lab Server"]
    lab -- web --> sci["Scientists Worldwide"]
```

### 4.5 Healthcare Alert Routing Flow (CodeBlue Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Patient Vital Sign Anomaly"] --> det["Sensor Detects Threshold Breach"]
    det --> tri["Triage by Severity Score"]
    tri --> minor["Minor Anomaly Stored Locally"]
    tri --> major["Major Anomaly Forwarded Immediately"]
    major --> pub["Publish to Subscribe Broker"]
    pub --> amb["Ambulance Crew Notified"]
    pub --> er["ER Dashboard Updated"]
    pub --> doc["On-call Doctor Paged"]
    minor --> batch["Batch Upload Every 5 Minutes"]
    batch --> ehr["EHR Updated"]
```

### 4.6 Industrial Predictive Maintenance Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vib["Vibration Sensor"] --> edge["Edge Gateway with FFT"]
    edge --> feature["Feature Extraction RMS, kurtosis, peak"]
    feature --> ml["ML Anomaly Classifier"]
    ml -->|"Normal"| store["Log to Database"]
    ml -->|"Fault Imminent"| alarm["Raise Alert"]
    alarm --> tech["Maintenance Crew Dispatched"]
    tech --> fix["Replace Bearing before Failure"]
    fix --> vib
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
*List any three major applications of WSN in environmental monitoring and briefly explain the role of the sensor nodes in each.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**
> 1. **Forest fire detection** — Temperature, humidity, and smoke sensor nodes are deployed in a grid; they detect abnormal heat/smoke signatures and trigger an early-warning alert via multi-hop routing to a base station. **(1 mark)**
> 2. **Precision agriculture** — Soil-moisture, pH, and leaf-wetness nodes help farmers automate irrigation and fertilizer application, conserving water and improving yield. **(1 mark)**
> 3. **Volcano / seismic monitoring** — Geophone and acoustic nodes sample seismic vibrations at high rate and forward via time-synchronized multi-tier network to a research station. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Differentiate between a Wireless Body Area Network (WBAN) and a general-purpose WSN in terms of scale, data rate, and power budget.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks, table format preferred):**

| Parameter | WBAN | General WSN |
|---|---|---|
| **Scale** | Personal (1–10 nodes per person) | Wide-area (10s to 1000s of nodes) |
| **Data rate** | 10 kbps to 10 Mbps (ECG, EEG, video) | Low (0.1 – 10 kbps typical) |
| **Power budget** | Ultra-low (< 1 mW average) with energy harvesting | Battery + sometimes solar (tens of mW) |

(1 mark per row of correct comparison.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Format:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice is given — students must answer either **OR** set.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture and design challenges of deploying WSNs for **forest fire detection**. Discuss the role of in-network data aggregation in extending network lifetime.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A WSN with 50 nodes is deployed in a 100 m × 100 m forest region. Each node has initial energy 1 J and sends a 1000-bit packet per round. Using the first-order radio model with $E_{elec}$ = 50 nJ/bit and $\epsilon_{amp}$ = 100 pJ/bit/m², with path-loss exponent n = 2, estimate the number of reporting rounds before the **first node dies**, assuming each node communicates **directly** with a base station located 70 m away.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

##### Part (a): Forest Fire Detection Architecture & Data Aggregation

**1. Why WSN for forest fire (2 marks):**
Traditional satellite/observation-tower methods have **low resolution**, **long revisit times**, and **high cost**. A dense WSN gives:
- Real-time, in-situ measurement
- Spatial granularity at meter-level
- Autonomous operation for months
- Early warning even when fire starts underground (smoldering)

**2. Architecture (3 marks):**
- **Sensor layer:** MicaZ / TelosB motes equipped with **temperature (LM35)**, **humidity (SHT11)**, **CO (MQ-7)**, **smoke (MQ-2)**, and **IR flame sensors**. Density: 1 mote per 25 m² to 1 per 100 m².
- **Cluster layer:** Cluster heads (CH) run **LEACH-like rotation** to balance energy. They perform **temporal and spatial correlation** — e.g., if 3 of 5 neighbors in a region all read > 60 °C, trigger an alarm.
- **Sink/BS layer:** Connected to GSM/Satellite uplink. Triggers SMS to forest officials.
- **Duty cycling:** Nodes sleep > 99% of the time, wake every 10–30 s to sample.

**3. In-network aggregation (2 marks):**
Each raw sample is 16–24 bits. Without aggregation, 100 nodes sending 100 readings/min = **10 000 packets/min**, draining batteries in days. With aggregation:
- **Temporal aggregation:** CH averages 10 successive readings.
- **Spatial aggregation:** CH queries neighbors; reports only **max/min/mean** of a region.
- **Compression:** Use **wavelet transform** or **compressed sensing** to send a sketch, not raw data.

This reduces traffic by **80–95%**, extending lifetime from weeks to **years**.

##### Part (b): Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Given data (1 mark):**
$N$ = 50, $E_0$ = 1 J, $k$ = 1000 bits, $d$ = 70 m, $E_{elec}$ = 50 nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{amp}$ = 100 pJ/bit/m², $n$ = 2.

**Step 2 — Energy per transmission (3 marks):**

$$E_{TX} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$$

$$E_{TX} = (50 \times 10^{-9})(1000) + (100 \times 10^{-12})(1000)(70)^{2}$$

$$E_{TX} = 5 \times 10^{-5} + 10^{-7} \times 4900$$

$$E_{TX} = 5 \times 10^{-5} + 4.9 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$E_{TX} = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} + 49.0 \times 10^{-5} = 54.0 \times 10^{-5} = 5.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Energy per reception (1 mark):**

$$E_{RX} = E_{elec} \cdot k = 50 \times 10^{-9} \times 1000 = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J}$$

**Step 4 — Energy per node per round (1 mark):**

$$E_{round} = E_{TX} + E_{RX} = 5.4 \times 10^{-4} + 5.0 \times 10^{-5} = 5.9 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}$$

**Step 5 — Number of rounds before first node dies (1 mark):**

$$R_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{E_0}{E_{round}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1.0}{5.9 \times 10^{-4}} \right\rfloor$$

$$R_{max} = \left\lfloor 1694.91 \right\rfloor = 1694 \text{ rounds}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final answer:** Each node can transmit approximately **1694 rounds** before exhausting its battery. If 1 round = 10 minutes, lifetime ≈ **11.8 days** — confirming that **multi-hop clustering is essential** for long-term deployment.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the architecture, sensors, and key challenges of **Wireless Body Area Networks (WBAN)** for patient monitoring. Compare IEEE 802.15.6 with Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) for WBAN use.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **predictive maintenance** architecture of WSN in an industrial plant. How does edge analytics (FFT, ML) improve over a pure SCADA approach?

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

##### Part (a): WBAN Architecture and Standards Comparison

**1. WBAN architecture (3 marks):**
A WBAN has three tiers (already shown in Section 4.2):
- **Tier 1 — Intra-BAN sensors** (on-body, in-body): ECG, EMG, EEG, SpO2, glucose, motion, temperature.
- **Tier 2 — Personal Device (PD):** A smartphone or hub that acts as a sink. Performs local storage, encryption, and decision.
- **Tier 3 — Beyond-BAN:** Wi-Fi / 4G / 5G uplink to hospital servers, doctor's device, ambulance dispatch.

**2. Key sensors used (2 marks):**
- **ECG electrodes** (chest): 100–500 Hz, 12-bit
- **SpO2 pulse-oximeter** (wrist/finger): 100 Hz
- **Glucose sensor** (subcutaneous): every 1–5 min
- **Temperature** (skin/patch): 0.1 Hz
- **Motion / IMU** (wrist/ankle): 50–100 Hz
- **Galvanic skin response**: 10 Hz

**3. Design challenges (1 mark):**
- Energy: 1–2 mW budget, harvesting from body heat/motion
- Biocompatibility, security (HIPAA), mobility, RF absorption (SAR limits)

**4. IEEE 802.15.6 vs BLE (1 mark):**

| Feature | IEEE 802.15.6 | BLE 5.x |
|---|---|---|
| **Range** | 3–5 m | 10–100 m |
| **Data rate** | Up to 10 Mbps (UWB) | 2 Mbps |
| **Power** | Ultra-low (optimized for body) | Low (general IoT) |
| **Latency** | Deterministic, slotted | Best-effort |
| **Security** | Strong (medical-grade) | AES-128, moderate |
| **Node count** | Up to 256 | Many, but optimized for few |
| **Use fit** | Medical-grade WBAN | Fitness / consumer |

##### Part (b): Predictive Maintenance with WSN

**1. Architecture (3 marks):**
(Refer to Section 4.6 diagram.)
- **Vibration sensors** (MEMS accelerometers, 1–10 kHz) on motor housings, bearings, gearboxes.
- **Temperature sensors** on casings and oil reservoirs.
- **Acoustic emission sensors** for early crack detection.
- All feed an **edge gateway** that runs **FFT**, **kurtosis**, **crest factor** extraction, and an **ML classifier** (e.g., Random Forest, 1D-CNN).
- Gateway uplinks only **features / anomaly flags** to SCADA / DCS.

**2. Edge analytics pipeline (3 marks):**
- **Step 1 — Sampling:** 10 kHz vibration, 100 Hz temperature.
- **Step 2 — Windowing:** 1-second windows, 50% overlap.
- **Step 3 — FFT / Wavelet:** Extract frequency-domain features.
- **Step 4 — ML Inference:** Classifier outputs **health score** (0–100).
- **Step 5 — Decision:** If score < threshold, raise **predictive alert** N days before failure.

**3. Advantages over pure SCADA (1 mark):**
- SCADA only monitors **slow variables** (current, temperature) and detects **failure after it occurs**.
- WSN + edge analytics detects **incipient faults** (bearing wear, imbalance, looseness) **weeks earlier**, reducing downtime by **30–70%** and maintenance cost by **10–25%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> In Part B, students often:
> 1. **Confuse WBAN with general WSN** — WBAN operates at personal scale with very tight energy/biocompat constraints; do not use MicaZ-class motes in a WBAN answer.
> 2. **Skip numerical units** in the radio model — always write $E_{elec}$ in J/bit and $\epsilon_{amp}$ in J/bit/m² explicitly.
> 3. **Forget in-network aggregation** when discussing lifetime — clustering/LEACH is the *expected* keyword.
> 4. **Omit real case study** — always anchor your answer to **at least one named project** (GDI, CodeBlue, Golden Gate Bridge, Snam, etc.). This is the single most common reason for losing 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save This for the Night Before Exam):**

- [x] **Three application pillars:** Environmental, Healthcare, Industrial — all are 14-mark favorites.
- [x] **Always use the 4-part answer template:** Need → Architecture → Sensors/Case Study → Challenges.
- [x] **First-order radio model** $E_{TX} = E_{elec} k + \epsilon_{amp} k d^{n}$ — must be memorized.
- [x] **Path-loss exponent** $n$: free-space = 2, forest = 3–4, factory = 1.5–2.5, on-body = 6+.
- [x] **WBAN** operates at body scale, uses IEEE 802.15.6, has ultra-low power and strict security.
- [x] **Industrial WSN** uses WirelessHART/ISA-100 with TDMA + channel hopping for determinism.
- [x] **Environmental WSN** relies on duty cycling, in-network aggregation, and clustering (LEACH).
- [x] **Latency budgets:** Industrial < 10 ms, Healthcare alert < 100 ms, Environmental seconds–minutes.
- [x] **Memorize 5 case studies:** Great Duck Island, ZebraNet, CodeBlue, Golden Gate Bridge SHM, Snam Pipeline.
- [x] **Cite standards explicitly** — IEEE 802.15.4, 802.15.6, WirelessHART, 6LoWPAN, LoRa.
- [x] **Energy harvesting** is the future for all three domains — mention in any open-ended question.
- [x] **Data rate hierarchy:** Temperature ≈ 0.01 Hz < ECG ≈ 100 Hz < Seismic ≈ 200 Hz < EMG ≈ 1 kHz < Vibration ≈ 10 kHz.
- [x] **Security triad** (CIA: Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication) is the answer to *any* privacy question in WBAN.
- [x] **Final numerical check trick:** In the radio model, $E_{elec}$ is **always in nJ/bit** and $\epsilon_{amp}$ **always in pJ/bit/m²** — convert before plugging in.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT863 - ADHOC AND WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Terminology):**
> In the context of **Ad Hoc and Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)**, a *security attack* is defined as any intentional or unintentional action that compromises the **confidentiality, integrity, or availability (CIA Triad)** of data, services, or network resources. Due to the open, distributed, infrastructure-less, and resource-constrained nature of these networks, they are inherently vulnerable to a wider range of attacks than traditional wired networks.

## 1.1 The CIA Triad — Foundation of Security

| Pillar | Meaning | WSN Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Keeping data secret from unauthorized nodes | An attacker must not read sensor data (e.g., troop movement) |
| **Integrity** | Ensuring data is not tampered with in transit | The temperature reading "35°C" must not be altered to "85°C" |
| **Availability** | Services & data must be accessible when needed | A sensor must not be jammed out of service |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Open Classroom

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Classroom Without Walls**
> Imagine a classroom where students (nodes) are sitting in an open field with no walls, no teacher (no central authority), and anyone can join or leave the discussion. Some students are honest, but a few troublemakers (attackers) can:
> - **Eavesdrop** (Passive attack) on the whispered answers of others.
> - **Yell false answers** (Active attack) to confuse the class.
> - **Repeat old messages** (Replay attack) out of context.
> - **Pretend to be the teacher** (Impersonation / Sybil attack).
> - **Block the blackboard** (Jamming / DoS attack).
>
> A wireless ad hoc network behaves exactly like this open classroom — **no central authority, shared open medium, dynamic topology, and trust issues**.

## 1.3 Broad Classification of Attacks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Grand Divisions (KTU Standard):**
>
> 1. **Passive Attacks** — The attacker only *observes* and *eavesdrops* without modifying anything. Hard to detect because the network behaves normally.
> 2. **Active Attacks** — The attacker *modifies, drops, injects, or disrupts* traffic. Easier to detect but causes direct harm.

| Feature | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Eavesdropping, traffic analysis | Tampering, disruption, impersonation |
| **Data Modification** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Very Hard | Moderate to Easy |
| **Impact on Network** | Indirect (privacy loss) | Direct (service breakdown) |
| **Examples** | Snooping, Traffic Analysis | Blackhole, Wormhole, Flooding, Sybil, Jamming |

## 1.4 Layer-Wise Attack Categorization (Critical for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Classify attacks based on the OSI/Protocol layer."* Memorize the following mapping.

| Layer | Specific Attack(s) |
|---|---|
| **Physical Layer** | Jamming, Tampering, Eavesdropping |
| **Data Link / MAC Layer** | Collision, Exhaustion, Unfairness, Traffic Analysis |
| **Network Layer** | Blackhole, Wormhole, Grayhole, Sinkhole, Sybil, Hello Flood, Selective Forwarding |
| **Transport Layer** | Flooding, Desynchronization |
| **Application Layer** | Repudiation, Data Corruption, Clone Attack |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Active Attacks — Detailed Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Active attacks are worth **2-mark short questions** and **7-mark detailed sub-questions**. Master the following eight attacks.

### 2.1.1 Blackhole Attack

- **What:** A malicious node advertises itself as having the *shortest* or *freshest* path to the destination (often by sending forged RREQ with minimum hop count).
- **Effect:** All neighbouring nodes route their data packets toward the blackhole node. Once received, the packets are **silently dropped**.
- **Why it works:** In reactive protocols like **AODV**, the first RREP received is accepted without verification.
- **Real-world utility in understanding:** Models a malicious router absorbing traffic in IP networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Variant — Grayhole Attack:** A smarter cousin. The node selectively drops only certain packets (e.g., drops every 3rd packet) to evade detection.

### 2.1.2 Wormhole Attack

- **What:** Two colluding malicious nodes create a *high-speed out-of-band tunnel* (e.g., wired link or long-range antenna) between two distant points in the network.
- **Effect:** Packets travelling through the tunnel appear to take an impossibly short route, so the wormhole nodes attract a huge amount of traffic. This can be combined with **blackhole** or **sinkhole** to destroy data.
- **Why dangerous:** Even protocols with cryptographic authentication fail because the attacker replays packets bit-perfectly.
- **Analogy:** A magical underground tunnel that connects two distant cities — the "wormhole" makes the path look shorter.

### 2.1.3 Sybil Attack

- **What:** A single malicious node assumes **multiple false identities** simultaneously.
- **Effect:** It can outvote other nodes in a voting protocol, skew distributed storage, or corrupt fault-tolerant algorithms (e.g., Byzantine fault tolerance).
- **Why it works:** No centralized certification authority in ad hoc/WSNs.
- **Analogy:** A single person voting multiple times in an election by wearing different masks.

### 2.1.4 Hello Flood Attack

- **What:** A node (often a laptop-class attacker with high transmission power) broadcasts **Hello packets** at enormous power.
- **Effect:** Every node in a vast area believes the attacker is its neighbour and routes traffic through it.
- **Why it works:** Hello packets are rarely authenticated, and the receiving node has no way to verify transmission range.

### 2.1.5 Sinkhole Attack

- **What:** A compromised node lures all traffic from a region toward itself by advertising strong routing metrics.
- **Effect:** Combined with selective forwarding or blackhole, it cripples data delivery to the **Base Station (sink)**.

### 2.1.6 Selective Forwarding Attack

- **What:** A malicious node selectively drops certain packets while forwarding others normally.
- **Effect:** Hard to detect because the node appears to be functioning.
- **Counter:** Multi-path routing.

### 2.1.7 Flooding Attack (Transport Layer)

- **What:** An attacker sends numerous connection requests to a target, exhausting its memory/CPU.
- **Effect:** **Denial of Service (DoS)** — the legitimate node cannot accept new connections.

### 2.1.8 Desynchronization Attack

- **What:** Attacker forges packets with incorrect sequence numbers, causing the endpoints to resynchronize repeatedly and never complete data exchange.

## 2.2 Passive Attacks — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Eavesdropping (Snooping)
- Attacker passively listens to the wireless medium.
- Since WSNs broadcast on RF, any node within range can overhear.

### 2.2.2 Traffic Analysis
- Even if messages are **encrypted**, the attacker can analyze *volume, timing, and routing paths* to deduce sensitive information (e.g., which node is the base station, which areas have high activity).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following mathematical metrics are standard for **attack detection probability** and **packet drop analysis** in WSN security questions.

| Symbol | Formula / Metric | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_{d}$ | $\displaystyle P_{d} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Detection Probability (True Positive Rate) | dimensionless (0–1) |
| $P_{fa}$ | $\displaystyle P_{fa} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False Alarm Probability | dimensionless (0–1) |
| $PDR$ | $\displaystyle PDR = \frac{N_{recv}}{N_{sent}}$ | Packet Delivery Ratio | dimensionless (0–1) |
| $E_{D}$ | $\displaystyle E_{D} = \frac{\sum E_{tx} + \sum E_{rx}}{N_{delivered}}$ | Energy Dissipation per packet | Joules (J) |
| $T_{D}$ | $\displaystyle T_{D} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{rx,i} - t_{tx,i})$ | Average End-to-End Delay | seconds (s) |
| $THR$ | $\displaystyle THR = \frac{N_{delivered}}{T_{total}}$ | Network Throughput | packets/sec |
| $N_{D}$ | $\displaystyle N_{D} = N_{sent} - N_{recv}$ | Number of Dropped Packets (used to detect blackhole) | packets |

> Where:
> - $TP$ = True Positives (attack correctly identified)
> - $FP$ = False Positives (normal node wrongly flagged)
> - $FN$ = False Negatives (attack missed)
> - $N_{sent}, N_{recv}$ = packets sent and received
> - $E_{tx}, E_{rx}$ = transmission & reception energy

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Attack Type | Real-world Parallel |
|---|---|
| Blackhole | Compromised router dropping traffic |
| Wormhole | BGP route hijacking in ISP networks |
| Sybil | Fake social media accounts in elections |
| Hello Flood | Rogue Wi-Fi access point with high power |
| Jamming | Military signal suppression in electronic warfare |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> While pure mathematical derivations are limited in a "security attacks" topic, KTU examiners love **analytical reasoning** and **numerical verification** of attack impact using the formulas above. Below is a worked numerical problem and a detailed attack-impact derivation.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Detection Probability in a Blackhole Attack

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Question):**
In a WSN with $N = 100$ nodes, a blackhole node is active. The IDS module reports:
- True Positives ($TP$) = 18
- False Negatives ($FN$) = 2
- False Positives ($FP$) = 5
- True Negatives ($TN$) = 75

**Step 1: Recall the Detection Probability formula**

$$
P_{d} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$
P_{d} = \frac{18}{18 + 2}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator**

$$
18 + 2 = 20
$$

**Step 4: Compute the ratio**

$$
P_{d} = \frac{18}{20} = 0.90
$$

**Step 5: Express as percentage**

$$
P_{d} = 90\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The IDS correctly identifies 90% of the actual blackhole activity.

---

**Step 6: Compute the False Alarm Probability**

$$
P_{fa} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

$$
P_{fa} = \frac{5}{5 + 75} = \frac{5}{80}
$$

$$
P_{fa} = 0.0625 = 6.25\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** Only 6.25% of normal nodes are wrongly flagged — an acceptable false-alarm rate.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Packet Delivery Ratio Drop Due to Blackhole

**Setup:**
A source node sends $N_{sent} = 1000$ packets toward the sink via a path that includes **one blackhole node**. The blackhole drops $N_{drop} = 400$ packets silently. No other losses occur.

**Step 1: Compute packets received at sink**

$$
N_{recv} = N_{sent} - N_{drop}
$$

$$
N_{recv} = 1000 - 400 = 600
$$

**Step 2: Apply the PDR formula**

$$
PDR = \frac{N_{recv}}{N_{sent}}
$$

$$
PDR = \frac{600}{1000} = 0.60
$$

**Step 3: Express the drop in PDR relative to the no-attack baseline (PDR = 1.0)**

$$
\Delta PDR = 1.0 - 0.60 = 0.40
$$

$$
\Delta PDR \% = 40\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** A single blackhole causes a **40% drop in packet delivery** — a devastating impact.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation: Why Hello Flood Defeats AODV

1. **AODV assumption:** "If I receive a Hello from Node X, then X is within my radio range and is a valid neighbour."
2. **Attacker action:** Uses a **laptop-class device with a directional antenna of $\geq 30$ dBm** to broadcast Hello.
3. **Result:** Nodes spread across a **500-metre radius** all believe the attacker is their neighbour.
4. **Cascading failure:** These nodes then set the attacker as their *next-hop* in the route table.
5. **Final outcome:** All traffic from this region is routed to a single malicious node — leading to eavesdropping or selective forwarding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:**
> *"Why can't strong encryption prevent Hello Flood?"*
> **Answer:** Hello Flood is not about *content* — it's about the **physical signal reaching the victim**. The attacker doesn't need to decrypt anything; it just abuses the *trust of proximity*.

## 3.4 Attack-Impact Severity Matrix (High-Yield for KTU)

| Attack | Confidentiality | Integrity | Availability | Detection Difficulty |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Eavesdropping** | ⚠️ High | — | — | Very Hard |
| **Blackhole** | — | — | ⚠️ High | Moderate |
| **Wormhole** | — | — | ⚠️ High | Hard |
| **Sybil** | — | ⚠️ High | ⚠️ Medium | Hard |
| **Jamming** | — | — | ⚠️ Very High | Easy (signal strength) |
| **Hello Flood** | ⚠️ Medium | — | ⚠️ High | Moderate |
| **Selective Forwarding** | — | ⚠️ High | ⚠️ Medium | Hard |
| **Sinkhole** | — | — | ⚠️ High | Moderate |
| **Flooding (Transport)** | — | — | ⚠️ Very High | Easy |
| **Desynchronization** | — | ⚠️ High | ⚠️ High | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams map the **flow of attacks** and **defense layers** in WSN — the kind of architecture-level visualization KTU expects for **7-mark answers**.

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Attack Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Security Attacks in Ad Hoc and WSN] --> B[Passive Attacks]

```
