# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Eight Pillars of Future WSN/Adhoc Networks

## 2.1 Pillar 1: Internet of Things (IoT) Integration

**Core Idea:** Every sensor node becomes a *first-class citizen* of the Internet with a unique IPv6 address (6LoWPAN / RPL protocol).

- **How it works:** Sensors run a tiny TCP/IP stack using **6LoWPAN** (RFC 4944) over IEEE 802.15.4, allowing direct IP communication.
- **Why it matters:** Eliminates the proprietary gateway bottleneck.
- **Routing Protocol:** **RPL** (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks) — forms a **DODAG** (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph) rooted at the sink.

$$
\text{Rank}_{\text{node}} = f(\text{ETX}, \text{hop count}, \text{energy residual})
$$

## 2.2 Pillar 2: 5G and Beyond (B5G/6G)

The three 5G use-cases directly map to WSN paradigms:

| 5G Pillar | KTU Term | WSN/Adhoc Application |
|-----------|----------|----------------------|
| **eMBB** (enhanced Mobile BroadBand) | High throughput | Video-surveillance WSNs |
| **URLLC** (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency) | <1 ms latency | Industrial IoT, V2X (Vehicular) |
| **mMTC** (massive Machine-Type Comms) | $10^6$ devices/km² | Smart agriculture, smart metering |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Concept:** *Network Slicing* allows a single physical 5G infrastructure to host multiple virtual WSNs (e.g., one slice for emergency WSN, another for entertainment IoT) using **SDN** and **NFV** (Network Function Virtualization).

## 2.3 Pillar 3: AI/ML in WSN

**Operational Logic (Why & How):**
- **Why:** Classical WSN routing (AODV, DSR, LEACH) cannot adapt to dynamic conditions.
- **How:** ML models run on edge devices (TinyML) to predict link quality, detect anomalies, and optimize cluster-head selection.

**Common ML models deployed in WSN:**
- **Q-Learning** (Reinforcement Learning) for routing — learns optimal next-hop by *rewarding* low-energy, low-delay paths.
- **Autoencoders** for anomaly detection — learn normal data distribution, flag deviations.
- **Federated Learning** — trains a global model without sending raw sensor data (preserves privacy).

## 2.4 Pillar 4: Edge and Fog Computing

**Why:** Sending all WSN data to a distant cloud is energy-expensive and high-latency.

**Three-tier architecture:**

$$
\text{Sensor Node} \rightarrow \text{Edge Gateway} \rightarrow \text{Fog Node} \rightarrow \text{Cloud}
$$

| Layer | Latency | Computation Power | KTU Example |
|-------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Sensor** | 0 ms | µW (TinyML) | Temperature reading |
| **Edge** | <10 ms | mW (Raspberry Pi) | Local aggregation |
| **Fog** | 10–50 ms | W (Micro-server) | Region-wise analytics |
| **Cloud** | 100+ ms | kW (Data center) | Global ML training |

## 2.5 Pillar 5: Software-Defined Networking (SDN) for WSN

**Core Concept:** Decouples the *control plane* (decisions) from the *data plane* (forwarding).

- **SDN Controller** (e.g., ONOS, Floodlight) has a global view of the WSN.
- **OpenFlow protocol** carries control messages.
- **Benefits to WSN:** Dynamic re-tasking, energy-aware load balancing, single-point policy enforcement.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** SDN in WSN is *not* the same as SDN in data centers. The **control channel itself consumes energy**, so the protocol overhead must be carefully evaluated.

## 2.6 Pillar 6: Blockchain for Security in WSN

**Why:** Classical WSN security (TinySec, SPINS) uses *symmetric keys* which are vulnerable to single-point compromise.

**How Blockchain helps:**
- Each WSN event becomes an **immutable block**.
- **Consensus** (e.g., Proof of Stake) among cluster heads validates transactions.
- **Lightweight blockchains** (IOTA Tangle, Nano) are designed for IoT-scale.

> [!WARNING]
> **Honest Trade-off:** Blockchain adds *energy + storage + latency* overhead. It is **not** suitable for ultra-low-power nodes — only for cluster heads or gateways.

## 2.7 Pillar 7: Energy Harvesting WSN

**Sources of ambient energy:**

| Source | Power Density | Use Case |
|--------|---------------|----------|
| **Solar (outdoor)** | **100 mW/cm²** | Agriculture WSN |
| **Vibration** | **10–100 µW/cm²** | Industrial machinery |
| **RF (TV/WiFi)** | **0.1 µW/cm²** | Indoor IoT |
| **Thermal (TEG)** | **60 µW/cm²** | Body-area networks |
| **Wind** | **1 mW/cm²** | Outdoor nodes |

**Design Equation (Energy Neutral Operation — ENO):**

$$
E_{\text{harvested}} \geq E_{\text{consumed}} \quad \text{(ENO condition)}
$$

## 2.8 Pillar 8: UAV-Assisted and Cognitive Radio WSNs

- **UAV-WSN:** Drones act as *mobile sinks* or *aerial relay nodes*, solving the *energy hole* problem near static sinks.
- **Cognitive Radio (CR-WSN):** Nodes dynamically sense spectrum holes and switch channels, enabling **Dynamic Spectrum Access (DSA)**.

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All key formulas for Module 4 — memorize these for ESE 2024.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Boundary |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | $E_{tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2$ | Free-space transmission energy | $d \leq d_0$ |
| 2 | $E_{tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4$ | Multi-path transmission energy | $d > d_0$ |
| 3 | $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs} / \epsilon_{mp}}$ | Crossover distance | meters |
| 4 | $E_{rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$ | Reception energy | Joules |
| 5 | $E_{ENO} = E_{harvested} - E_{consumed} \geq 0$ | Energy Neutral Operation | Joules/cycle |
| 6 | $\text{Network Lifetime} = \min_{i} \frac{E_{0,i}}{E_{consumed,i}}$ | Time until first node dies (FND) | seconds |
| 7 | $P_{\text{out}} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot P_t / N_0}$ | Outage probability (CR-WSN) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T_{5G} \leq 1 \text{ ms}$ | URLLC latency bound | milliseconds |
| 9 | $S_{\text{RPL}} = \frac{1}{\text{ETX}_{avg}}$ | RPL stability metric | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Throughput}_{5G} = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon capacity | bits/s/Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in designing *energy-efficient cluster protocols* (LEACH, SEP, DEEC), *predictive maintenance* in Industry 4.0, and *smart agriculture* deployments across Kerala (coconut, cardamom, paddy).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Number of Clusters in LEACH (K-Means variant for emerging WSN)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-yield derivation** for KTU 2024 ESE — frequently asked in Module 4 since LEACH remains the *baseline* against which all emerging protocols (like E-LEACH, ML-LEACH) are benchmarked.

**Given:**
- $N$ sensor nodes uniformly distributed in an $M \times M$ region.
- $K$ clusters, each with one cluster head (CH).
- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴.
- Packet size: $k$ bits.

**Step 1 — Energy consumed by a non-CH node to send $k$ bits to its CH at distance $d_{\text{toCH}}$:**

$$
E_{\text{non-CH}} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{\text{toCH}}^{2}
$$

(Assuming free-space model, since intra-cluster distance is small.)

**Step 2 — Energy consumed by a CH to receive $N/K - 1$ packets and send aggregated data to BS at distance $d_{\text{toBS}}$:**

$$
E_{\text{CH}} = \left(\frac{N}{K} - 1\right) \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{N}{K} \cdot k \cdot E_{DA} + E_{tx}(k, d_{\text{toBS}})
$$

where $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/signal is the data-aggregation cost.

**Step 3 — Substitute the multi-path model for long-haul CH → BS transmission (since $d_{\text{toBS}} > d_0$):**

$$
E_{tx}(k, d_{\text{toBS}}) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Total energy per round:**

$$
E_{\text{total}} = K \cdot E_{\text{CH}} + (N - K) \cdot E_{\text{non-CH}}
$$

**Step 5 — Expected value of $d_{\text{toCH}}^{2}$ (from probability theory for uniform $M \times M$ region):**

$$
E[d_{\text{toCH}}^{2}] = \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi K}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Step 5 into Step 4 and differentiate with respect to $K$ to find the optimal $K^*$:**

$$
\frac{\partial E_{\text{total}}}{\partial K} = 0
$$

Solving the derivative equation:

$$
K^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{\text{toBS}}^{2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Let $N = 100$, $M = 100$ m, $d_{\text{toBS}} = 150$ m.

$$
K^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{10 \text{ pJ}}{0.0013 \text{ pJ}}} \cdot \frac{100}{150^{2}}
$$

$$
K^{*} = \sqrt{15.92} \cdot \sqrt{7692.3} \cdot \frac{100}{22500}
$$

$$
K^{*} = 3.99 \cdot 87.7 \cdot 0.00444
$$

$$
K^{*} \approx 1.55 \rightarrow \text{round to } K = 2 \text{ or } 3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For this scenario, only **2–3 optimal clusters** are needed. The emerging trend is to use **ML-based dynamic K** selection (e.g., K-Means++ or reinforcement learning) rather than the static formula above.

## 3.2 Derivation: Network Lifetime under Energy Harvesting

**Given:**
- Initial energy: $E_0 = 5$ J per node.
- Consumption rate: $P_c = 50$ mW.
- Harvested power: $P_h = 30$ mW (solar, averaged over day/night).

**Step 1 — Without harvesting (classical WSN):**

$$
T_{\text{lifetime}} = \frac{E_0}{P_c} = \frac{5 \text{ J}}{0.05 \text{ W}} = 100 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — With harvesting (ENO condition):**

Effective power balance:

$$
P_{\text{net}} = P_c - P_h = 50 \text{ mW} - 30 \text{ mW} = 20 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 3 — Time to drain initial stored energy:**

$$
T_{\text{stored}} = \frac{E_0}{P_{\text{net}}} = \frac{5}{0.020} = 250 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — After stored energy depletes (if $P_h < P_c$):**

The node still operates on harvested energy but enters a *duty-cycled* mode. The effective operational duty cycle:

$$
\delta = \frac{P_h}{P_c} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.6 \quad \text{(60% active time)}
$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent lifetime extension factor:**

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{1 - \delta} = \frac{1}{0.4} = 2.5\times
$$

The harvested node can therefore theoretically achieve **2.5× the classical lifetime**, even with sub-ENO harvesting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Insight:** Modern solar-powered WSNs (e.g., Libelium Waspmote) achieve **>10 years** lifetime by combining *ENO + adaptive duty cycling + ML-predicted sleep scheduling* — a direct KTU-relevant future trend.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Classical vs. Emerging WSN

| Aspect | Classical WSN (Pre-2020) | Emerging WSN (Post-2020) | KTU Module 4 Trend |
|--------|--------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Routing** | LEACH, PEGASIS, AODV | RPL, Q-Learning, DRL-based | AI-driven |
| **MAC** | S-MAC, B-MAC | TDMA-based 5G NR, TSCH | Time-Slotted |
| **Security** | TinySec, SPINS | Blockchain, Post-Quantum | Decentralized |
| **Computation** | At sink only | Edge, Fog, TinyML | Distributed |
| **Power** | Battery only | Harvesting + battery | Self-sustaining |
| **Topology** | Static | UAV-aided, Mobile sinks | Adaptive |
| **Internet** | Gateway-based | Native IP (6LoWPAN) | IoT-integrated |
| **Spectrum** | Licensed ISM | Cognitive Radio + 5G | Dynamic |

flowchart TB

```

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Energy Harvesting Decision Loop

> [!NOTE]
> Shows the ENO decision loop — every node continuously checks whether harvested energy exceeds consumption to decide Active / Sleep mode.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA[Start of Cycle] --> senseA[Harvest Energy Eh]
    senseA --> consA[Compute Consumption Ec]
    consA --> checkA{Eh greater than Ec}
    checkA --> yesA[Stay Active Transmit]
    checkA --> noA[Enter Sleep Mode]
    yesA --> nextA[Next Cycle]
    noA --> nextA
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Q: Define the term *Internet of Things (IoT)* in the context of Wireless Sensor Networks. Mention any two enabling protocols that allow sensor nodes to communicate over IP.**

**Model Answer:**

> The *Internet of Things (IoT)* in the WSN context refers to the extension of Internet connectivity to resource-constrained sensor nodes, allowing them to send/receive data using standard IP-based protocols rather than proprietary gateway-based stacks.

**Two enabling protocols:**
1. **6LoWPAN** (RFC 4944) — Adaptation layer that compresses IPv6 headers to fit IEEE 802.15.4 frames (128-bit IPv6 → ~40-byte compressed header).
2. **RPL** (RFC 6550) — Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks; builds a *DODAG* rooted at the border router.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Sample QP]`

**Q: What is *Energy Harvesting* in WSN? List any two ambient energy sources with their typical power densities.**

**Model Answer:**

> *Energy Harvesting* in WSN is the process of capturing ambient energy (solar, thermal, vibration, RF) from the environment to recharge or supplement a node's battery, enabling energy-neutral operation (ENO).

**Two ambient sources:**
1. **Solar (outdoor)** — approx. **100 mW/cm²** (bright sunlight).
2. **Vibration** — approx. **10–100 µW/cm²** (industrial machinery, human motion).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, ESE Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three pillars of 5G (eMBB, URLLC, mMTC)** and map each pillar to a representative WSN/Adhoc application. Discuss how *Network Slicing* enables these three pillars to coexist.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the architecture of **Software-Defined Networking (SDN) for WSN** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of *OpenFlow* and the *SDN Controller* in enabling dynamic re-tasking of sensor nodes.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Pillars of 5G + Network Slicing:**

| Pillar | Full Form | Target Metric | WSN/Adhoc Application |
|--------|-----------|---------------|------------------------|
| **eMBB** | enhanced Mobile BroadBand | >1 Gbps peak | Wildlife surveillance with HD video drones |
| **URLLC** | Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Comm. | <1 ms latency, 99.999% reliability | V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) crash avoidance |
| **mMTC** | massive Machine-Type Comm. | $10^6$ devices/km² | Smart agriculture, smart metering in Kerala villages |

**Network Slicing:** A single physical 5G infrastructure is logically partitioned into multiple *virtual slices*, each with isolated resources (bandwidth, latency, compute) tailored to a use case. In WSN, one slice can be dedicated to *emergency WSN* (URLLC), another to *crop monitoring* (mMTC), and a third to *video WSN* (eMBB). Implemented via **SDN + NFV (Network Function Virtualization)**.

**(b) SDN for WSN — Architecture:**

- **Data Plane:** Sensor nodes / Open vSwitches that simply forward packets.
- **Control Plane:** A logically-centralized *SDN Controller* (e.g., ONOS, Floodlight) that decides forwarding rules.
- **Southbound API:** *OpenFlow protocol* carries `flow_mod` messages from controller to switches.
- **Northbound API:** REST/gRPC interface for WSN applications to express intent.
- **Application Plane:** Energy-aware routing app, security policy app, etc.

**Role of OpenFlow:** Standardized message format for installing flow rules. Each rule has *match fields* (input port, MAC, IP) and *actions* (forward, drop, modify).

**Role of SDN Controller:** Global view → computes optimal routes based on *energy residual*, *link quality*, and *application priority* → pushes rules to switches.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common 14-mark Mistakes:**
> 1. Confusing **SDN with traditional routing** — SDN does NOT run routing protocols on each node; routing is computed centrally.
> 2. Forgetting to mention the **energy cost of the control channel** in WSN-SDN (control packets drain the very batteries we are trying to save).
> 3. Not drawing the *block diagram* (mandatory for 7-mark sub-questions in ESE).
> 4. Mixing up **eMBB, URLLC, mMTC** acronyms — write the full form *first*, then the abbreviation.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, ESE Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of an Energy Harvesting WSN**. Derive the **Energy Neutral Operation (ENO) condition** and compute the network lifetime for a node with $E_0 = 5$ J, $P_c = 50$ mW, and $P_h = 30$ mW.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe **TinyML and Federated Learning** as emerging AI paradigms in WSN. Explain how they overcome the limitations of *cloud-only* machine learning in resource-constrained sensor environments.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Energy Harvesting WSN Architecture:**

**Block diagram (described in text for Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    envA[Ambient Source] --> harvA[Harvesting Circuit]
    harvA --> rectA[Rectifier and MPPT]
    rectA --> battA[Rechargeable Battery or Supercap]
    battA --> pmA[Power Management Unit]
    pmA --> sensorA[Sensor and MCU]
    sensorA --> txA[Radio Transmitter]
    pmA -.Control.-> sensorA
```

**Components:**
- **Ambient Source:** Solar panel, piezoelectric, thermoelectric, RF antenna.
- **Harvesting Circuit + MPPT:** Maximum Power Point Tracker extracts peak power.
- **Energy Storage:** Li-ion battery or supercapacitor (buffers day/night cycles).
- **PMU:** Decides Active / Sleep mode based on ENO check.
- **Sensor + Radio:** Performs the sensing task.

**ENO Condition Derivation:**

For a node to operate perpetually:

$$
E_{\text{harvested over period T}} \geq E_{\text{consumed over period T}}
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{T} P_h(t) \, dt \geq \int_{0}^{T} P_c(t) \, dt
$$

In the steady-state (constant average $P_h$, $P_c$):

$$
P_h \cdot T \geq P_c \cdot T \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_h \geq P_c
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given $E_0 = 5$ J, $P_c = 50$ mW $= 0.050$ W, $P_h = 30$ mW $= 0.030$ W.

Net drain:

$$
P_{\text{net}} = P_c - P_h = 0.050 - 0.030 = 0.020 \text{ W}
$$

Time to drain initial stored energy:

$$
T = \frac{E_0}{P_{\text{net}}} = \frac{5}{0.020} = 250 \text{ s}
$$

After this point, since $P_h < P_c$, the node must enter duty-cycled mode with duty cycle:

$$
\delta = \frac{P_h}{P_c} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.6
$$

Equivalent lifetime extension factor:

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{1-\delta} = 2.5\times
$$

**(b) TinyML and Federated Learning:**

| Aspect | TinyML | Federated Learning (FL) |
|--------|--------|--------------------------|
| **Where it runs** | On the *sensor node itself* (ARM Cortex-M0/M4) | Across *many edge devices*, coordinated by a server |
| **Model size** | <100 KB (TensorFlow Lite Micro) | Larger (kB–MB), split across devices |
| **Data** | Local sensor data only | Local data stays on device — only *gradients* sent |
| **Benefit** | Zero-latency, no radio energy for raw data | Privacy-preserving, bandwidth-efficient |
| **Limitation** | Tiny models → lower accuracy | Needs synchronization, non-IID data |

**How they overcome cloud-ML limitations:**
1. **Latency:** Real-time inference on-node (<1 ms) vs. 100+ ms round-trip to cloud.
2. **Energy:** Avoid transmitting raw data (radio is the biggest energy hog).
3. **Privacy:** FL keeps sensitive data (e.g., patient vitals) on-device.
4. **Scalability:** No cloud bandwidth bottleneck for mMTC-scale WSNs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 14-mark Energy Harvesting Question:**
> 1. **Not showing the MPPT block** — required for full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — always state Joules, mW, seconds explicitly.
> 3. **Stating ENO condition as $E_h = E_c$** — it should be $\geq$, not $=$, to account for losses.
> 4. **Mixing up active and sleep power** — typical active power is **10–50 mW**, sleep is **<1 µW** (three orders of magnitude difference).
> 5. **Forgetting the duty cycle analysis** when $P_h < P_c$ — this is a 2–3 mark bonus differentiator.

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

- **Eight Pillars of Future WSN:** IoT, 5G/6G, AI/ML, Edge/Fog, SDN, Blockchain, Energy Harvesting, UAV/CR.
- **IoT protocols:** 6LoWPAN (header compression) + RPL (DODAG routing).
- **5G Triple:** eMBB (high bandwidth), URLLC (<1 ms), mMTC ($10^6$ devices/km²).
- **Network Slicing:** Enabled by SDN + NFV; one physical infra → many virtual WSNs.
- **AI/ML in WSN:** TinyML (on-node), Q-Learning (routing), Federated Learning (privacy).
- **Edge/Fog vs Cloud:** Latency order — Edge (10 ms) < Fog (50 ms) < Cloud (100 ms).
- **SDN in WSN:** Control plane (controller) + Data plane (sensor/switch) + OpenFlow (protocol).
- **Blockchain:** Uses cluster heads as validators; trade-off = energy + storage overhead.
- **Energy Harvesting ENO:** $P_h \geq P_c$; otherwise duty cycle $\delta = P_h/P_c$.
- **LEACH Optimal Clusters:** $K^* = \sqrt{N/2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}} \cdot M / d_{\text{toBS}}^2$.
- **Crossover distance:** $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}}$.
- **RPL Rank:** Function of ETX + hop count + energy residual; builds DODAG toward root.
- **UAV-WSN benefit:** Solves *energy hole* problem near static sink; acts as mobile data collector.
- **Cognitive Radio WSN:** Dynamic Spectrum Access (DSA) using spectrum sensing.
- **Solar power density:** **100 mW/cm²** (bright sun); Vibration: **10–100 µW/cm²**.
- **URLLC target:** Latency **≤ 1 ms**, reliability **99.999%**.
- **5G Shannon capacity:** $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$.
- **Common 14-mark mistakes to avoid:** Missing block diagrams, wrong units, no MPPT in harvesting, no duty-cycle analysis, mixing up acronyms.
- **Real-world Kerala context:** Smart coconut farming, paddy monitoring, Vembanad lake water-quality WSN, Wayanad landslide early-warning WSN.
